We can now introduce the spin-2 tensor harmonics, which are relevant for the description of gravitational radiation. First of all we need the spin wavefunction for $s = 2$ with a definite value of $s _ { z }$ ; this wavefunction isatracelee $t _ { i k } ^ { ( s _ { z } ) }$ ,and is obtained takingtwospin-1wavefuction $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } ) }$ and $\xi _ { k } ^ { ( m _ { 2 } ) }$ , and combining them with the appropriate Clebsch-Gordan coefficients,

$$
t _ { i k } ^ { ( s _ { z } ) } = \sum _ { m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } = - 1 } ^ { 1 }  \parallel 1 1 m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } \mathopen { } \mathclose \bgroup | 2 s _ { z }  \xi _ { i } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } ) } \xi _ { k } ^ { ( m _ { 2 } ) } .
$$

The five tensors $t _ { i k } ^ { ( s _ { z } ) }$ ，with $s _ { z } = \rvert 0 , \pm 1 , \pm 2$ , are symmetric and tracelss, and play the role that the three vectors $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( s _ { z } ) }$ with $s _ { z } = 0 , \pm 1$ played for vector spherical harmonics,that is,we combine them with the scalar spherical harmonics to obtain the spin-2 tensor spherical harmonics,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle ( { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { l } ) _ { i k } \equiv ( Y _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { l 2 } ) _ { i k } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad = \sum _ { l _ { z } = - l } ^ { l } \sum _ { s _ { z } = - 2 } ^ { 2 } \langle 2 l s _ { z } l _ { z } | j j _ { z } \rangle Y _ { l l _ { z } } ( \theta , \phi ) t _ { i k } ^ { ( s _ { z } ) } . } } \end{array}
$$

Just as_in the vector case, $( \mathbf { T } _ { j j _ { 4 } } ^ { l } ) _ { i k }$ are by construction eigenfunctions of the $\mathbf { L } ^ { 2 }$ operator (and for this reason are called pure-orbital $s = 2$ tensor spherical harmonics),but have no special property with respect etaoa to the radial unit vector $j = l \pm 2 , l \pm 1 , l$ (if $l \geqslant 2$ . Similarly to the case of vector harmonics, or $\} = 1 , 2 , 3$ if $l = 1$ ,or $j = 2$ if $l = 0$ (that is,the possible quantum combinations of spin $s = 2$ and orbital angular momentum $l$ ） can be expressed in terms of combinations,called the pure-spin $s = 2$ tensor spherical harmonics,with definite properties under rotations along the radial directions. For $j \geqslant 2$ ,these are given

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { S 0 } = a _ { 1 1 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j + 2 } + a _ { 1 2 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j } + a _ { 1 3 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j - 2 } , } \\ { { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { E 1 } = a _ { 2 1 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j + 2 } + a _ { 2 2 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j } + a _ { 2 3 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j - 2 } , } \\ { { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { E 2 } = a _ { 3 1 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j + 2 } + a _ { 3 2 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j } + a _ { 3 3 } { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j - 2 } , } \\ { { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { B 1 } = b _ { 1 1 } i { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j + 1 } + b _ { 1 2 } i { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j - 1 } , } \\ { { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { B 2 } = b _ { 2 1 } i { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j + 1 } + b _ { 2 2 } i { \bf T } _ { j j _ { z } } ^ { j - 1 } , } \end{array}
$$

where the coefficients are given in Table 3.1. These combinations can be expressed in terms of the scalar spherical harmonics as follows50

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { S 0 } ) _ { i j } = [ n _ { i } n _ { j } - ( 1 / 3 ) \delta _ { i j } ] Y _ { l m } , } \\ & { ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 1 } ) _ { i j } = c _ { l } ^ { ( 1 ) } \left( r / 2 \right) ( n _ { i } \partial _ { j } + n _ { j } \partial _ { i } ) Y _ { l m } , } \\ & { ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 1 } ) _ { i j } = c _ { l } ^ { ( 1 ) } \left( i / 2 \right) ( n _ { i } L _ { j } + n _ { j } L _ { i } ) Y _ { l m } , } \\ & { ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 } ) _ { i j } = c _ { l } ^ { ( 2 ) } r ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { i j , i ^ { \prime } j ^ { \prime } } ( \hat { \mathbf { n } } ) \partial _ { i ^ { \prime } } \partial _ { j ^ { \prime } } Y _ { l m } , } \\ & { ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 } ) _ { i j } = c _ { l } ^ { ( 2 ) } r \Lambda _ { i j , i ^ { \prime } j ^ { \prime } } ( \hat { \mathbf { n } } ) ( i / 2 ) \bigr ( \partial _ { i ^ { \prime } } L _ { j ^ { \prime } } + \partial _ { j ^ { \prime } } L _ { i ^ { \prime } } ) Y _ { l m } , } \end{array}
$$

where, as usual, $\Lambda _ { i j , i ^ { \prime } j ^ { \prime } }$ is the tensor that implements the TT projection, see eq. (1.36), and

$$
c _ { l } ^ { ( 1 ) } = \left( \frac { 2 } { l ( l + 1 ) } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } , \qquad c _ { l } ^ { ( 2 ) } = \left( 2 \frac { ( l - 2 ) ! } { ( l + 2 ) ! } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

A complete set of $s = 2$ spherical harnmonic is given by eqs.(3.268) 13.272) where $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { S 0 }$ has $l \geqslant 0$ $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 1 }$ and $\boldsymbol { \Upsilon } _ { l m } ^ { B 1 }$ have $l \geqslant 1$ while $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 }$ and $\mathbb { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 }$ have $l \geqslant 2$ 51 The above tensors are allsymmetric and traceless by construction. On a traceless-symmetric tensor $h _ { i j }$ ,the transversality the condition eliminates three degrees of freedom,and indeed we $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 }$ and $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 }$ aretransverse,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \boxed { \begin{array} { c c } { \boldsymbol { n } _ { i } ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 } ) _ { i j } = 0 , } & { \boldsymbol { n } _ { i } ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 } ) _ { i j } = 0 . } \end{array} } } \end{array}
$$

The five pure-spin $s = 2$ tensor harmonic\$ are appropriate for describing hefive independent components of a massive spin-2 particle. However, ns we discussed in Problem 1.1 a massless particle with quantum number has only two components rather than $2 s + 1$ ，with helicities $h = \pm s$ Under a rotation by an angle $\theta$ around the $\hat { \bf n }$ axis， $( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { S 0 } ) _ { i j }$ is invariant since it depends only on $n _ { i } , n _ { j }$ .Thereforein the massless case it describes spi-0 particle. Instead $( \dot { \mathbf { T } } _ { l m } ^ { E 1 } ) _ { i j }$ and $( \mathbf { \Gamma } _ { l m } ^ { B 1 } ) _ { i j }$ have one index $i$ or $j$ proportional to $n _ { i }$ or $n _ { j }$ ，which is invariant,while the other index is crried by a vector in the transverse plane.Therefore they combine give the two eigenvectors of helicitywith $h = \pm 1$ and describe a massless vector particle.Finally,the transverse and traceless tensors $| \tilde { \Gamma } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 } \rangle _ { i j }$ and $( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 } ) _ { i j }$ aveod nse to the states with which make up a massless particle with $\therefore \vert = 2$ .Therefore,even if we use the name “spin-2 tensor harmonics”,

50As in the vector case, we now switch notation from $j , j _ { z }$ to $l , m$ ，but one should be aware that these indices refer to the total angular momentum; we reserve instead the notation $( i , j )$ for the spatial indices of vectors.

(3.71)ad (3.272),vanish for and for

it is important to understand that they can be used to describe eitlie the five polarization states of a massive spin-2 particle,or to describel massless field; in the latter case these five degrees of freedom decompost into the two degrees of freedom of a massless particle with $h = \pm 2$ the two degrees of freedom of a massless particle with $h = \pm 1$ ,and on degree of freedom corresponding to a massless scalar particle.Observe that the labels O,1or 2 in $S 0 , E 1 , B 1 , E 2 , B 2$ refer to the (absolute value of the) helicity carried in the massless case.

In particular,in standard geheral relativity the graviton is a massles particle with helicity $\pm 2$ or,equivalently,the tensor $h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } }$ that describes GWs in the TT gauge,beside being symmetric and traceless, is also transverse.Thereforeinitsexpansionenteronly $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 }$ and $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 }$ while the other components are eliminated by gauge invariance,as discussed in Sections 1.2 and 2.2.2.In the wave zone, where $h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } }$ decreases as $1 / \tau$ the most general form of $h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } } ( t , r , \theta , \phi )$ is then

<table><tr><td>福 1 G 8 ！ M 1 r C4 l=2 m=-l</td><td>[ulm(T)ij（0，）+Um(Tm）i（0，）)],</td></tr></table>

where $u _ { l m }$ and $v _ { l m }$ are functioris of retarded time $t - r / c$ andthefactor $G / c ^ { 4 }$ in front is a useful normalization of $u _ { l m } , v _ { l m }$ .The other pure spin $s = 2$ tensor harinonics can enter in extensions of general relativity in which further degrees of freedom are present,and the condition of transversality no longer holds. Furthermore,in scalar-tensor extensions of general relativity, $h _ { i j }$ is not even traceless and there is a sixth degree of freedom corresponding to the trace of $h _ { i j }$ ，which is a scalar field. So. in the most general case we must include all five spin-2 tensor harmonics (3.268)-(3.272),and we must further add $\delta _ { i j } Y _ { l m }$ ，which is not traceless and accounts for the scalar field corresponding to the trace part.The function $\delta _ { i j } Y _ { l m }$ can be combined with $( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { S 0 } ) _ { i j }$ to give a purely longitudinal and a purely transverse (but not traceless) tensor harmonic,

$$
( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { L 0 } ) _ { i j } = n _ { i } n _ { j } Y _ { l m } , \quad ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { T 0 } ) _ { i j } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } ( \delta _ { i j } - n _ { i } n _ { j } ) Y _ { l m } ,
$$

with $l \geqslant 0$ .The coeffcients in eqs.(3.268)-(3.272) and in eq.(3.276) are chosen so that the pure-spin harmonics are orthonormal,

$$
\int d \Omega ( { \bf T } _ { l m } ^ { J } ) _ { i j } ( { \bf T } _ { l ^ { \prime } m ^ { \prime } } ^ { J ^ { \prime } } ) _ { i j } ^ { \ast } = \delta ^ { J J ^ { \prime } } \delta _ { l l ^ { \prime } } \delta _ { m m ^ { \prime } } ,
$$

where the label $J$ takes the values $L 0 , T 0 , E 1 , B 1 , E 2 , B 2$ Finally， we observe from the explicit expressions that T,T lad $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 }$ $\pi _ { l } = ( - 1 ) ^ { l }$ $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 1 }$ $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 }$ type”parity $\pi _ { l } = ( - 1 ) ^ { l + 1 }$

Equation (3.275) is the main result of this subsection. It is the generalization to the spin-2 field $\left| h _ { i j } \right|$ of the more usual expansion of the solution of the wave equation for a relativistic scalar field in the wave zone,in terms of (scalar) spherical harmonics,

$$
\phi ( t , r , \theta , \phi ) = \frac 1 r \sum _ { l = 0 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { m = - l } ^ { l } c _ { l m } Y _ { l m } ( \theta , \phi ) ,
$$

with $c _ { l m }$ functions of $t - r / c$ ,in the wave zone. Comparing eqs.(3.275) and (3.278) we see the following importaht differences:(1) a scalar field has only one spin degree of freedom and therefore,at each angular momentum level $l , m$ ,it is described bya single function $c _ { l m } ( t - r / c )$ A gravitational wave,instead,has two helicity states.Therefore there are twosetsftesphalic $\dot { \mathbf { T } } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 }$ and $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 }$ ,and correspondinglytwo sets of functions $u _ { l m } ( t - r / c )$ and $v _ { l m } ( t - r / c )$ .These states are transverse,see eq.(3.274),one with electric-type parity and the other with magnetic-type parity. Because of eq|(3.277),the pure-spin harmonics provides an orthonormal basis for these modes.(2） The expansion of $h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } }$ starts from total angular momentum $l = 2$ It is impossible to construct a GW with total angular momentum l= O or l = 1. This counting of degrees of freedom is in full agreement with the discussion of graviton states in Problem 1.2.

Our next task is to relate the coefficients $u _ { l m } , v _ { l m }$ in eq. (3.275） to the appropriate multipole moments of the source,as we do in the next subsection.52

# Relation with the source moments

where it is understood that all the $S ^ { p q } \vert ^ { i _ { 1 } . . . i _ { \alpha } }$ are functions of $t - r / c$ On the other hand,in the previous section we have seen that the most general expansion for $h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } }$ in the wave zone is given by

where again it is understood that $u _ { \mathit { l m } }$ ahd $v _ { { l m } }$ are functions of $t - r / c$ Comparing the two expressions，we can determine $u _ { \mathit { l m } }$ and $v _ { l m }$ To obtain $u _ { i m }$ wemultiplyothidesofey $( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 } ) _ { i j } ^ { * }$ and we integrate over $d \Omega$ .On the right-hand side,using the orthonormality condition (3.277),we single out $u _ { \ell m }$ ,while on the left-hand side we insert the expression (3.279) for $h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } }$ . Similarly, to obtain $v _ { l m }$ we multiply by

$( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 } ) _ { i j } ^ { * }$ . Then we get

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { u _ { l m } = \sum _ { \alpha = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 4 } { \alpha ! } \left( \partial _ { 0 } ^ { \alpha } S ^ { p q , i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { \alpha } } \right) \int d \Omega \left( { \bf T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 } \right) _ { i j } ^ { * } \Lambda _ { i j , p q } n _ { i _ { 1 } } \cdots n _ { i _ { \alpha } } , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { v _ { l m } = \sum _ { \alpha = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 4 } { \alpha ! } \left( \partial _ { 0 } ^ { \alpha } S ^ { p q , i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { \alpha } } \right) \int d \Omega \left( { \bf T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 } \right) _ { i j } ^ { * } \Lambda _ { i j , p q } n _ { i _ { 1 } } \cdots n _ { i _ { \alpha } } . } } } \end{array}

Since the Lambda tensor projects on the transverse and traceless par of a tensor,and $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 }$ and $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 }$ ralretrse

$$
( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { J } ) _ { i j } ^ { * } \mathbb { \Lambda } _ { i j , p q } = ( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { J } ) _ { p q } ^ { * } ,

(with $J = E 2 , B 2 )$ ,and wecansimplifytheaboveexpresions,

$$

53In general,ifone wishes toompute the total radiated power consistently to a given order in $\upsilon ^ { 2 } / c ^ { 2 }$ ,one cannot stop to the leading order in $\scriptstyle { \boldsymbol { v } } ^ { 2 } / c ^ { 2 }$ in the computations of ${ \boldsymbol { u } } _ { l m }$ .For instance,the subleading terms in the mass quadrupole $u _ { 2 m }$ are of the same order,in the radiated power,as the leading term of the mass octupole $u _ { 3 m }$ Furthermore, for self-gravitating sources,we cannot use the expansion over flat space as we have done in this chapter,and the computation must be performed using the post-Newtonian formalism presented in Chapter 5.

The computation of the integrals is involved, but can be performed orde by order in $v / c$ To leading order in $v / c$ ，we perform it in detail in Problem 3.5.53 The result is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { u _ { l m } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 6 \pi } { ( 2 l + 1 ) ! ! } \left[ \displaystyle \frac { l } { 2 } ( l - 1 ) ( l + 1 ) ( l + 2 ) \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } \mathcal { V } _ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } ^ { l m * } \partial _ { 0 } ^ { l - 2 } S ^ { i _ { 1 } i _ { 2 } , i _ { 3 } \ldots i _ { l } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } \times \left[ 1 + O \left( \displaystyle \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 2 } } \right) \right] . } } \end{array}
$$

We now want to write the time derivatives of $S ^ { i _ { 1 } i _ { 2 } , i _ { 3 } \ldots i _ { l } }$ in terms of the derivatives of the momenta of $T ^ { 0 0 }$ ,as follows.Fromtheconservationo energy-momentum tensor, $\partial _ { \mu } T ^ { \dot { \mu } \nu } = 0$ ，we have the rclations $\partial _ { 0 } T ^ { 0 0 } =$ $- \partial _ { i } T ^ { 0 i }$ and $\partial _ { 0 } T ^ { 0 i } = - \partial _ { j } T ^ { j i }$ We can combine them to get $\partial _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } T ^ { 0 0 } =$ $\partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } T ^ { i j }$ .Then,integrating twice by parts,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \ddot { M } ^ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } = \int d ^ { 3 } x ( \partial _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } T ^ { 0 0 } ) x ^ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x ^ { i _ { l } } } } \\ { { } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \int d ^ { 3 } x ( \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } T ^ { i j } ) x ^ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x ^ { i _ { l } } } } \\ { { } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \int d ^ { 3 } x T ^ { i j } \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } ( x ^ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x ^ { i _ { l } } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

For $l \geqslant 2$ ,as in our case,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } ( x ^ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x ^ { i _ { l } } ) = ( \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } x ^ { i _ { 1 } } x ^ { i _ { 2 } } ) x ^ { i _ { 3 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x ^ { i _ { l } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ { = ( \delta _ { i } ^ { i _ { 1 } } \delta _ { j } ^ { i _ { 2 } } + \delta _ { i } ^ { i _ { 2 } } \delta _ { j } ^ { i _ { 1 } } ) x ^ { i _ { 3 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x ^ { i _ { l } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

where thedots denote the other similar terms;in total there are $l ( l - 1 ) / 2$ terms of this type.Therefore

$$
\ddot { M } ^ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } = 2 ( S ^ { i _ { 1 } i _ { 2 } , i _ { 3 } , \ldots i _ { l } } + \ldots ) ,
$$

where the dots denote all other $l ( l - 1 ) / 2$ pairing of indices (the permutation of the first two indices $i , j$ in $S ^ { i j , k l \cdots }$ is already taken into account by the overall factor of 2).This is the generalization to arbitrary $\it l$ of the relation $\ddot { M } ^ { i j } = 2 S ^ { i j }$ found in eq. (3.52). Once we contract the left- and gsoi $\mathcal { Y } _ { i _ { 1 } . . . i _ { l } } ^ { l m * }$ ,wic istotaly symetric, all these permutations give the same result,so

$$
y _ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } ^ { l m * } S ^ { i _ { 1 } i _ { 2 } , i _ { 3 } , \ldots i _ { l } } = \frac 1 { l ( l - 1 ) } | y _ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } ^ { l m * } \ddot { M } ^ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } .
$$

Therefore,in eq. (3.285),

$$
y _ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } ^ { l m * } \partial _ { 0 } ^ { l - 2 } S ^ { i _ { 1 } i _ { 2 } , i _ { 3 } , \ldots i _ { l } } = \frac 1 { c ^ { l - 2 } } \frac 1 { l ( l - 1 ) } y _ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } ^ { l m * } \frac { d ^ { l } } { d t ^ { l } } M ^ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } .
$$

Then we get

$$
{ \overline { { u _ { l m } } } } = { \frac { d ^ { l } } { d t ^ { l } } } I _ { l m } { \Big \vert } ,
$$

where, to leading order in $( \upsilon / c ) ^ { 2 }$ ，

$$
I _ { l m } = \frac { 1 } { c ^ { l - 2 } } \frac { 1 6 \pi } { ( 2 l + 1 ) ! ! } \left[ \frac { ( l + 1 ) ( l + 2 ) } { 2 l ( l - 1 ) } \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } \mathcal { y } _ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } ^ { l m * } M ^ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } .
$$

Similarly,repeating the same analysis for $v _ { l m }$ (and using results discussed in Problem 3.5), we get

$$
{ v _ { l m } } = { \frac { d ^ { l } } { d t ^ { l } } } S _ { l m } ,
$$

where, again to leading order in $( \upsilon / c ) ^ { 2 }$

$$
S _ { l m } = \frac { 1 } { c ^ { l - 1 } } \frac { 3 2 \pi } { ( 2 l + 1 ) ! ! } \left[ \frac { l ( l + 2 ) } { 2 ( l - 1 ) ( l + 1 ) } \right] ^ { \left\lfloor 1 / 2 \right. } y _ { i i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l - 1 } } ^ { l m * } \epsilon _ { i j k } P ^ { j , k i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l - 1 } } ,
$$

where $P ^ { j , k i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l - 1 } }$ are the momenta of the linear momentum.54

Comparing with eq.(3.238) we see that $I _ { l m }$ and $S _ { l m }$ are just the spherical components of the tensors $M ^ { i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { l } }$ and $\epsilon _ { i j k } P ^ { j , k i _ { 1 } . . . i _ { l - 1 } }$ ,respectively, apart from an $l$ -dependent normalization.The tensor $M ^ { i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { l } }$ represents the moments of $\scriptstyle { T ^ { 0 0 } } / { c ^ { 2 } }$ ; if the source is non-relativistic and has a negligible self-gravity, $\scriptstyle { T ^ { 0 0 } } / { c ^ { 2 } }$ is the same as the mass density. Instead,from eqs. (3.40) and (3.41)， we see that $\epsilon _ { i j k } P \dagger ^ { j , k }$ is the angular momentum, and that $\epsilon _ { i j k } P ^ { j , k i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l - 1 } }$ are the momenta of the angular momentum. Writing explicitly

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { M ^ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 2 } } \int d ^ { 3 } x T ^ { 0 0 } x ^ { i _ { 1 } } \cdots x ^ { i _ { l } } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad = \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 2 } } \int d ^ { 3 } x T ^ { 0 0 } r ^ { l } n ^ { i _ { 1 } } \cdots n ^ { i _ { l } } , } } \end{array}
$$

54The expressions that we have computed in this section for $I _ { l m }$ and $S _ { l m }$ are valid only to leading order in $v / c ,$ i.e. they are Newtonian expressions. The full expansion in $\upsilon / c$ is studied in Thorne (1980),Section $\mathrm { v }$

and using eq. (3.234),we canrewriteeq. (3.292) as again to leading order in $\upsilon ^ { 2 } / c ^ { 2 }$ . Similarly we find

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { S _ { l m } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { c ^ { l } } \frac { 3 2 \pi } { ( 2 l + 1 ) ! ! } \left[ \frac { l ( l + 2 ) } { 2 ( l - 1 ) ( l + 1 ) } \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } } } \\ { { \times \epsilon _ { j p q } \displaystyle \int d ^ { 3 } x r ^ { l } T ^ { 0 p } ( \mathcal { V } _ { j i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { l - 1 } } ^ { l m } ) ^ { * } n ^ { q } n ^ { i _ { 1 } } \dots n ^ { i _ { l - 1 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

This expression can be written in terms of the vector spherical harmonic $\mathbf { Y } _ { l m } ^ { B }$ ded of this vector harmonic,one finds

$$
( { \bf Y } _ { l m } ^ { B } ) _ { i } = \left( \frac { l } { l + 1 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \epsilon _ { i j k } y _ { k i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { l - 1 } n } ^ { l m } n ^ { j } n ^ { i _ { 1 } } \ldots n ^ { i _ { l - 1 } } ,
$$

and therefore,to leading order in $( v / c ) ^ { 2 }$ ，

<table><tr><td>1</td><td>32π Sm=(2l+1） [2(-1]</td><td>(+2) 1/2 drlToiYm).</td></tr></table>

The coefficients of the expansion of $h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } }$ have therefore been writen as integrals over the source of quantities which depend on the energymomentum tensor.

# Radiated power

We can finally write the radiated power to allorders in the multipole ex-pansion. In eq. (3.275) we have expressed $h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } }$ in the basis of $\mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 } , \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 }$ Inserting eq. (3.275) into eq.(1.153)and using the orthonormality relation (3.277),we find

$$
\int d \Omega \dot { h } _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } } \dot { h } _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } } = { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { G ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 8 } } } \left| \sum _ { l = 2 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { m = - l } ^ { l } \left[ | \dot { u } _ { l m } | ^ { 2 } + | \dot { v } _ { l m } | ^ { 2 } \right] . \right.
$$

Therefore

<table><tr><td>dE G dt 32πc5</td><td>8 |dl+1Im ！ MM dtl+1 l=2 m=-l</td><td rowspan="2">2 +</td><td>|dl+1S1m 2 ）， dt+1</td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

where $I _ { l m }$ and $\boldsymbol { S _ { l m } }$ are functions of retarded time $t - r / c$ .The power is therefore a sum of terms each one associated with a single mass multipole or angular momentum multipole,and there are no mixed terms.

As a check of the above result,we cah verify that we reproduce the mass quadrupole and the current quadrupole radiation.We start from the mass quadrupole. Equation (3.292),with $l = 2$ gives

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { } } & { { } } & { { I _ { 2 m } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 6 \pi } { 5 \sqrt { 3 } } \ y _ { i j } ^ { 2 m * } M _ { i j } } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { = \displaystyle \frac { 1 6 \pi } { 5 \sqrt { 3 } } \ y _ { i j } ^ { 2 m * } Q _ { i j } \ , } } \end{array}
$$

where we could replace $M _ { i j }$ by $Q _ { i j }$ since $\mathcal { V } _ { i j } ^ { 2 m * }$ is traceless in the $( i , j )$ indices.From eq. (3.301),the power radiated by the quadrupole is

$$
P _ { \mathrm { q u a d } } = \frac { G } { 3 2 \pi c ^ { 5 } } \left( \frac { 1 6 \pi } { 5 \sqrt { 3 } } \right) ^ { 2 } \langle \ddot { Q } _ { i j } \ddot { Q } _ { k l } \rangle \sum _ { m = - 2 } ^ { 2 } { y } _ { i j } ^ { 2 m * } { y } _ { k l } ^ { 2 m } .
$$

We now use the identity55

$$
\sum _ { m = - 2 } ^ { 2 } y _ { i j } ^ { 2 m * } y _ { k l } ^ { 2 m } = \frac { 1 5 } { 1 6 \pi } ( \delta _ { i k } \delta _ { j l } + \delta _ { i l } \delta _ { j k } - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \delta _ { i j } \delta _ { k l } ) ,
$$

and we correctly recover eq. (3.75). The current quadrupole is checked Similarly:from eq.(3.294) we have

$$
S _ { 2 m } = \frac { 1 } { c } \frac { 6 4 \pi } { 1 5 \sqrt { 3 } } y _ { i l } ^ { 2 m * } \epsilon _ { i j k } P ^ { j , k l } .
$$

Since

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \epsilon _ { i j k } P ^ { j , k l } = \epsilon _ { i j k } \displaystyle \int d ^ { 3 } x { \cal T } ^ { 0 j } x ^ { k } x ^ { l } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { = - \displaystyle \int d ^ { 3 } x \left( \epsilon _ { i k j } x ^ { k } { \cal T } ^ { 0 j } \right) x ^ { l } } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \ = - \displaystyle \int d ^ { 3 } x j _ { \ast } ^ { i } x ^ { l } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { = - J ^ { i , l } , } } \end{array}
$$

we get

$$
S _ { 2 m } = - \frac { 1 } { c } \frac { 6 4 \pi } { 1 5 \sqrt { 3 } } \mathcal { V } _ { i j } ^ { 2 m * } J _ { i , j } .
$$

Then,using again the identity (3.304),and recalling that $J _ { i , j } \delta _ { i j } = 0$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle P _ { \mathrm { c u r r ~ q u a d } } = \frac { G } { 3 2 \pi c ^ { 7 } } \left( \frac { 6 4 \pi } { 1 5 \sqrt { 3 } } \right) ^ { 2 } \langle \vec { J } _ { i , j } ^ { \top } , \vec { J } _ { k , l } ^ { \top } \rangle \sum _ { m = - 2 } ^ { 2 } y _ { i j } ^ { 2 m * } { y } _ { k l } ^ { 2 m * } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \frac { 8 G } { 4 5 c ^ { 7 } } \langle \vec { J } _ { i , j } ^ { \top } , \vec { J } _ { k , l } ^ { * } \rangle \left( \delta _ { i k } \delta _ { j l } + \delta _ { i l } \delta _ { j k } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \frac { 8 G } { 4 5 c ^ { 7 } } \langle \vec { J } _ { i , j } ^ { \top } ( \vec { J } _ { i , j } ^ { * } + \vec { J } _ { j , i } ) \rangle } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \frac { 1 6 G } { 4 5 c ^ { 7 } } \langle \vec { J } _ { i j } \vec { \mathcal { T } } _ { i j } \rangle , } } \end{array}
$$

55This identity could be provedusing the explicit expressions (3.218） for $y _ { i j } ^ { 2 m }$ However,thesimplest way toderive it is to observe that，after summing over $m = - 2 , \ldots , 2$ ，there is no longer a dependence on the direction chosen as quantization axis for $j _ { z }$ ，S0 the right-hand side of eq.(3.304) can only depend on combinations of Kronecker deltas,i.e.it must be of the form $a \delta _ { i k } \delta _ { j l } + b \delta _ { i l } \delta _ { j k } + c \delta _ { i j } \delta _ { k l }$ Since the left-hand side of eq.(3.304) gives zero when contracted with $\delta _ { i j }$ ,we must have $\delta _ { i j } ( a \delta _ { i k } \delta _ { j l } + b \delta _ { i l } \delta _ { j k } + c \delta _ { i j } \delta _ { k l } ) = 0$ which fixes $c = - ( 1 / 3 ) ( a + b )$ .Then α and $b$ are fixed comparing the left and right-hand side of eq.(3.304) for two different values of the indices,using the explicit expressions (3.218).

agreement with eq. (3.154).

# 3.6Solved problems

As a first simple application,we compute the quadrupole radiation emited bya non-relativistic system with just one degree of freedom,that perform harmonic oscillations along the $z$ axis,

$$
z _ { 0 } ( t ) = a \cos { \omega _ { s } t } ,
$$

with $a \omega _ { s } ~ \ll ~ c$ and $\omega _ { s } ~ > ~ 0$ ．Aswe explained in Section 3.3.5,the whole formalism that we have developed for computing the emission of gravitational radiation is consistent only if we have a closed system,on which no external forces are acting.So,in this case the actual physical system that we have in mind could be made for instance of two masses connected by a spring,and $z _ { 0 } ( t )$ is the relative coordinate in the center-of mass system.For the moment we consider the case where the rest length of the spring is zero (which is uot realistic for a one-dimensional spring,but is representative of a number ol situations where some degree of freedom performs a simple harmonic motion),

The mass density is then

$$
\rho ( t , \mathbf { x } ) = \mu \delta ( x ) \delta ( y ) \delta ( z - z _ { 0 } ( t ) ) ,
$$

where $\mu$ is the reduced mass of the system,and the second mass moment is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle M ^ { i j } ( t ) = \int d ^ { 3 } x \rho ( t , { \bf x } ) x ^ { i } x ^ { j } } \ ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = \mu z _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) \delta ^ { i 3 } \delta ^ { j 3 } } \ ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = \mu a _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \frac { 1 + \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t } { 2 } \delta ^ { i 3 } \delta ^ { j 3 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Inserting this into eq.(3.72),we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle h _ { + } ( t ; \theta , \phi ) = - \frac { 1 } { r } \bigg | \frac { G } { c ^ { 4 } } \ddot { M } _ { 3 3 } ( t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } ) \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \\ { = \frac { 2 G \mu a ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { \eta c ^ { 4 } } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ( 2 \omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } ) , } \\ { \displaystyle h _ { \times } ( t ; \theta , \phi ) = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/94c6c6affd7053598471c2ca4e09b26f8675f41ca4a1d761a8e997f9233626a8.jpg)  
Problem 3.1. Quadrupole radiation from an oscillating mass   
Fig.3.4 A source oscillating along the $z$ axis (double arrow),and the lines of force of the GW emitted in a direction with $\theta = \pi / 2$

Therefore we have monochromatic radiation at a frequency $\omega = 2 \omega _ { s }$ ,with a purely plus polarization,see Fig.3.4.

The angular distribution is independent of $\phi$ (reflecting the cylindric symmetry of the source),and has a maximum at $\theta = \pi / 2$ ,i.e.in the direction orthogonal to the axis along which the source oscillates.Observe that the radiation vanishes along the $z$ axis.This reflects the fact that only the components of the motion of the source transverse to the line-of-sight contribute to the production of GWs. This is a general result,which follows from the fact that $\Lambda _ { i j , k l } n ^ { k } = \Lambda _ { i j , k l } n ^ { l } = 0$ That is,the Lambda tensor projects the motion of the source onto the plane transverse to the propagation direction.

Observe also that the pattern of lines of force of the GW shown in Fig.3.4 is a physical result,independent of our conventions.The fact that we call it a “plus”polarization,instead,is related to our choice of axes $( \hat { \mathbf { u } } , \hat { \mathbf { v } } )$ with respect to which the plus and cross polarizations are defined.With our definition, $( \hat { \mathbf { u } } , \hat { \mathbf { v } } )$ are obtained from the $\big ( \hat { \mathbf { x } } , \hat { \mathbf { y } } \big )$ axes applying the rotation matrix $\mathcal { R }$ given in eq.(3.70),see also Fig.3.2. Thus,in Fig.3.4,for the particular propagation direction for which the lines of force are shown (i.e.fora direction n such that $\theta = \pi / 2$ ，wehave $\hat { \mathbf { v } } = - \hat { \mathbf { z } }$ ,while $\hat { \bf { u } }$ is along the intersection of this transverse plane with the $( x , y )$ plane.If,in this transverse plane,we instead used $( \hat { \mathbf { u } } , \hat { \mathbf { v } } )$ axesrotated by 45 degrees to define $h _ { + }$ and $h _ { \times }$ ，we would rather call the pattern of Fig.3.4 a purely cross polarization, compare with Figs.1.2 and 1.3, while for axes rotated by a generic angle $\psi$ we would have a mixture of plus und cross polarizations,according to eq. $( 2 . 1 9 4 )$

The radiated power is computed from eq. (3.73),

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left( \displaystyle \frac { d P } { d \Omega } \right) _ { \mathrm { q u a d } } = \frac { r ^ { 2 } c ^ { 3 } } { 1 6 \pi G } \langle \dot { h } _ { + } ^ { 2 } \rangle } \\ { = \frac { G \mu ^ { 2 } a ^ { 4 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 6 } } { 2 \pi c ^ { 5 } } \sin ^ { 4 } \theta , } \end{array}
$$

where we used $\langle \cos ^ { 2 } ( 2 \omega _ { s } t ) \rangle = 1 / 2$ .Alternatively,we can recover the same result using eq.(3.73)in the form

$$
\left( \frac { d P } { d \Omega } \right) _ { \mathrm { \scriptsize { q u a d } } } = \frac { G } { 8 \pi c ^ { 5 } } \Lambda _ { 3 3 , 3 3 } ( \hat { \bf n } ) \langle \ddot { \cal M } _ { 3 3 } ^ { 2 } \rangle ,
$$

and observing that,from eq. (1.39),

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \Lambda _ { 3 3 , 3 3 } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 1 - n _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin ^ { 4 } \theta \nonumber , } } \end{array}
$$

since $n _ { 3 } = \cos \theta$ . In Fig.3.5 we show this angular distribution,in the $( x , z )$ plane. The integration over the solid angle gives

$$
P _ { \mathtt { q u a d } } = { \frac { 1 6 } { 1 5 } } { \frac { G \mu ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 5 } } } a _ { \ i } ^ { * } \omega _ { s } ^ { 6 } .
$$

Ihe total energy radiated over one period $T \equiv 2 \pi / \omega _ { s }$ of the source motion is therefore

$$
\langle { \cal E } _ { \mathrm { q u a d } } \rangle _ { T } = \frac { 3 2 \pi } { 1 5 } \frac { G \mu ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 5 } } a ^ { 4 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 5 } .
$$

$$
\langle E _ { \mathrm { q u a d } } \rangle _ { T } = \frac { 3 2 \pi } { 1 5 } \frac { G \mu ^ { 2 } } { a } \left( \frac { v } { c } \right) ^ { 5 } .
$$

Observe that $G \mu ^ { 2 } / a$ is of order of the gravitational self-energy of an object of mass $\mu$ and size $a$ .In the quadrupole approximation,the energy radiated over cycle is suppressed,with respect to this energy scale,by a factor $( v / c ) ^ { 5 }$

This result becomes physically more transparent if we rewrite it in terms of $\boldsymbol { v } = a \omega _ { s }$ (which is the maximum speed of the source),

Finally,it is instructive to consider the case of two masses connected by a spring with a rest length $L$ ,so their relative coordinate obeys

Observe that $L$ has an invariant meaning,because $z _ { 0 } ( t )$ is the relative position between the two masses (compare with the discussion in Section 3.3.5),so we amot set it to zero with a choice of the origin. Now we get

$$
z _ { 0 } ( t ) = L + a \cos { \omega _ { s } t } .
$$

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/4d1b7adc3ef1897effc2cfc324b6a2bc8a6f1ac1033474775b88a1a0cc089cee.jpg)  
Fig.3.5 The angular distribution of the quadrupole radiation,for a mass oscillating along the $z$ axis. We represent the angular distribution plotting the function $\rho = \sin ^ { 4 } \theta$ ，where $( \rho , \theta )$ are the polar coordinates in the $( x , z )$ plane and $\theta$ is measured from the $z$ axis.The full threedimensional pattern has cylindrical symmetry around the $z$ axis.

$$
z _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) = { \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t + 2 L a \cos \omega _ { s } t + \mathrm { c o n s t . } ,
$$

and eq. (3.312) becomes

$$
h _ { + } ( t ; \theta , \phi ) = \frac { 2 G \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { r c ^ { 4 } } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta [ a ^ { 2 } \cos ( 2 \omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } ) + L a \cos ( \omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } ) ] ,
$$

while still $h _ { \times } ~ = ~ 0$ Thus，beside having gravitational radiation at $\omega _ { \mathrm { g w } } =$ $2 \omega _ { s }$ ，we also have radiation at $\omega _ { \mathrm { g w } } = \omega _ { s }$ ．Observe that,in the power,them is no interference between these two terms because $\langle \cos ^ { 2 } ( 2 \omega _ { s } t ) \rangle = 1 / 2 =$ $\left. \cos ^ { 2 } ( \omega _ { s } t ) \right.$ ,but

$$
\langle 2 \cos ( 2 \omega _ { s } t ) \cos ( \omega _ { s } t ) \rangle = \langle \cos ( 3 \omega _ { s } t ) + \cos ( \omega _ { s } t ) \rangle = 0 .
$$

# Problem 3.2.Quadrupole radiation from a mass in circular orbit

In this problem we consider a binary system with masses $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ and we assume that the relative coordinate is performing a circular motion. We assume for the moment that the orbital motion is given,and we neglect am back-reaction on the motion due to GW emission.In this form,this is jus a simple exercise,propedeutic for understandinga real self-gravitating binary system.In Section 4.1 we will include the effect of the GW back-reactiol within linearized theory. Furthermore,beyond lowest-order in $v / c$ wecannot keep the space-time as flat when describing a self-gravitating system,and th correct formalism for computingthe $v / c$ corrections will be the subject ol Chapter 5.

So,for the moment,we rather assign ourselves the trajectory.We choos the $( x , y , z )$ frame so that the orbit lies in the $( x , y )$ plane,and is given by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x _ { 0 } ( t ) \equiv R \cos ( \omega _ { s } t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle y _ { 0 } ( t ) = R \sin ( \omega _ { s } t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle z _ { 0 } ( t ) \equiv 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

(The phase $\pi / 2$ is a useful choice of the origin of time.）We denote by $\mu =$ $m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } / ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } )$ the reduced mass of the system.From eq.(3.129),in the CM frame the second mass moment is $M ^ { i j } = \mu x _ { 0 } ^ { i } ( t ) x _ { 0 } ^ { j } ( t )$ s0

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { M _ { 1 1 } = \mu R ^ { 2 } \frac { 1 - \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t } { 2 } , } \\ & { M _ { 2 2 } = \mu R ^ { 2 } \frac { 1 + \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t } { 2 } , } \\ & { M _ { 1 2 } = - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mu R ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \omega _ { s } t , } \end{array}
$$

while the other components vanish. Therefore we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \ddot { M } _ { 1 1 } = 2 \mu R ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t , } \\ { \ddot { M } _ { 1 2 } = 2 \mu R ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \omega _ { s } t , } \end{array}
$$

and $\ddot { M } _ { 2 2 } = - \ddot { M } _ { 1 1 }$ .Plugging these expressions intoeq. (3.72)weget

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle h _ { + } ( t ; \theta , \phi ) = \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { 4 G \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } R ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 4 } } \left( \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } { 2 } \right) \cos ( 2 \omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } + 2 \phi ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle h _ { \times } ( t ; \theta , \phi ) ) = \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { 4 G \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } R ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 4 } } \cos \theta \sin ( 2 \omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } + 2 \phi ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Thus,the quadrupole radiation is at twice the frequency $\omega _ { s }$ of the source. It is nteresting to observe that the dependences $h _ { + } \sim ( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta )$ and $h _ { \times } \sim \cos \theta$ ire a general consequence of eq. (3.72)，whenever $M _ { 1 3 } = M _ { 2 3 } = M _ { 3 3 } = 0$ and eeo igidbodyotatingaroudoeofisrcipais)silli this type of angular dependence.

As for the dependence on $\phi$ ,it can be clearly understood physically:conrary to the oscillating mass of the previous problem,now the source is not nvariant under rotations around the $z$ axis,since at any given value of $t$ the mass $\mu$ is ata specific point along the orbit,which is changed by a rotation around the $z$ axis； therefore $h _ { + }$ and $h _ { \times }$ havea dependence on $\phi$ However, since the orbit is circular,a rotation of the source by an angle $\Delta \phi$ around the zaxis is the same asa time translation $\Delta t$ with $\omega _ { s } \Delta t = \Delta \phi$ ,and therefore the dependence of $h _ { + }$ and $h _ { \times }$ on $\phi$ is only through the combination $\omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } + \phi$

From the observational point of view,we have only access to the radiation that abinary star emits in the direction which points from the star toward us.The angle $\theta$ is therefore equal to the angle $\iota$ between the normal to the orbit and the line-of-sight (see Fig.3.6).The distance $r$ to an astrophysical source is,for most practical purposes,a constant.56 As long as,during the observation,we can neglect the proper motioh of the source (which however sometimes is not the case,as we will discuss ih Section 7.6),also the angle $\phi$ is fixed, so we have $\omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } + \phi = \omega _ { s } t + \alpha$ with $\alpha = \phi - \omega _ { s } r / c$ a fixed constant. Then we can shift the origin of time so that $2 \downarrow _ { s } t + 2 \alpha  2 \omega _ { s } t$ plus an integer multiple of $2 \pi$ s0 $\cos ( 2 \omega _ { s } t + 2 \alpha )  \cos ( 2 \omega _ { s } t )$ and $\sin ( 2 \omega _ { s } t + 2 \alpha )  \sin ( 2 \omega _ { s } t )$ Therefore,this observer can write the GWs received by a binary system (as long as the approximation of a fixed circular drbit is valid,see Section 4.1) as

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle h _ { + } ( t ) = \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { 4 G \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } R ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 4 } } \left( \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } \iota } { 2 } \right) \cos ( 2 \omega _ { s } t ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle h _ { \times } ( t ) = \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { 4 G \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } R ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 4 } } \cos \iota \sin ( 2 \omega _ { s } t ) . } } \end{array}
$$

If we se the orbit edge-on, $\iota = \pi / 2$ ,then $h _ { \times }$ vanishes and therefore the GW is linearly polarized.Instead,at $\iota = 0$ ， $h _ { + }$ and $h _ { \times }$ have the same amplitude; i this case,since the former is proportional to $\sin ( 2 \omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } )$ while the latter $1 0 \cos ( 2 \omega _ { s } t _ { \mathrm { r e t } } )$ ,in the plane $( h _ { + } , h _ { \times } )$ the radiation describes a circle parametrized by $t$ ,that is,the radiation is circularly polarized.To understand what this means in more physical terms,we consider the pattern of lines of force orresponding to a circular polarization. If we have a purely plus polarization, $k _ { 4 } = A _ { + } \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t$ ,according to eq.(1.96) we havea force field

$$
{ \bf F } _ { + } = - 2 \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } A _ { + } { \bf v } _ { + } \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t ,
$$

where the vector field $\mathbf { v } _ { + } ( x , y )$ has components $( x , - y )$ ．This force field is shown in Fig.1.2.If instead we have a purely cross polarization of the form $h _ { x } = A \times \sin { 2 \omega _ { s } t }$ ,according to eq.(1.96) we Havea force field

$$
\mathbf { F } _ { \times } = - 2 \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } A _ { \times } \mathbf { v } _ { \times } \sin 2 \omega _ { s } t ,
$$

where the vector field $\mathbf { v } _ { \times } ( x , y )$ has components $( y , x )$ This force feld is hown in Fig.1.3.If we have both $h _ { + } = A _ { + } \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t$ and $h \times = A \times \sin { 2 \omega _ { s } t }$

56As we will discuss in Section 7.6, there are situations in which we must take into account corrections due to motion of the Earth around the Sun, or more precisely around the Solar System Barycenter (SSB).So,more generally,we can take $r$ to be the distance from the source to the SSB. This however is only important for observations lasting at least few months.We will see in Section 4.1 that,in ground-based interferometers,the gravitational waves emitted bya binary system are observable only for about the last 15 minutes before the system coalesces.

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/fcbdf4d9398dca7c3b8649991e8ab8dee855507de57bce21cac82c5811cc1e0a.jpg)  
Fig. 3.6 The geometry of the problem in a frame $( x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime } , z ^ { \prime } )$ where a fixed observer is at large distance along the positive $z ^ { \prime }$ axis. The normal to the orbit makes an angle $\iota$ with the $z ^ { \prime }$ axis.

and furthermore $A _ { + } = A _ { \times } \equiv A$ ,we have

$$
\mathbf F = - 2 \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 2 } A [ \mathbf v ] _ { + } \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t + \mathbf v \times \sin 2 \omega _ { s } t ] .
$$

Observe that V+ and Vx are orthogonal,V+·Vx =O,and therefore the patten of lines of forces described by eq.(3.335)is the same as the pattern of Fig.12 that rotates uniformly so that at $\omega _ { s } t = 0$ it is the same as Fig.1.2,at $\omega _ { s } t = \pi$ 4 it is the same as Fig.1.3,and so pn.

Atintermediate values oft thelamplitudes for h+ and hx are different and therefore we have elliptic polarization,i.e.in the plane (h+,hx） the radiation describes an ellipse parametrized|by t.We see that,from a measurement d the degree of polarization,i.e.of the relative amplitude of $h _ { + }$ and $h _ { \times }$ ,we cal deduce the inclination $\iota$ of the orbit.

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/f5895ed15c3b57a98c2a2db436ed84090e2ea66e45b411bdf6d19d9da3457bc3.jpg)  
Fig.3.7 The function $g ( \theta )$ in polar coordinates. The angle $\theta$ is measured from the vertical axis.

The angular distribution of the radiated power,in the quadrupole approx imation,is obtained as usual using eq. (3.73),

$$
\left( \frac { d P } { d \Omega } \right) _ { \mathrm { \scriptsize { q u a d } } } = \frac { r ^ { 2 } c ^ { 3 } } { 1 6 \pi G } \langle \dot { h } _ { + } ^ { 2 } + \dot { h } _ { \times } ^ { 2 } \rangle .
$$

Inserting here eq. (3.332),and using $\langle \cos ^ { 2 } ( 2 \omega _ { s } t ) \rangle = \langle \sin ^ { 2 } ( 2 \omega _ { s } t ) \rangle = 1 / 2$ we ge

<table><tr><td rowspan="2">（dP d quad</td><td>2Gμ²R g（0），</td></tr><tr><td>πc5</td></tr></table>

where

$$
g ( \theta ) = \left( \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta .
$$

The radiation is maximum at θ= O,i.e. in the direction normal to the plane of the orbit．A polar plot of g(0) is shown in Fig.3.7.Observe that contrary to the angular distribution found in Problem 3.1,g(0) never vanishes since,whatever the angleθ at which an observer is located,there is always a component of the source motion orthogonal to the observer's line-of-sight.

57If oneisinterested onlyinthe total power,rather than in the angular distribution,it is actually simpler to derive it directly from eq.(3.75):we use again the reference frame where the orbit is given by eq.(3.324),so

Integrating eq.(3.337) over the solid angle we get the total power radiated in the quadrupole approximation,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \ddot { M } _ { i j } = 4 \mu \omega _ { s } ^ { 3 } R ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \times \left( \begin{array} { c c c } { - \sin 2 \omega _ { s } t } & { \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t } & { 0 } \\ { \cos 2 \omega _ { s } t } & { \sin 2 \omega _ { s } t } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right) _ { i j } . } \end{array}
$$

Since this is traceless,it is also equal to $\ddot { Q _ { i j } }$ .Plugging this into eq.(3.75)，we recover eq.(3.339).

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { P _ { \mathtt { q u a d } } = { \frac { 3 2 } { 5 } } { \frac { G \mu ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 5 } } } R ^ { 4 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 6 } } \\ & { { \phantom { \ } } = { \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } } { \frac { G \mu ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 5 } } } R ^ { 4 } \omega ^ { 6 } , } \end{array} }
$$

where $\omega = 2 \omega _ { s }$ is the frequency of the GW.57 The energy radiated in one period T= 2π/ws of the source motion is therefore,writing U= wgR,

$$
\langle E _ { \mathrm { q u a d } } \rangle \tau = \frac { 6 4 \pi } { 5 } \frac { G \mu ^ { 2 } } { R } \left( \frac { v } { c } \right) ^ { 5 } .
$$

Similarly to the result found ineq.(3.319),this is suppressed bya factor $( v / c ) ^ { 5 }$ with respect to the energy scale $G \mu ^ { 2 } / R$

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/7fb413f6ca528c6b7e2c339aa5fdc7c28f492a2cd833d7c5497e6eb38d36f4ae.jpg)

In this problem we compute the mass octupole and current quadrupole radiation generated bya binarysystemof reduced mass $\mu$ ,whose center-of-mass mordinate describes a circular trajectory.

Before applying these results to a real binary star,it is important to repeat the caveat already made in the previous problem:in binary systems held together by gravitation,when we wish to study the higher-order terms in $v / c _ { : }$ we can no longer take the space-time as flat.For instance,the general-relativistic corrections to the $1 / r ^ { 2 }$ Newton's law produce modifications to the trajectory, of higherorderin $v / c$ ：As we will see in Chapter 5 when we study the post-Newtonian formalism, this and other effects produce corrections $O ( v ^ { 2 } / c ^ { 2 } )$ to the power radiated in the quadrupole approximation. Since the octupole radiation is itself suppressed by a factor $v ^ { 2 } / c ^ { 2 }$ compared to quadrupole radiation, f we wish to compute the total radiated power to this order,a consistent treatment requires the inclusion of these relativistic corrections to the trajectory, ind will be deferred to Chapter 5.

The purpose of this exercise is therefore qnly to illustrate,in a simplified bit somewhat academic setting of a circular orbit in flat space-time,some Iiteresting features of mass octupole and current quadrupole radiation,which Will e useful for understanding physically some aspects of the correct results lor self-gravitating systems,that will be discussed in Chapter 5.

With this caveat,we can now assgn the orbit and proceed with the comitation.If we want to compute the radiation emitted from the star in the lretion of the observer,it is simpler to use the geometrical setting of Fig. 3.6 labeling now the axes of this figure as $( x , y , z ) _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ rather than $( x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime } , z ^ { \prime } ) )$ , in which the observer is along the $z$ axis.The equatioh of the orbit in this frame is

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { } } & { { } } & { { x _ { 0 } ( t ) = R \cos \omega _ { s } t , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { y _ { 0 } ( t ) = R \cos \iota \sin \omega _ { s } t , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { z _ { 0 } ( t ) = R \sin \iota \sin \omega _ { s } t , } } \end{array}
$$

ind is obtained from an orbit lying in the $( x , y )$ plane,performing a rotation w an angle $\iota$ around the $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { x } }$ axis.We set the observer in the $z$ direction,so we compute the radiation emitted along $\hat { \mathbf { n } } = ( 0 , \ 0 , 1 )$ .For the octupole radiation, m. (3.141) gives

$$
\left( h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } } \right) _ { \mathrm { o c t } } = \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { 2 G } { 3 c ^ { 5 } } \Lambda _ { i j , k l } ( \hat { \bf n } ) \ddot { M } _ { k l 3 } .
$$

As usual, inactual computations,it is more convenient to use $M ^ { i \cdot j k }$ rather than $\mathcal { O } ^ { i j k }$ ）We found in eq.(3.63)that，when $\hat { \mathbf { n } } = ( 0 , 0 , 1 )$ ,in the multilication of a matrix $\ddot { M } _ { k l 3 }$ by the Lambda tensor the components of $\ddot { M } _ { k l 3 }$ mith $k = 3$ or $l = 3$ do not contribute,so we just need to compute $M _ { a b 3 }$ with $_ { 4 , 6 = 1 , 2 }$ With the trajectory given in eq.(3.324) we find,for $a , b = 1 , 2$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbb { M } _ { a b 3 } = \mu x _ { a } ( t ) x _ { b } ( t ) z ( t ) } \\ & { \quad = \mu R ^ { 3 } \sin \iota \sin \omega _ { s } t \left( \begin{array} { c c } { \cos ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } t } & { \cos \iota \sin \omega _ { s } t \cos \omega _ { s } t } \\ { \cos \iota \sin \omega _ { s } t \cos \omega _ { s } t } & { \cos ^ { 2 } \iota \sin ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } t } \end{array} \right) _ { a b } . } \end{array}
$$

ccording to eq.(3.63)，when $\hat { \mathbf { n } } = ( 0 , 0 , 1 )$ the contraction with the Lambda sor amounts to replacing $M _ { 1 1 3 }$ with $( 1 / 2 ) ( M _ { 1 1 3 } - M _ { 2 2 3 } )$ and $M _ { 2 2 3 }$ with $- 1 / 2 ) \big ( M _ { 1 1 3 } - M _ { 2 2 3 } \big )$ .Therefore

$$
\Lambda _ { a b , c d } ( \hat { \bf n } ) M _ { c d 3 } = \mu R ^ { 3 } \sin \iota \sin \omega _ { s } t \times
$$

$$
\times \left( \begin{array} { c c } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \cos ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } t - \cos ^ { 2 } \iota \sin ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } t ) } & { \cos \iota \sin \omega _ { s } t \cos \omega _ { s } t } \\ { \cos \iota \sin \omega _ { s } t \cos \omega _ { s } t } & { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \big ( \cos ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } t - \cos ^ { 2 } \iota \sin ^ { 2 } \omega _ { s } t \big ) } \end{array} \right) _ { a b } .
$$

Taking the third time derivative we find

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( h _ { + } ) _ { \mathrm { o c t } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { G \mu R ^ { 3 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 3 } } { 1 2 c ^ { 5 } } \sin \left[ \left( 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \iota - 1 \right) \cos \omega _ { s } t - 2 7 ( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } \iota ) \cos 3 \omega _ { s } t \right] , } } \\ { { ( h _ { \times } ) _ { \mathrm { o c t } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { G \mu R ^ { 3 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 3 } } { 1 2 c ^ { 5 } } \sin 2 \iota \left[ \sin \psi _ { s } t - 2 7 \sin 3 \omega _ { s } t \right] . \qquad ( 3 . 3 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \alpha ) _ { \mathrm { o c t } } . } } \end{array}
$$

As expected,we have radiation both at $\omega = \omega _ { s }$ and at $\omega = 3 \omega _ { s }$ .Thecurremt quadrupole radiation can be computed similarly;actually,it is even simpler t0 compute first the sum of the mass octupole and current quadrupole radiation which is obtained directly from $\dot { S } ^ { k l , m }$ ,see eq. (3.54).For a non-relativiste point particle of mass $\mu$ (or,equivalently,fora two-body system in the center of-mass,with reduced mass $\mu$ ),eqs.(3.32)and (3.120) give

$$
S ^ { k l , m } = \mu \dot { x } ^ { k } \dot { x } ^ { l } x ^ { m } ,
$$

and the radiation along the $z$ axis is

$$
\left( h _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } } \right) _ { \mathrm { o c t + c q } } = \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { 4 G } { c ^ { 5 } } \Lambda _ { i j , k l } ( \hat { \bf n } ) \dot { S } ^ { k l , 3 } ,
$$

where the subscript“cq” stands for “current quadrupole". The computatio is completely analogous to the one that we just performed above,and gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( h _ { + } ) _ { \mathrm { o c t + c q } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { G \mu R ^ { 3 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 3 } } { 2 c ^ { 5 } } \sin \iota \left[ ( \cos ^ { 2 } \iota - 3 ) \cos \omega _ { s } t - 3 ( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } \iota ) \cos 3 \omega _ { s } t \right] , } } \\ { { ( h _ { \times } ) _ { \mathrm { o c t + c q } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { G \mu R ^ { 3 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 3 } } { 2 c ^ { 5 } } \sin 2 \iota \left[ \sin \omega _ { s } t - 3 \sin 3 \omega _ { s } t \right] . \qquad \quad \displaystyle ( 3 . 3 4 \sin 3 \omega _ { s } t ) . } } \end{array}
$$

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/f8886e4c302bd6c2046f4e9699f4605464af5e6e39086c61de5a98def7df88f8.jpg)  
Fig. ${ \bf 3 . 8 } \ \log _ { 1 0 } [ P ( \omega ) / P ( 2 \omega _ { s } ) ] ,$ asa function of $\omega / \omega _ { s }$ ，for $v / c = 1 0 ^ { - 2 }$ ， including the contributions of the mass quadrupole,of the mass octupole,and of the current quadrupole. The line at $\omega = 2 \omega _ { s }$ is due to the mass quadrupole and each of the two lines at $\omega = \omega _ { s }$ and $\omega = 3 \omega _ { s }$ is the sum of the contributions of the mass octupole and current quadrupole.

Therefore also the current quadrupole contribution,which is the difference between eqs.(3.348)and eqs.(3.345),is a sum of terms with frequencies $\omega _ { r }$ and $3 \omega _ { s }$ .The contribution to the total radiated power from the mass octupole and the current quadrupole is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle P _ { \mathrm { o c t + c q } } = \frac { r ^ { 2 } c ^ { 3 } } { 1 6 \pi G } 2 \pi \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 } d \cos \iota \langle \dot { h } _ { + } ^ { 2 } + \dot { h } _ { \times } ^ { 2 } \rangle } } \\ { { \displaystyle \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 1 6 \pi G } } = \frac { 4 2 4 } { 1 0 5 } \frac { G \mu ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 7 } } R ^ { 6 } \omega _ { s } ^ { 8 } . } } \end{array}
$$

It is interesting to compare the power at $\omega = \omega _ { s } , 3 \omega _ { s }$ (both generated by the mass octupole plus the current quadrupole)，with the power at $\omega =$ $2 \omega _ { s }$ ，generated by the mass quadrupole (and which also receives corrections $O ( v ^ { 2 } / c ^ { 2 } )$ from a post-Newtonian treatment of the orbit).From eq.(3.348) $\mathrm { w e }$ find

$$
P ( \omega _ { s } ) = \frac { 1 9 } { 6 7 2 } \left( \frac { v } { c } \right) ^ { 2 } P ( 2 \omega _ { s } ) ,
$$

and

$$
P ( 3 \omega _ { s } ) = \frac { 1 3 5 } { 2 2 4 } \left( \frac { v } { c } \right) ^ { 2 } P ( 2 \omega _ { s } ) ,
$$

where $P ( 2 \omega _ { s } )$ is the leading-order quadrupole result,eq. (3.339)． In Fig.3.8 we show the relative intensity of the three spectral lines at $\omega = \omega _ { s } , 2 \omega _ { s }$ and $3 \omega _ { s }$ ,for $v / c = 1 0 ^ { - 2 }$ .Observe that the vertical scale is logarithmic.

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/5ff4d6261bfd1e5e3efe45bc5c51cfe023c22c0094164b4310d43f85aa49270e.jpg)

We have seen that the next-to-leading term in the multipole expansion is proportional to $\dot { S } ^ { k l , r n }$ .In this problem we discuss the decomposition of $\dot { S } ^ { k l , m }$ into irreducible representations of the rotation group $S O ( 3 )$ ,and we will understand the group-theoretical origin of the mass pctupole and current quadrupole terms.

Let us recall that the irreducible representations of Lie groups,as for instance $O ( N )$ or $U ( N )$ ，are conveniently expressed in terms of Young diagrams.A Youngdiagram isaset of $n$ boxes,organized into $r$ lines of length $n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } , \ldots , n _ { r }$ with $n _ { 1 } \geqslant n _ { 2 } \geqslant . . . \geqslant n _ { r }$ and $n _ { 1 } + . . . + n _ { r } = n$ .In each box we put an index $i _ { 1 } , \dots , i _ { n }$ For $O ( N )$ each index takes the values $1 , \ldots , N$ ， which is the dimension of the vector representation. The irreducible tensor representations can be obtained antisymmetrizing first over the indices in the columns and then symmetrizing over the indices in the lines.58 For $O ( N )$ we must also remove the traces on all pairs of symmetric indices.For example,a generic tensor with three indices $k , l , m$ is decomposed into irreducible representations of $O ( N )$ as follows.First df all,we remove from the tensor all the traces.Let us call $T ^ { k l m }$ the resulting traceless tensor，so that $T ^ { k m m } = T ^ { m l m } = T ^ { k k m } = 0$ (repeated indices are summed over).Thenthe decomposition of $T ^ { k l r n }$ is shown in Fig.3.9.The Young diagram $( a )$ repreents the tensor obtained symmetrizing $T ^ { k l m }$ over all indices;the diagram $( b )$ represents the tensor obtained antisymmetrizing first over $( k , m )$ ,which gives Pim-Tmlk,and then symmetrizing over the pair (k,l). This gives

$$
\mathrm { d i a g r a m } \left( b \right) : \quad T ^ { k l m } + T ^ { l k m } | - T ^ { m l k } - T ^ { m k l } .
$$

The tensor corresponding to the diagram $( c )$ is obtained similarly,antisymmetrizing first over $( k , l )$ and then symmetrizing over $( k , m )$ ,and gives

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \ddot { } \stackrel { } { \operatorname { d i a g r a m } } ( c ) : \quad T ^ { k l m } + T ^ { m l k } \stackrel { } { - } T ^ { l k m } - T ^ { l m k } \stackrel { } { . } } \end{array}
$$

Finally the Young diagram $( d )$ represents the tensor obtained antisymmetrizingTklm over allindices.59 Counting the independent components we see that traceless,but otherwise generic,tensor with three indices $k , l , m$ ,each taking the values 1,2,3,has l8 components,and that the diagram (a) represents a unsor with seven components,(b) and (c) with five components each,and i) has one component，so we get $1 8 = 7 + 5 + 5 + 1$ .Recall that the repsentations of the rotation group can also be labeled by the spin $\mathcal { S }$ ,and the presentation labeled by $s$ has dimension $2 s + 1$ 、Then,the representation ith dimension seven,i.e.the diagram (a)，corresponds to $s = 3$ ，while the Epresentations with dimension five correspomds to $s = 2$ 、Denoting bys the representation with spin $s$ ,andby $\oplus$ the direct sum of representations,the lecomposition ofFig.3.9reads

$$
\begin{array} { r } { T ^ { k l m } \in \mathbf { 3 } \oplus \mathbf { 2 } \oplus \mathbf { 2 } \oplus \mathbf { 1 } . } \end{array}
$$

We can now understand the decomposition_given in eq. (3.139),in terms of ig.3.9. Our starting point is the tensor $\dot { S } ^ { k \dag , m }$ This is symmetric in $( k , l )$ nd has no special symmetry with respect to|the other indices.Therefore,it not an irreducible representation of $S O ( N )$

First of allobservethat,ineq.(3.138),wecanreplace $\dot { S } ^ { k l , m }$ by the tensor in hichallthe traces havebeenremoved.Infact,subtracting from $\dot { S } ^ { k l , m }$ aterm

58It is more common to first symmetrize over the lines and then antisymmetrize over the columns (see,e.g. Hamermesh (1962), Section 10.6).The two procedures are however equivalent and for our purposes the former is more convenient.

59If,rather than O(N)，we consider $S O ( N )$ ,we must also take into account that there is a globally defined antisymmetric tensor $\epsilon ^ { i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { N } }$ . In this case the representations obtained one from the other contracting antisymmetric indices with the é tensor are equivalent. In particular,for $S O ( 3 )$ we have the tensor $\epsilon ^ { i j k }$ ．So,for instance,an antisymmetric tensor with two indices $A ^ { k l }$ is equivalent to a vector $A _ { i } = \epsilon _ { i k l } A ^ { k l }$ whilea totally antisymmetric tensor $A ^ { i j k }$ is equivalent to a scalar $A$ through the relation $A = \epsilon _ { i j k } A ^ { i j k }$ . Since the indices take the values $i = 1 , \dots , N$ we cannot antisymmetrize over more than $N$ indices,and there are no diagrams with more than $N$ lines,for $O ( N )$ .For $S O ( N )$ a column with $N$ box is equivalent to a scalar,aswe see contracting with the totally antisymmetric tensor,and can be eliminated. Therefore a Young diagram of $S O ( N )$ can always be reduced to an equivalent diagram with no more than $N - 1$ rows.

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { T } ^ { k m } } & { = \left( \left[ \underset { \bar { t } } { \star } \right] \times \left[ \underset { \bar { t } } { \cdot } \right] \right) \times \underset { \bar { t } = 1 } { \overset { \cdot } { \underbrace { \left[ \bar { t } \right] } } } } & { = \left( \left[ \underset { \bar { t } } { \star } \right] \cdot \left[ \underset { \bar { t } } { \star } \right] \right) \times \underset { \bar { t } = 1 } { \overset { \cdot } { \underbrace { \left[ \bar { t } \right] } } } } \\ & { = \underset { \mathrm { ( ) } } { \overset { \cdot } { \underbrace { \left[ \bar { t } \right] } } } \left[ \underset { m } { \overset { \cdot } { \underbrace { \left[ \bar { t } \right] } } } \right] + \underset { \mathrm { ( ) } } { \overset { \cdot } { \underbrace { \left[ \bar { t } \right] ^ { m } } } } + \underset { \mathrm { ( ) } } { \overset { \cdot } { \underbrace { \left[ \bar { t } \right] ^ { m } } } } + \underset { \mathrm { ( ) } } { \overset { \cdot } { \underbrace { \left[ \bar { t } \right] } } } } \\ & { \mathrm { ( a ) } } \end{array}
$$

Fig.3.9 The Young diagrams corresponding to irreducible tensor representatiom with three indices $k , l , m$

proportional toδkl we get in eq.(3.138)afactor proportional to Ajnmd which vanishes because $\Lambda _ { i , j , k k } = 0$ ，while subtracting from $\dot { S } ^ { k l , m }$ a term pro portional to Skm or to δim we get a factor proportional to Aij,kini =0.W can therefore consider Skl,m as a tensor from which all traces have alread been removed (this is analogous to the fact that,in the quadrupole term,w could substitute $M ^ { i j }$ with $Q ^ { i j }$ ).

Figure 3.9 gives the decomposition in irreducible representations ofagener traceless tensorTklHowever，Sklmisnot generic,butissymmetricin(k (and of course remains symmetric also after we remove allits traces). Then when we decompose Skl,m in irreducible representations as inFig.3.9,thed agrams (c)and (d) do not contribute,since the are obtained antisymmetrizing frst over $( k , l )$ which,applied to $\Dot { S } ^ { k l , m }$ ， gives zero. Therefore,in the decom position of Skl,m,only two irreducible representationsappear:

·diagram (a)，which is the totally symmetric and traceless combinatiol and therefore has the symmetries of the mass octupole; · diagrams (b),which has the structure given in eq. (3.352). For a tensol $T ^ { k l m }$ symmetric in $( k , l )$ ,the structure of indices given in eq.(3.352) simplifies to

$$
2 T ^ { k l m } - T ^ { m l k } - T ^ { m k l } .
$$

Identifying Tklm with Pm,l,(which is indeed symmetricin (k,l), w see that this is precisely the structure of indices of the current quadru pole term in eq. (3.139).

We therefore understand that the algebraic identity (3.139) expresses th decomposition of Skl,m in irreducible representations of SO(3). The mass oc tupole corresponds to the Young diagram (a) in Fig.3.9,and is a spin-3 repre sentation,while the current quadrupole corresponds to the Young diagram (b) in Fig.3.9,and is a spin-2 representation. The representations corresponding to the Young diagrams (c) and (d) instead do not appear，because Slm symmetric in $( k , l )$

The fact that pk,m +pl,km2pm,kl is a spin-2 tensor is the originofil name“current quadrupole":the term“quadrupole”refers to the spin-2 nature of the operator,and the term“qurrent”refers to the fact that is is obtained from the momenta of the momentum Pi.

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/aa33beba31ee10a2ece8d27ae2e6ab0189282d5c7b3e11205a78e5d0e89fe857.jpg)

Problem 3.5 Computation of Tem,B2)mi1  ma

In Section 3.5 we have seen that the coefficients of the multipole expansion, ${ \boldsymbol { u } } _ { l m }$ and $\upsilon _ { \ell m }$ ,are given in terms of integrals over the solid angle of the quantity $( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { E 2 } ) _ { i j } ^ { * } { n } _ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot { n } _ { i _ { \alpha } }$ for $u _ { \ell m }$ ,and $( \mathbf { T } _ { l m } ^ { B 2 } ) _ { i j } ^ { \bar { \star } } n _ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot n _ { i _ { \alpha } }$ ,for $v _ { l m }$ ,see eqs. (3.283) and (3.284).In this problem we compute explicitly these integrals.As a first step, we consider

$$
\int d \Omega Y _ { l m } ^ { \ast } n _ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot n _ { i _ { \alpha } } ,
$$

with $l , \alpha$ arbitrary integers.Using eq.(3.234),we can write it as

$$
( y _ { j _ { 1 } , \dots j _ { l } } ^ { l m } ) ^ { * } \int d \Omega n _ { j _ { 1 } } \dots n _ { j _ { l } } n _ { i _ { 1 } } \dots n _ { i _ { \alpha } } .
$$

We can now perform this integral using eq(3.23).If $\alpha \ : < \ : l$ ,in the product of Kronecker delta of eq.(3.23) there are not enough indices of the type $i _ { 1 } , \dots , i _ { \alpha }$ to be contracted with the indices of the type $j _ { 1 } , \dots j _ { l }$ ,and therefore necessarily we have at least one Kronecker delta involving two indices of the group $j _ { 1 } , \dots j _ { l }$ ,e.g.terms containing $\delta _ { j _ { 1 } j _ { 2 } }$ Then,since $y _ { j _ { 1 } , \dots j _ { l } } ^ { l m }$ is traceles, the result is zero.Therefore the integral in eq.(3.356) can be non-vanishing only if $\alpha \geqslant l$ .If $\alpha = l$ ,we found below eq.(3l237) that

$$
\int d \Omega Y _ { l m } ^ { \ast } n _ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot n _ { i _ { l } } = 4 \pi \frac { | l | } { ( 2 l + 1 ) ! ! } \left( y _ { i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { l } } ^ { l m } \right) ^ { \ast } .
$$

Now,observe that $u _ { \mathit { l m } }$ is expressed as a sum over $\alpha$ of terms containing the o-th time derivatives of $S ^ { i j , i _ { 1 } \ldots i _ { \alpha } }$ ,see eq. (3.283).In order of magnitude,if $d$ is the size of the source,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \partial _ { 0 } ^ { \alpha + 1 } S ^ { i j , i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { \alpha } i _ { \alpha + 1 } } \sim { \displaystyle \frac { \omega _ { s } d } { c } } \wp _ { 0 } ^ { \alpha } S ^ { i j , i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { \alpha } } , } } \\ { { = { \displaystyle \frac { \upsilon } { c } } \partial _ { 0 } ^ { \alpha } S ^ { i j , i _ { 1 } \dots i _ { \alpha } } , } } \end{array}
$$

( \partial _ { 0 } ^ { l - 2 } S ^ { \phi , i , j , i - 1 , i - 2 } ) y _ { i j \underline { { i } } , \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot i _ { l - 2 } } ^ { l m + 1 } } \\ & { = \frac { \displaystyle 1 6 \pi } { \displaystyle ( 2 l + 1 ) ! } [ \frac { \displaystyle 1 } { \displaystyle 2 } l ( l - 1 ) ( l + 1 ) ( l + 2 ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } ( \partial _ { 0 } ^ { l - 2 } S ^ { \phi , i , j , i - 1 , i - 2 } ) y _ { i j \underline { { i } } , \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot i _ { l - 2 } } ^ { i m } . } \end{array}

where the final integral as been performed using eq. (3.23). The integral for Uim is performed similarly using eqs.(3.363)and (3.364),with the final result given in the text.

# Further reading

·The quadrupole radiation is discussed in all general relativity textbooks,see in particular Weinberg (1972)，Misner，Thorne and Wheeler (1973), Landau and Lifshitz,Vol.II(1979) and Straumann (2004).

·The radiation from sources with arbitrary velocity is discussed in Weinberg (1972)，Section 10.4. Gravitational wave generation is also discussed in detail in the reviews Thorne (1983) and (1987).

· Radiation reaction for slow-motion sources is discussed in Misner,Thorne and Wheeler (1973),Sections 36.8 and 36.11.

·The multipole expansion for time-dependent fields in terms of STF tensors was introduced by Sachs (1961)and Pirani (1964).Thorne (1980) derived the slow-motion expansion of the mass and spin multipole moments,both in STF and in spherical tensor form.The closed-form expression for these moments in STF form is derived in Damour and Iyer (l99la).A detailed review of the multipole expansion for GWs,as well as a historical overview of the relevant literature,is Thorne (198O)．A phys ical discussion of current quadrupole radiation is given in Schutz and Ricci (2001).

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/a72097d2cafae4311bf9ec874c9a170c532ac34d5e0a7ebeb8f45c4f3cca509b.jpg)

# Applications

In this chapter we apply the formalism that we have developed to various instructive problems. The systems that we examine here are still somewhat idealized,compared to real astrophysical sources.This allows us to understand the essence of the phy\$ical mechanisms with a minimum of complications,and forms the basis fora more detailed study of realistic sources,which will be the subject of Vol.2.

We begin,in Section 4.1,with the study of binary systems,taking the bodies as point-like and moving at first dn a Newtonian trajectory. We will compute how the back-reaction of GWs affects the motion of the sources,inducing the inspiral and coalescence of the binary system,and we will see how this,in turn,affects the emission of the GWs themselves.

In Section 4.2 we willcompute the radiation emitted by spinning rigid bodies,which are a first idealization of rotating neutron stars.

In Section 4.3 we compute the radiation emitted by a body falling radially into a black hole.A full resolution of this problem requires expansion over the Schwarzschild metrid,rather than over flat spacerime of linearized theory,and will be deferred to Vol. 2. However,we will see that the low-frequency part of the spectrum can be computed using a flat space-time background,so we can perform here this part f the computation.We will also compare the situation in which the infalling particle is point-like with that of a real star,which can be disrupted by the tidal gravitational force of the black hole. This is particularly instructive because it allows us to compare the coherent and the incoherent emission of GWs.

In Section 4.4 we study the radiation emitted by a mass accelerated bv an external force.It will be interesting to compare the results with the electromagnetic radiation from an accelerated charge.We will see that,while the electromagnetic field of a relativistic charge is beamed imto a small angle in the forward directiqn,this does not happen in the gravitational case.Finally,some computational details are collected in A Solved Problems section,at the end of the chapter.

# 4.1 Inspiral of compact binaries

In this section we consider a binary system made of two compact stars, such as neutron stars or black holes,and we treat them as point-like, with masses $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 }$ ，and positions $\mathbf { r } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathbf { r } _ { 2 }$ .In the Newtonian approximation，and in the center-of-mass frame (CM),the dynamics reduces to a one-body problem with mass equal to the reduced mass

4
