$\mathsf { h } _ { ( \mathrm { M } \times \mathrm { M } _ { i j } ) } ^ { \alpha \beta }$   
$\mathsf { \Omega } ^ { \mathsf { n } } ( \mathsf { M } _ { i j } \times \mathsf { M } _ { k l } )$

$$
Q _ { l } ( x ) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log { \left( \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } \right) } - a _ { l } + O \left[ ( x - 1 ) \log ( x - 1 ) \right] ,

where the constant $\begin{array} { r } { a _ { l } \equiv \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { l } \left| k ^ { - 1 } \right. } \end{array}$ .Fromthis,hanging arblefro $z$ to $y = ( z - r ) / c$ ，we get

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \bigsqcup _ { \mathrm { r e t } } ^ { - 1 } \Big [ \frac { n _ { L } } { r ^ { 2 } } H \big ( t - r / c \big ) \Big ] = \frac { c n _ { L } } { 2 r } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } d y H ( t - \frac { r } { c } - y ) \Big [ \log \Big ( \frac { c y } { 2 r } \Big ) + 2 a _ { l } \Big ] } } \\ & { } & { + O ( \frac { \log r } { r ^ { 2 } } ) . \qquad ( 5 . 1 6 \big ) } \end{array}
$$

Thus,asymptotically this is $O ( 1 / r )$ ，and contributes to the radiation field. We can now compute the hereditary term in (M×Mig），considering for definiteness the component $\alpha = \beta = 0$ Inserting eq. (5.147) into $N ^ { \alpha \beta } [ \mathsf { h } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { h } _ { 1 } ]$ and keeping only the mixed monopole-quadrupole terms we find43

$$
N ^ { 0 0 } [ \mathsf { h } , \mathsf { h } ] _ { ( \mathrm { M } \times \mathrm { M } _ { i j } ) } = - \frac { 8 \mathrm { M } } { r ^ { 2 } c ^ { 4 } } n _ { i } n _ { j } \mathrm { M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 4 ) } + O \left( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \right) .
$$

Using eq. (5.160) we therefore find

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \sf h } _ { ( { \bf M } \times { \bf M } _ { i j } ) } ^ { 0 0 } = \frac { 8 \bf M } { r c ^ { 4 } } n _ { i } n _ { j } \int _ { r } ^ { \infty } d z Q _ { 2 } \left( \frac { z } { r } \right) { \bf M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 4 ) } ( t - z / c ) } } \\ { { + \mathrm { ~ i n s t a n t a n e o u s ~ t e r m s } . } } \end{array}
$$

Theinstantaneous terms are straightforwardly computed using eq.(5.158 Similarlyonecancompute theothertensorcomponentsof $\mathsf { h } _ { ( \mathrm { M } \times \mathrm { M } _ { i j } ) } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ as well as the quadrupole-quadrupole term (MxM），and one finds that they all have hereditary Contributions,besides the instantaneous terms.44

Using the asymptotic expansion (5.162) we can now compute the contribution of $\mathsf { h } _ { 2 } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ to the radiation fieldat infinity,and therefore to the radiative multipole moments. For $\mathrm { U } _ { L }$ we get

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \mathrm { U } _ { L } ( u ) = \mathrm { M } _ { L } ^ { ( l ) } ( u ) + \displaystyle \frac { 2 G \mathrm { M } } { c ^ { 3 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { + \infty } d \tau \mathrm { M } _ { L } ^ { ( l + 2 ) } ( u - \tau ) \left[ \log \left( \displaystyle \frac { c \tau } { 2 r } \right) + \kappa _ { l } \right] } } \\ { { + O \left( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 5 } } \right) , \qquad \quad \quad ( 5 . 1 \mathrm { ~ a } ) } } \end{array}
$$

where, as usual, $u = t - r / c$ and

$$
\kappa _ { l } = \frac { 2 l ^ { 2 } + 5 l + 4 } { l ( l + 1 ) ( l + 2 ) } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { l - 2 } \frac { 1 } { k } .
$$

We see that the second term in eq. (5.165) is a $O ( 1 / c ^ { 3 } )$ correction (i.e. a 1.5PN correction) to the leading result given in eq. (5.146). The $\log r$ term in the correction is typical of the harmonic coordinate system that we are using and,as we mentioned, can be eliminated going to radiative coordinates. From the study of the logarithmic terms at infinity one finds that retarded time $U$ in radiative coordinates is related to the harmonic coordinates $( t , r )$ by

$$
U = t - \frac { r } { c } - \frac { 2 G \mathrm { M } } { c ^ { 3 } } \ln \left( \frac { \eta } { r _ { 0 } } \right) + { \cal O } \left( G ^ { 2 } \right) ,
$$

where $r _ { 0 }$ is the arbitrary constant discussed in Note 29 on page 258, and provides a scale for the logarithm.Its arbitrariness corresponds to a freedom in the choice of a system of radiative coordinates,and in particular we see from eq.(5.167) that it can be reabsorbed into a shift in the origin of retarded time $U$ .So it is a a gauge-dependent constant that will not influence any physical result,as it is already clear from the fact that the starting expression (5.165) is independent of $r _ { 0 }$ . In terms of these radiative coordinates,eq.(5.165)becomes

$$
\begin{array} { l c r } { { \displaystyle \mathrm { U } _ { L } ( U ) = \mathrm { M } _ { L } ^ { ( l ) } ( U ) + \frac { 2 G \mathrm { M } } { c ^ { 3 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { + \infty } d \tau \mathrm { M } _ { L } ^ { ( l + 2 ) } ( U - \tau ) \left[ \log \left( \frac { c \tau } { 2 r _ { 0 } } \right) + \kappa _ { l } \right] } }  \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + O \left( \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 5 } } \right) , ~ } } \end{array}
$$

le. inside the logarithm, $r$ is replaced byl $r _ { 0 }$ . Similarly,for the currentiype multipoles one finds

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \mathrm { V } _ { L } ( U ) = \mathrm { S } _ { L } ^ { ( l ) } ( U ) + \frac { 2 G \mathrm { M } } { c ^ { 3 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { + \infty } d \tau \mathrm { S } _ { L } ^ { ( l + 2 ) } ( U - \tau ) \left[ \log \left( \frac { c \tau } { 2 r _ { 0 } } \right) + \pi _ { l } \right] } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + O \left( \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 5 } } \right) , ~ } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\pi _ { l } = \frac { l - 1 } { l ( l + 1 ) } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { l - 1 } \frac { 1 } { k } .
$$

Ofcourse,the relation between the radiative and algorithmic moments is Heded with the highest accuracy for the lowest multipole moments,since he contribution of higher multipoles to the radiation field is suppressed higher powers of $1 / c$ 、In particular,to compute the GW production a binary system up to 3PN,we need the relation between the $l = 2$ ass moment $\mathrm { U } _ { i j }$ and $\mathrm { M } _ { i j }$ up to 3PN order,which is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \vert _ { \boldsymbol { q } } ( U ) = \mathbf { M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( U ) + \frac { 2 G \mathsf { M } } { c ^ { 3 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { + \infty } d \tau \mathsf { M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 4 ) } ( U - \tau ) \left[ \log \left( \frac { c \tau } { 2 r _ { 0 } } \right) + \frac { 1 1 } { 1 2 } \right] } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ - \frac { 2 G } { 7 c ^ { 5 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { + \infty } d \tau \mathsf { M } _ { a \langle i } ^ { ( 3 ) } ( U - \tau ) \mathsf { M } _ { j \rangle a } ^ { ( 3 ) } ( U - \tau ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ - \frac { G } { c ^ { 5 } } \left[ \frac { 2 } { 7 } \mathsf { M } _ { a \langle i } ^ { ( 3 ) } \mathsf { M } _ { j \rangle a } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \frac { 5 } { 7 } \mathsf { M } _ { a \langle i } ^ { ( 4 ) } \mathsf { M } _ { j \rangle a } ^ { ( 1 ) } \right] - \frac { 1 } { 7 } \mathsf { M } _ { a \langle i } ^ { ( 5 ) } \mathsf { M } _ { j \rangle a } - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \epsilon _ { a b \langle i } \mathsf { M } _ { j \rangle a } ^ { ( 4 ) } \mathrm { S } _ { b } \Bigg ] } }  \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l c r } { { + \displaystyle \frac { 2 G ^ { 2 } \mathsf { M } ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 6 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { + \infty } d \tau \mathsf { M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 5 ) } ( U - \tau ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \times \left[ \log ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { c \tau } { 2 r _ { 0 } } \right) + \frac { 5 7 } { 7 0 } \log \left( \frac { c \tau } { 2 r _ { 0 } } \right) + \frac { 1 2 4 6 2 7 } { 4 4 1 0 0 } \right] } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + { \cal O } \left( \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 7 } } \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

The 1.5PN correction,in thefirst line,is the hereditary monopole quadrupole term in $\mathsf { h } _ { 2 } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ that we have computed above. In thesecom line we have a hereditary 2.5PN contribution,due to the quadrupole quadrupole term in $\mathsf { h } _ { 2 } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ ,andinthethirdlinewehavetheinstantae contribution from this quadrupole-quadrupole term. The term propor tional to $G ^ { 2 } \mathrm { { M ^ { 2 } } }$ is a monopole-monopole-quadrupole term in the secom post-Minkowskian iteration $\mathsf { h } _ { 3 } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ ,and again it is ahereditary term.

Below we discuss the physics behind these non-local contributions First,we observe that the above results allow us to compute the wave form and,from it,we can obtain the energy radiated at infinity. Thi can be obtained inserting eq.(5.144) into the expression for the radiate energy,eq.(1.153). The result for the radiated power $P$ ，as a functiom of retarded time $U$ ,is (compare with the linearized result,eq. (3.210))

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { { \cal P } = \displaystyle \sum _ { l = 2 } ^ { + \infty } \frac { G } { c ^ { 2 l + 1 } } \left\{ \frac { ( l + 1 ) ( l + 2 ) } { ( l - 1 ) l l ! ( 2 l + 1 ) ! ! } \langle \mathrm { U } _ { L } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( U ) \mathrm { U } _ { L } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( U ) \rangle \right. } \\ & { } & { \left. + \frac { 4 l ( l + 2 ) } { c ^ { 2 } ( l - 1 ) ( l + 1 ) ! ( 2 l + 1 ) ! ! } \langle \mathrm { V } _ { L } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( U ) \mathrm { V } _ { L } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( U ) \rangle \right\} . } \end{array}
$$

For example, up to 2PN order,this equation gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle P = \frac { G } { c ^ { 5 } } \biggl \{ \frac { 1 } { 5 } \mathrm { U } _ { i j } ^ { ( 1 ) } \mathrm { U } _ { i j } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 1 8 9 } \mathrm { U } _ { i j k } ^ { ( 1 ) } \mathrm { U } _ { i j k } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \frac { 1 6 } { 4 5 } \mathrm { V } _ { i j } ^ { ( 1 ) } \mathrm { V } _ { i j } ^ { ( 1 ) } \right] } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 4 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 9 0 7 2 } \mathrm { U } _ { i j k m } ^ { ( 1 ) } \mathrm { U } _ { i j k m } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \frac { 1 } { 8 4 } \mathrm { V } _ { i j k } ^ { ( 1 ) } \mathrm { V } _ { i j k } ^ { ( 1 ) } \right] + { \cal O } \left( \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 6 } } \right) \biggr \} , } } \end{array}
$$

(where the average is understood),compare with the 1PN result of lin earized theory given in eq. (3.156).

# Physical meaning of hereditary terms. “Non-linear memory and “tails”

At first sight,the appearance of terms that are not instantaneous i quite surprising. For an interaction that propagates in flat space a the speed of light,the signal detected at time $t _ { 0 }$ ,coming from a source at a distance $r$ ,depends only on the instantaneous state of the sourc at retarded time $u _ { 0 } = t _ { 0 } - r / c$ ，so it depends on the source multipok moments and on its derivatives,all evaluated at $u = u _ { 0 }$ .However,the sources that generates GWs also curve space-time,so GWs necessarily propagate in a curved space.We see that,as a consequence,besides the instantaneous terms there are also hereditary terms,given by integrals over retarded time from $- \infty$ to $u _ { 0 }$ ,which therefore depend on the value of the multipole moments at all times $u \leqslant u _ { 0 }$

In other words,the intuition stemming from flat space-time suggested that GWs propagate on the light cones,while we find that they rather propagate both on and inside the light cones.45 It is as if the gravitational interaction did not propagate just with speed $c$ ，but with all possible speeds $0 < v \leqslant c$

Physically,this result canbe better understood using a field-theoretica. language,in terms of back-scattering of gravitons.For instance,the L.5PN hereditary term,in the frst line of eq.(5.171),depends both on derivatives of the quadrupole moment $\mathrm { M } _ { i j }$ ,which in a field-theoretical language is associated to a graviton line,and on the mass M of the source,so it corresponds to scattering of gravitons off the background curvature generated by the mass $M$ of the source. The 2.5PN hereditary term in the second line of eq. (5.171) rather corresponds to a gravitongraviton scattering process (in the language of Feynman graphs, it would be related to a three-graviton vertex),while the terms proportional to $G ^ { 2 } M ^ { 2 }$ is a higher-order correction to the scattering ofa graviton offthe xternal curvature. In this sense,gravitons always propagate locally at the speed of light. However,their arrival time is delayed because they ran repeatedly scatter back and forth,either with the background gravitational field or among themselves. The same effect is known to take place in the propagation of the electromagnetic field in curved spacetime. However,while conceptually it is legitimate to consider the propagation of electromagnetic waves in flat space-time,where they just propagate on the light cone,for GWs this limiting case strictly speaking loes not exist， since the source that generates GWs necessarily proces also a curvature of the background space-time,and also because GWs scater among themselves (or,in the language of Feynman graphs, lecause of non-Abelian graviton vertices). So, this propagation inside he light cone unavoidably occurs,when one takes into account the PN orrctions.We see hereareflection of the fact that gravity is an intrinically non-linear theory.

Looking more closely at the hereditary terms in eq. (5.171),we can istinguish between two types of terms,with and without the factor gT inside the integral. We examine first the $2 . 5 \mathrm { P N }$ hereditary term in he second line. Introducing $V = U - \tau$ ,this integral can be writen as

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle F _ { i j } ( U ) \equiv \int _ { - \infty } ^ { U } d V \mathbb { M } _ { a  i  } ^ { ( 3 ) } ( V ) \mathbb { M } _ { j  \rangle a } ^ { ( 3 ) } ( V ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } d V K ( U , V ) \mathbb { M } _ { a  i  } ^ { ( 3 ) } ( V ) \mathbb { M } _ { j  \rangle a } ^ { ( 3 ) } ( V ) , } } \end{array}
$$

45Indeed it was already known since the 1950s,from the study of the initial value problem in general relativity,that,given some initial data on a space-like hypersurface $S$ ,todetermine the gravitational field at a point $P$ we need not only the values of the initial data on the intersection of $S$ with the past (curved-space) light cones of $P$ but also the data inside this intersection,see the Further Reading.

ere the kernel ${ \cal K } ( U , V ) = \theta ( U - V )$ is fat and equal to one for $V < U$ ， nd vanishes for $V > U$ . Consider a source whose multipole moments re constant in the far past,46 then it becomes active,and finally is med off at some value of time which,for a far observer at a given istance $r$ ，corresponds to a given value of retarded time，say $U =$ For $U > U _ { 0 }$ wecansplit theintegralineq.(5.174)as $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { U ^ { \ast } } d V =$

46This was among the basic assumptions of the method,see Note 24 on page 255 and guarantees the convergence of the integral at $V = - \infty$

v+su .However,since the integrandin eq.(5.174) vanishe for $V > U _ { 0 }$ ,the integration between $U _ { 0 }$ and $U$ gives zero, and for all $U > U _ { 0 }$ we have $F _ { i j } ( U ) = F _ { i j } ( U _ { 0 } )$ : the integral remains frozen forevel at the value it had at $U = U _ { 0 }$ .Thus,the contribution to the Gw amplitude due to this term rerhains non-zero even after the source has been switched off.

47The existence of such a memory effect was also found by Christodoulou, from a rigorous mathematical study of the asymptotic behavior of the gravitational feld at null infinity,and is also known as the “Christodoulou memory"．The term“non-linear”distinguishes it from a memory effect that exists already in linearized theory,see the Further Reading section.

This is due to the fact that, in eq. (5.174)，very remote times are weighted as much as more recent times,since the kernel $\kappa ( U , V )$ is fat function of the integration variable $V$ ，for $V < U$ Therefore, the result is really determined by the cumulative history of the source,in cluding its very remote past.For this reason,it is called a “memory effect”,and the 2.5PN hereditary term in the second line of eq. (5.171) which is non-linear in $\mathrm { M } _ { i j }$ ,is called the ${ } ^ { 6 6 } 2 . 5 \mathrm { P N }$ non-linear memory integral".47 Observe however that,taking the time derivative of $F _ { i j } ( U )$ we obtain an instantaneous term. Thus in the energy flux,which i determined by $\dot { h } _ { i j } ^ { \mathrm { T T } }$ ,the2.5Poleteiesta taneous contribution.

Consider next the 1.5PN hereditary term in thefirst line of eq.(5.171) Introducing again $V = U - \tau$ ,we get an integral of the form

$$
G _ { i j } ( U ) \equiv \int _ { - \infty } ^ { U } d V { \mathrm M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 4 ) } ( V ) \log \left( \frac { U - V } { 2 P _ { 0 } } \right) ,
$$

where $P _ { 0 } = r _ { 0 } / c$ is an arbitraryconstant with dimensions of time. We split

$$
\int _ { - \infty } ^ { U } d V = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \psi - 2 P } d V + \int _ { U - 2 P } ^ { U } d V ,
$$

and,in the first integral,we integrate twice by parts.Using the fact that the derivatives of $\mathrm { M } _ { i j }$ go tb zero sufficiently fast for $U \to - \infty$ ,we get

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { G _ { i j } ( U ) = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 P } \mathrm { M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( U - 2 P ) \displaystyle + \int _ { U - 2 P } ^ { U } d V \mathrm { M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 4 ) } ( V ) \log \left( \displaystyle \frac { U - V } { 2 P _ { 0 } } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ - \int _ { - \infty } ^ { U - 2 P } \displaystyle \frac { d V } { ( U - V ) ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( V ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The terms in the first line involve only values of $V$ in the “recent past" $U - 2 P \leqslant V \leqslant U$ ,while the contribution from the remote past, $- \infty <$ $V \leqslant U - 2 P$ is in the integral ih the second line. This way of rewriting $G _ { i j } ( U )$ allows us to understand that the contribution of very remote times, $V  - \infty$ ，is weighted with a quadratically decreasing kernel $( U - V ) ^ { - 2 }$ ,contrary to what happens in the memory integral, where the kernel is flat and very remote times contribute as much as recent times A contribution of the type (5.177) is called a “tail integral". Thus,the first line in eq. (5.171) gives the 1.5PN tail integral,and the last line gives the 3PN tail integral.

In conclusion, the GWs emitted by a source which at some value ol time suddenly switches off, can be considered as made by three distinct pieces: the wavefront, which is due to the instantaneous terms; a tail, which effectively travels at a slower speed and therefore arrives later, and which smoothly fades away with time；and,finally,a“memory”, which is a persistent DC (i.e. zero-frequency) contribution.

# 5.3.5Radiation reaction

# Radiation radiation in electromagnetism

Radiation reaction is a classical problem that was first studied in electromagnetism (the pioneering works were by Lorentz,in 1892 and 1902, andby Planck,in1897).48 As a warm-up,letusrecall these classical re sults. Consider a system of electric charges $e _ { a }$ moving under their mutual Imfluence.Being accelerated by their interaction,they radiate electromagnetic waves.This emisson costs energy,which must be drainedfrom the mechanical energy of the system.This means that there must be a force which acts on these charges and performs the work necessary to acount for the energy loss. This force is called the back-reaction force due to the emission of radiation,or simply the radiation reaction.

In other words we expect that,when we compute the total electromaghetic field due to a system of charges in mutual interaction, to a term describing electromagnetic waves in the far zone should correspond a erm in the near zone, that describes a radiation reaction force acting n the charges.This is indeed the case. The dynamics in the near zone an be studied starting from the expression for potential (Φ,A) in terms bf the charge density p and the current density j,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \phi ( t , { \bf x } ) = \int d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { 1 } { | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | } \rho ( t - | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | / c , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \bf A } ( t , { \bf x } ) = \frac { 1 } { c } \int d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { 1 } { | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | } { \bf j } ( t - | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | / c , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

We perform an expansion for small retaitdation effects, just as we have one for the gravitational feld when we have discussed the PN expanion,and we insert the result in the Lagrangian for a point-like charge in this external field,which is

$$
L _ { a } = - m c ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - \frac { v _ { a } ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 2 } } } - e _ { a } \phi ( \mathbf { x } _ { a } ) + \frac { e _ { a } } { c } \mathbf { v _ { a } } { \cdot } \mathbf { A } ( \mathbf { x } _ { a } ) .
$$

he result (see Landau and Lifshitz, Vol.II (1979), Sections 65 and 75) that,up to second in v/c,the Lagrangian is conservative,i.e. depends ly on the positions and velocities of the particles,and reads

$$
= \sum _ { a } \frac { 1 } { 2 } m _ { a } v _ { a } ^ { 2 } + \frac { m _ { a } v _ { a } ^ { 4 } } { 8 c ^ { 2 } } - \sum _ { a > b } \frac { e _ { a } e _ { b } } { r _ { a b } } \left\{ 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 c ^ { 2 } } [ \mathbf { v } _ { a } \cdot \mathbf { v } _ { b } + ( \mathbf { v } _ { a } \cdot \hat { \mathbf { r } } _ { a b } ) ( \mathbf { v } _ { b } \cdot \hat { \mathbf { r } } _ { a b } ) ] \right\} .
$$

he analogous result for the gravitationalfeld was given in eqs. (5.55) d(5.56).） To this order only even powers of v/c enter,and the term mear in $v / c$ vanishes because of charge conservation. Indeed,were it

48 Actually,thefact thatafinite sped of propagation of the interaction induces aradiation-reaction force was already proposed by Laplace in 1776,in the context of the gravitational interaction of the Earth-Moon system,and for this reason it has also been called the Laplace effect.See Chapter 2 of Kennefick (2007).

not for electromagnetic-wave lemission,only even powers of $v / c$ could appear,for the same argument based on timc-reversal that we discussed on page 239 for the gravitational field. Starting from $O ( v ^ { 3 } / c ^ { 3 } )$ however. we have a non-vanishing contribution to the expansion of the potentials $\phi , \mathbf { A }$ ，which therefore must be the sought-for radiation-reaction term Indeed, expanding $\phi$ and $\mathbf { A }$ to this order,one finds that the correspond ing electric feld is

$$
{ \bf E } = \frac { 2 } { 3 c ^ { 3 } } \overleftrightarrow { \mathbf { d } } ,
$$

where $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { d } = \sum _ { a } e _ { a } \mathbf { x } _ { a } } \end{array}$ is the electric dipole moment of the system, whil the corresponding magnetic field vanishes. This electric field exerts on a charge $e _ { a }$ a force $\mathbf { F } _ { a } = e _ { a } \mathbf { E }$ , so the total work performed on all the charges is

$$
\sum _ { a } \mathbf { F } _ { a } { \cdot } { \mathbf { v } _ { a } } = { \frac { 2 } { 3 c ^ { 3 } } } { \overset { . . . } { \mathbf { d } } } { \cdot } \sum _ { a } e _ { a } \mathbf { v } _ { a } .
$$

Taking the time average and integrating by part, we get

$$
\langle \sum _ { a } { \bf F } _ { a } { \bf v } _ { a } \rangle = - \frac { 2 } { 3 c ^ { 3 } } \langle \ddot { { \bf d } } ^ { 2 } \rangle .
$$

This is just the negative of the energy radiated away in electromagneti waves in the dipole approximation. We see that the work done by the radiation reaction force,computed from a near-zone expansion, matches exactly the energy carried by the radiation field at future null infinity.

# Radiation reaction from GWs

In Section 4.1.3 we computed the effect of GW emission on the orbit of a binary system simply requiring that the energy and angular momentum carried away from the GWsat a given time,were drained from the orbita energy and angular momentum of the source at the corresponding value of retarded time. This is unavoidable in linearized theory,since energy and angular momentum must be conserved (and,for compact bodies we will see that their internal structure inffuences the dynamics only starting from 5PN order,see|page 288,so to order smaller than 5PN there is no internal degree of freedom that can relax, supplying the required energy). However in the full non-linear theory,given the non linear phenomena in the propagation of the GWs from the source to infinity that we have discussed,it is no longer obvious that the energy and angular momentum carried away by GWs at a large distance $r$ and time $t$ ,are balanced by losses of the system at the corresponding retarded time $t - r / c$

Anyway, we have by now all the tools necessary to verify explicitly the correctness of this energy-balance argument,sincc we have in principle determined,in an expansion in $v / c$ ,both theradiation feldat infinity and the metric in the near region. The latter determines the equation ol motion of the matter source. For a binary system,the equation of motion takes the form already schematically written down in eq. (5.68). The terms $O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } )$ and $O ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } )$ in eq. (5.68) are the 1PN and 2PN corrections respectively,and are non-dissipative (the Lagrangian giving the equations of motion up to $O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } )$ was explicitly written down in eqs. (5.55) and (5.56)).They describe various general relativistic corrections to the orbit,such as the periastron advance,etc.

We now want to find the leading term in the back-reaction,i.e. the first term which is odd in $\mathcal { V }$ (and that cannot be set to zero with a gauge transformation). This leading term can obtained by replacing $\tau _ { \mu \nu }$ with theenergy-momentumtensorofmatter $\dot { T } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ Then,eq.(5.132) becomes simply

$$
\mathsf { h } ^ { \alpha \beta } ( t , \mathbf { x } ) = - \frac { 4 G } { c ^ { 4 } } \int d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { 1 } { | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | } T ^ { \alpha \beta } ( t - | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | / c , \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

Since $T ^ { \alpha \beta }$ has compact support, we can expand its argument $t - | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | / c$ im powers of $| { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | / c$ , just as we did for electromagnetism,and the resulting integrals are convergent.49 One then finds that a number of lowest-order term vanish because of mass and momentum conservation, or they can be set to zero with a gauge transformation. The first nonanishing terms which are odd under time-reversal and cannot be gauged way are $O ( 1 / c ^ { 7 } )$ in $g _ { 0 0 }$ ， ${ \cal O } ( 1 / c ^ { 6 } )$ in $g _ { 0 i }$ ,and $O ( 1 / c ^ { 5 } )$ in $g _ { i j }$ , i.e., they re a 2.5PN correction to the metric (5.3). One can then compute the ate of dissipation of energy due to these non-conservative terms,and me finds that the result reproduces the Einstein formula for the emis-Tion of radiation in the quadrupole approximation,in linearized theory, (3.75).This computation was first performed by Chandrasekhar and Esposito (1970).

In the 197Os and early 1980s,however,the subtleties in the use of the PN expansion,that we have discussed at length in this chapter，were Iot yet fully understood,and the PN éxpansion was used over all of pace,including the far region.As we have seen,this unavoidably promces divergences in higher order. So,one had reproduced the correct idiation reaction,but as a term of an expansion in which subsequent ms diverge； not a very satisfying state of affairs. This was at the Msis of a controversy over the validity of determining the back-reaction mthe sources from the energy balance argument,and over the validity Einstein quadrupole formula itself, when applied to self-gravitation stems.The issue is particularly important,as we will see in the next apter,for its application to the change in orbital periods of binary mlsars,which constitutes the first experimental evidence of GW emis-ROm.

Nowadays,we know that the correct formalism implies a diferent ratment of the near- and far-field regions,and the PN result to all der is given by eqs.(5.132),(5.133) and (5.141),and is explicitly finite anks to the FP prescription, that coimes out from a correct use of forinalism. Thus,the 2.5PN radiation reaction is now part of a stematic and well-defined expansion. For a compact binary system is term is responsible for the decrease in the orbital period $P _ { b }$ The rt that we can compute it directly from the PN expansion in the near gion,without invoking any energy balance argument,provides a direct

49Since the integrand has compact support,the finite part prescription is unnecessary.Observe also that the homogeneous term given by eqs.(5.133) and (5.141) does not contribute to the leading radiation-reaction term (it contributes only starting from order 4PN).

50Actually，the existenceof hereditary terms was first observed in the 4PN near-region metric,see Blanchet and Damour (1988）and later in the radiation field at infinity.

and satisfying way of deriving the theoretical prediction for $\dot { P } _ { b }$ . As we will see in Chapter 6,this prediction has been confirmed by the observation in binary pulsars.

A check of the energy balance argument to even higher orders is technically more difficult. We have seen that the quadrupole radiation al infinity corresponds,in the near region,to a 2.5PN correction to the metric. Thus,the 1PN correction to the radiation feld corresponds to a 3.5PN correction to the near-region metric. Of course, to check the energy balance argument beyond leading order becomes more and more diffcult,since it requires the computation of higher and higher orden in the PN expansion of the near metric. For compact binary, the full near-zone metric has been explicitly computed up to 3.5PN order,and shown to be consistent with the loss of energy and angular momentum at infinity,see the Further Reading.Furthermore,even for the tail integra it has been possible to check explicitly the energy balance argument,and the tail term in the radiation at infinity has been shown to be correctly reproduced, for general PN sources, by a corresponding non-hereditary term in the near-region field.Since the tail integral is a 1.5PN cor rection to the radiation at infinity,the corresponding hereditary term appears in the 4PN near-regionmetric.50 To this order,one finds inded a hereditary correction to $g _ { 0 0 }$ given by

$$
\delta g _ { 0 0 } ( t , { \bf x } ) = - \frac { 8 } { 5 c ^ { 1 0 } } x ^ { i } x ^ { j } \mathrm { M } ( t ) \int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } d t ^ { \prime } \log \left( \frac { t - t ^ { \prime } } { 2 P } \right) \mathrm { M } _ { i j } ^ { ( 7 ) } ( t ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

with $P$ a time-scale.We see that it depends on the mass M and on the mass quadrupole $\mathrm { M } _ { i j }$ , just as the monopole-quadrupole terms $\mathsf { h } _ { ( \mathrm { M } \times \mathrm { M } _ { i j } } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ computed,in the far region,injeq.(5.164),and it has the same logarith mic singularity at the upper limit of the integral.

The explicit computation shows that the terms in the metric thal correspond to back-reaction (i.e. the terms described by antisymmetri waves,see Note 35) can be written as

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \left( \mathsf { h } _ { 1 } ^ { 0 0 } \right) _ { \mathrm { a n t i s y m } } = - \displaystyle \frac { 4 } { G c ^ { 2 } } V _ { \mathrm { r e a c t } } , } \\ & { } & { \left( \mathsf { h } _ { 1 } ^ { 0 i } \right) _ { \mathrm { a n t i s y m } } = - \displaystyle \frac { 4 } { G c ^ { 3 } } V _ { \mathrm { r e a c t } } ^ { i } , } \\ & { } & { \left( \mathsf { h } _ { 1 } ^ { i j } \right) _ { \mathrm { a n t i s y m } } = - \displaystyle \frac { 4 } { G c ^ { 4 } } V _ { \mathrm { r e a c t } } ^ { i j } , } \end{array}
$$

(plus terms that can be set to zero with a gauge transformation). Up to 4PN order in the near-zone metric (which gives the corrections up to_1.5PN order to the radiation reaction force)，the tensor potential $V _ { \mathrm { r e a c t } } ^ { i j }$ can be neglected, while the scalar potential $V _ { \mathrm { r e a c t } }$ and the vector potential $V _ { \mathrm { r e a c t } } ^ { i }$ are given by51

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { V _ { \mathrm { r e a c } } ( t , { \bf x } ) = - \frac { G } { 5 c ^ { 5 } } x _ { i j } \mathrm { I } _ { i j } ^ { ( 5 ) } ( t ) + \left. \frac { G } { c ^ { 7 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 1 8 9 } x _ { i j k } \mathrm { I } _ { i j k } ^ { ( 7 ) } ( t ) - \frac { 1 } { 7 0 } { \bf x } ^ { 2 } x _ { i j } \mathrm { I } _ { i j } ^ { ( 7 ) } ( t ) \right] \right. } \\ & { } & { \left. - \frac { 4 G ^ { 2 } \mathsf { M } } { 5 c ^ { 8 } } x _ { i j } \int _ { 0 } ^ { + \infty } d \tau \log \Big ( \frac { \tau } { 2 } \Big ) \mathrm { I } _ { i j } ^ { ( 7 ) } ( t - \tau ) + { \cal O } \left( \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 9 } } \right) , ( 5 . \mathsf { M } ) \right. } \end{array}
$$

$$
V _ { \mathrm { r e a c } } ^ { i } ( t , \mathbf { x } ) = \frac { G } { c ^ { 5 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 1 } \hat { x } _ { i j k } \mathrm { I } _ { j k } ^ { ( 6 ) } ( t ) - \frac { 4 } { 4 5 } \epsilon _ { i j k } x _ { j m } \mathrm { J } _ { k m } ^ { ( 5 ) } ( t ) \right] + O \left( \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 7 } } \right) .
$$

To 2.5PNorderinthemetric,onlythetermproportioal to $\mathrm { I } _ { i j } ^ { ( 5 ) }$ in eq. (5.190) contributes while the terms proportional to $1 / c ^ { 7 }$ in eq. (5.190) are a 3.5PN correction,and the tail integral in $V _ { \mathrm { r e a c } }$ is a 4PN correction. Similarly, taking into account the factor $1 / c ^ { 3 }$ in eq. (5.188),and comparing witheq.(5.3)，eseetht $V _ { \mathrm { r e a c } } ^ { i }$ is a $3 . 5 \mathrm { P N }$ correction. Thus, to 2.5PN order,the result reduces to the Burke-Thorne potential that we already discussed in eq.(3.114) (since $\mathrm { I } ^ { i j }$ ,to lowest order in $v / c$ ,reduces to the quadrupole moment $Q ^ { i j }$ of linearized theory).

The energy loss can be computed directly in terms of the potentials $V _ { \mathrm { r e a c } }$ and $V _ { \mathrm { r e a c } } ^ { i }$ ,and fully agrees with the energy loss computed from the radiative field at infinity.52 It is interesting to observe that the vector component $V _ { i }$ of the radiation-reaction potential is responsible for the loss of linear momentum, i.e. for the recoil of the center-of-mass of the source due to GW emission,and balances exactly the value that can be found computing the flux of linear momentum at infinity.

# 5.4The DIRE approach

We now discuss an approach due to Will, Wiseman and Pati and termed DIRE(Direct Integration of the Relaxed Einstein Equation) by the auhors.This formalism is similar in spirit but different in technical details rom the Blanchet-Damour approach. It can be proved,however, that he two formalisms are completely equivalent. For reasons of space we will limit ourselves to a brief description,referring the reader to Pati md Will (2000) for more details.

The basic strategy of this method isto start from eq. (5.83),and to terate the solution in a slow-motion $( v \ll c )$ ，weak-field $\left( \left| \mathsf { h } _ { \mu \nu } \right| \ll 1 \right)$ pproximation,in order to obtain in a systematic way the corrections to Imearized theory. To zeroth order we set $\mathsf { h } _ { \mu \nu } \equiv ^ { ( 0 ) } \mathsf { h } _ { \mu \nu } = 0$ over all spacefime, which means that ${ } ^ { ( 0 ) } g _ { \mu \nu } = \eta _ { \mu \nu }$ If we denote by $( { \cal N } ) _ { \mathsf { h } _ { \mu \nu } }$ the result ofthe $N$ -th iteration and by $( N ) _ { \mathcal { T } } \alpha \dot { \beta }$ the value of $\tau ^ { \alpha \beta }$ when $\dot { \mathsf { h } } _ { \mu \nu } = ^ { ( N ) } \mathsf { h } _ { \mu \nu }$ fhen the iterative rule is

$$
{ } ^ { ( N + 1 ) } \mathsf { h } ^ { \alpha \beta } ( t , \mathbf { x } ) = - \frac { 4 G } { c ^ { 4 } } \int d ^ { 4 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { { } ^ { ( N ) } \tau ^ { \alpha \beta } ( t ^ { \prime } , \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } ) \delta ( t ^ { \prime } - t + | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | / c ) } { | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | } .
$$

The equation of motion of matter is then obtained imposing the $N$ -th eration of the De Donder gauge condition (5.71), $\partial _ { \beta } ^ { ( N ) } \mathsf { h } ^ { \alpha \beta } = 0$

Setting ${ } ^ { ( 0 ) } \mathsf { h } _ { \mu \nu } = 0$ we have ${ \bf ( 0 ) } _ { \tau ^ { \alpha \beta } } = T ^ { \alpha \beta } \mathrm { \Lambda }$ , and the first iteration ${ } ^ { ( 1 ) } \mathsf { h } _ { \mu \nu }$ lives back the result of linearized theory,eq. (3.8). Since $\begin{array} { r } { \left( 0 \right) _ { \mathcal { T } } \alpha \beta = { T } ^ { \dot { \alpha } \beta } } \end{array}$ as compact support,the integral is well defined and no divergence ppears at this stage. The first PN correction is obtained computing $h _ { n } \alpha \beta$ This requires plugging ${ ( 1 ) } _ { \mathfrak { h } ^ { \alpha \beta } }$ in the expression for $\tau ^ { \alpha \beta }$ Since $1 h ^ { \alpha \beta }$ already includes GWs propagating to infinity, now the source is longer restricted to a compact reglon，and one must be careful in

handling the integral.

To compute the right-hand side of eq.(5.192) we proceed as follows. We consider a bound system,whose center of mass is taken to be at the origin of the coordinate system,and whose radial extension is always smaller or equal than a value $d$ ，We define the source zone as the world tube

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0008_pages_0281-0320/ocr/images/ae6b33e664ca4b4c52c9bfd2ff8ba712e8c7fd019358dcc4f1f0dbd487587fbd.jpg)  
Fig.5.2 The past light cone of the point $\mathrm { P _ { \mathrm { : } } }$ and the cylinder which bounds the near zone $\mathcal { D }$ Here the point $\mathrm { P }$ is in the far region.

$$
\begin{array} { r } { T = \left\{ x ^ { \alpha } \vert \eta < d , - \infty < t < \infty \right\} . } \end{array}
$$

Outside $\tau$ the energy-momertum tensor of matter vanishes, $T ^ { \alpha \beta } = 0$ We next introduce the length-scale $\mathcal { R } = \left( c / v \right) d$ . The near zone is defined by the world tube

53Thisisslightly different from the definition of $\mathcal { R }$ that we used discussing the Blanchet-Damour formalism,where we preferred to keep $\mathcal { R }$ (much）smaller than $( c / v ) d$ ,to make sure that the expansion parameter in the near zone is much smaller than one.Here however $\mathcal { R }$ is a formal parameter that separates the integral into an inner and an outer part,and whose cancellation,when we resum the two parts,will be checked explicitly,see below,so its precise value is irrelevant.For definiteness we assign it the value $( c / v ) d$ ，following Pati and Will (2000).

$$
\mathcal { D } = \{ x ^ { \alpha } | r | < \mathcal { R } , - \infty < t < \infty \} \ ,
$$

while the radiation zone is defined as the region at $r > \mathcal { R }$ .53 The Dirac delta in eq.(5.192) tells us that,in order to compute the feld $( N + 1 ) _ { \mathsf { h } ^ { \alpha \beta } }$ at a point $P = ( t , \mathbf { x } )$ ，we must integrate over the past flat-space null cone of $P$ .As we see from Fig.5.2,this past null cone intersects the world tube $\mathcal { D }$ in a hypersurface $\mathcal { N }$ (which of course is a three-dimensional hypersurface,but in Fig. 5.2 we suppressed one spatial dimension). In the figure we illustrated the situation in which $\mathrm { P }$ is in the far zone. In general, we wish tocompute $\left( N + 1 \right) _ { \mathsf { h } } \alpha \beta$ both when $\mathrm { P }$ is in the far zone (since this allows us to compute the GWs emitted) and when $\mathrm { P }$ isin the near zone,since this deteimines the equation of motion of matter. A picture similar to Fig.5.2 can be drawn when the tip of the cone,P is inside the cylinder that (in this simplified $2 + 1$ dimensional picture) bounds $\mathcal { D }$ For definiteness,in the following we specialize to the case where $\mathrm { P }$ is in the far region.

The integration over the past null cone $\mathcal { C }$ can then be split into an integration over $\mathcal { N }$ (defined as the part of the null cone which is in the near region） and an integration over the remainder ${ \mathcal { C } } - { \mathcal { N } }$ The integration over $\mathcal { N }$ gives,carrying out first the integration over $t ^ { \prime }$ ，

$$
^ { ( N + 1 ) } \mathsf { h } _ { N } ^ { \alpha \beta } ( t , \mathbf { x } ) = - \frac { 4 G } { c ^ { 4 } } \int _ { \mathcal { N } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { ^ { ( N ) } \tau ^ { \alpha \beta } ( t - | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | / c , \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } ) } { | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | } .
$$

Within $\mathcal { N }$ the integration variable $\mathbf { x } ^ { \prime }$ satisfies $| { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | < \mathcal { R }$ while, since $\mathrm { P }$ is in the far zone,we have $r \equiv | { \dot { \mathbf { x } } } | > { \mathcal { R } }$ . Therefore we can expand the $\mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime }$ dependence, in both occurrences of $\left| \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } \right|$ in the integrand,in powers of $| \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | / r$ This gives

$$
{ } ^ { ( N + 1 ) } \mathsf { h } _ { \mathcal { N } } ^ { \alpha \beta } ( t , \mathbf { x } ) = - \frac { 4 G } { c ^ { 4 } } \sum _ { l = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { l } } { l ! } \partial _ { L } \left[ \frac { 1 } { r } M ^ { \alpha \beta L } ( u ) \right] ,
$$

where

$$
M ^ { \alpha \beta L } ( u ) = \int _ { \mathcal { M } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \tau ^ { \alpha \beta } ( u , { \bf x ^ { \prime } } ) { x ^ { \prime } } ^ { L } .
$$

Here $u = t - r / c$ ,and $\mathcal { M }$ is the intersection of the near-zone world-tube $\mathcal { D }$ with the constant-time hypersurface $t _ { \mathcal { M } } = u$ . The integral is therefore expressed in terms of the multipole moments of $\tau ^ { \alpha \beta }$ ,and is explicitly convergent because the region $\mathcal { M }$ is bounded. For GWs,we are interested only in the spatial components $\mathsf { h } ^ { i j }$ ，and in the term decreasing as $1 / r$ ,so we can bring the factor $1 / r$ in eq (5.196)outside the derivatives. Using

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \partial _ { i } M ( u ) = \displaystyle \frac { \partial u } { \partial x ^ { i } } \frac { d M } { d u } } \\ { = - n ^ { i } \displaystyle \frac { d M } { d u } , } \end{array}
$$

where $n ^ { i }$ is the unit vector in the observation direction,and $d M ( u ) / d u =$ $d M / d t$ ,for GWs eq.(5.196) gives

$$
^ { ( N + 1 ) } \mathsf { h } _ { \mathcal { N } } ^ { i j } ( t , \mathbf { x } ) = - \frac { 4 G } { r c ^ { 4 } } \sum _ { l = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { m ! } \frac { \partial ^ { l } } { \partial t ^ { l } } \int _ { \mathcal { M } } \boldsymbol { d } ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \boldsymbol { \tau } ^ { i j } ( u , \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } ) ( \hat { \mathbf { n } } \cdot \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { l } .
$$

The next step is the computation of the outer integral,that is,of the integral over the region $\mathcal { C } - \mathcal { N }$ .In this outer region the energy-momentum tensor of matter vanishes,and the only contribution to $( N ) _ { \mathcal { T } } \alpha \beta$ comes from $\mathbf { \Phi } ^ { ( N ) } \mathsf { h } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ .We can therefore compute it using the expression of $\mathcal { ( N ) } _ { \sf h ^ { \alpha \beta } }$ ，as determined at the previousiteration level.The domain of integration is slightly complicated geometrically,as we see from Fig. 5.2, but the integration region can be expressed in a manageable form with an appropriate change of variables (see Pati and Will 2Ooo for details).

The original integral over the light cone $\mathcal { C }$ ,eq.(5.192)，was of course ndependent of $\mathcal { R }$ ,which is an arbitrary constant that we have introduced to split it into two pieces,one at $r < \mathcal { R }$ and one at $r > \mathcal { R }$ In contrast,the inner and outer integrals for $\mathsf { h } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ depend separately on the radius $\mathcal { R }$ ,and in particular they are divergent when $\mathcal { R } \to \infty$ .Observe in fact that the contribution of $\mathsf { h } ^ { \alpha \beta }$ to $\tau ^ { \alpha \beta }$ falls off at large distances as some power of $r$ Thus,if $\mathcal { R }$ is taken to infinity,for sufficiently high values of $\it l$ ,i.e.for large multipoles,the integral in eq.(5.199) diverges. This is in fact the divergence to be expected if we try to extrapolate the PN expansion to the far region,aswe discussed in Section 5.1.6. The correct procedure,then,is to keep $\mathcal { R }$ finite,and show that these $\mathcal { R }$ 1 dependent terms cancel against similar contributions from the remainder of the null cone, ${ \mathcal { C } } - { \mathcal { N } }$ ：This cancellation has indeed been proved explicitly to 2PN order (for terms proportional to positive powers of $\mathcal { R }$ ）in Wiseman and Will (1996) and to all orders,with a proof by induction,in Pati and Will (2OoO).This cancellation is of course inevitable, but it has I very important practical consequencelWhen computing for instance he inner integral,one will generally find terms which are independent $i i R$ as well as terms that depend on $\mathcal { R }$ (as a power,or logarithmically). However,we know that all $\mathcal { R }$ -dependent terms must cancel against simlar terms from the outer integral,so in the computation one can simply lrop them,and retain only the $\mathcal { R }$ -independent contributions.Similarly, me can drop all $\mathcal { R }$ -dependent terms in the outer integral.

Thus,one finally has a manifestly fihite and well-defined procedure lor computing systematically all higher-order corrections to linearized theory.

# 5.5Strong-field sources and the effacement principle

Until now we have assumed that the gravitational feld is never strom Our final aim,however,is to apply this formalism to systems containin compact objects such as neutron stars or black holes,in particular a compact binary system which is slowly inspiraling，with an orbita velocity $v \ll c$ For a binary system,the quantity $d$ that determine the characteristic source size is the typical orbital separation,andi satisfies $G m / d \sim ( v / c ) ^ { 2 }$ ，where $m$ is the total mass of the system. denote instead by $r _ { 0 }$ the characteristic size of the two stars (assumed fo notational simplicity to be comparable). Since we are considering th slow inspiral phase,we are in the regime d > ro.

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0008_pages_0281-0320/ocr/images/93d8036672830587a08adcbe9d1aa9bd4dc28aa591c951e8adf496ed88076ad5.jpg)  
Fig.5.3 The near region of a compact binary system. Within two balls,centered on the two stars,of radius of order a few times $r _ { 0 }$ ，the gravitational field is strong.

Using for defniteness the Blanchet-Damour formalism,the three ba sic ingredients are: the post-Minkowskian expansion in the outer soure region,the post-Newtonian expansion in the near region,and the fag that there is an intermediate region where we can match the two expan sions. Even for a binary made of compact objects,when we are at distance $r$ (measured from theicenter-of-mass of the system) of ordero a few times the orbital separation d,gravity is already suffciently wealk Then,from say r =1.5d up tor = o the post-Minkowskian expansio is justifed, even for compact binaries containing neutron stars or blad holes.Furthermore,when v <lc,the near zone extends up to distance R > d,and we have at our disposal a wide region where we can pelr form the matching to the PN solution. So the issue is whether, wit the methods that we have discussed,we can reliably compute the P solution in the near region $r < \mathcal { R }$ . Even in most of the near region th gravitational field is weak; however, within two balls centered on the two stars,of radius of ordera few times ro，see Fig.5.3,the gravitationa feld becomes strong. It is therefore unclear whether the PN expansiol is applicable.

We willseeinthissection that,in spite ofthis legitimate concern,tl formalism that we have developed is indeed valid for compact objects We will also estimate the effect of the internal structure of a compad object,and we will see that it shows up in the equations of motion onl at the very high 5PN order,well beyond the accuracy of existing com putations. This remarkable fact can be traced to a property of genera relativity, which has been termed “the effacement of the internal struc ture”.Since the same phenomenon takes place in Newtonian gravity,w begin with a discussion of this simpler case.54

# Effacement of the internal structure in Newtonian gravity

Treating the bodies as perfect fuids,a $N$ -body system in Newtonial gravity is described by the velocity feld of the fluid, $v ^ { i } ( t , \mathbf { x } )$ ,and by the mass density $\rho ( t , { \bf x } )$ , which is subject to the constraint to have a compacl support consisting of $N$ non-overlapping connected regions. We denot by $p$ the pressure, and we assume an equation of state $p = p ( \rho )$ The dynamics is governed by the continuity equation,

$$
\partial _ { t } \rho + \partial _ { i } ( \rho v _ { i } ) = 0 ,
$$

by the Euler equation

$$
\rho ( \partial _ { t } v ^ { i } + v ^ { j } \partial _ { j } v ^ { i } ) = - \partial _ { i } p + \rho \partial _ { i } U ,
$$

where $U$ is the sign-reversed gravitational potential (therefore $U > 0$ ）， and by Poisson equation

$$
\nabla ^ { 2 } U = - 4 \pi G \rho .
$$

We denote by $V _ { a }$ the volume occupied by the $a$ -th body.By definition, on its boundary $\partial V _ { a }$ we have $\rho = p = 0$ . The mass of the $a$ -thbody is given by

$$
m _ { a } = \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } x \rho ( t , { \bf x } ) ,
$$

and is a constant,thanks to the continuity equation. The center-of-mass poordinates of the $a$ -th body are defned by

$$
z _ { a } ^ { i } ( t ) = \frac { 1 } { m _ { a } } \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } x x ^ { i } \rho ( t , { \bf x } ) .
$$

Diferentiting twice with respect to time and using eqs.(5.200) and 5.201) we get

$$
m _ { a } \frac { d ^ { 2 } z _ { a } ^ { i } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \int _ { V _ { \mathrm { d } } } d ^ { 3 } x f _ { i } ,
$$

ith a force density

The potential $U$ is obtained solving eql(5.202) with the boundary conition that it vanishes at infinity,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal U } ( t , { \bf x } ) = G \int d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { \rho ( t , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } ) } { | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = \sum _ { a = 1 } ^ { N } G \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { \rho ( t , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } ) } { | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | } , } } \end{array}
$$

here,in the second line,we made use of the fact that $\rho$ is non-vanishing mly on the volumes $V _ { a }$ ，with $a = 1 , \ldots , N$ . The potential acting on the th body can therefore be split into a“self-part”,

$$
U ^ { \mathrm { ( s e l f ) , a } } ( t , { \bf x } ) = G \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { \rho ( t , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } ) } { | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | } ,
$$

Id an “external part”,

$$
U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , { \sf a } } ( t , { \bf x } ) = G \sum _ { b \neq a } \int _ { V _ { b } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { \rho ( t , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } ) } { | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | } .
$$

Correspondingly,the force acting on the $a$ -th body is decomposed in a self-force

$$
F _ { i } ^ { ( \mathrm { s e l f } ) , \mathsf { a } } = \int _ { V _ { a } } \mathopen { } \mathclose \bgroup \left| d ^ { 3 } x \left[ - \partial _ { i } p + \rho \partial _ { i } U ^ { ( \mathrm { s e l f } ) , \mathsf { a } } \aftergroup \egroup \right] \aftergroup \egroup \right| ,
$$

(in which we also included the pressure term) and an external force

$$
F _ { i } ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } = \int _ { V _ { \mathrm { a } } } d ^ { 3 } x \rho \partial _ { i } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } .
$$

If one were to make a naive estimate of these two forces, based solely ol dimensional analysis,one would write (assuming for definiteness that al bodies have comparable masses $m$ ） $F ^ { ( \mathrm { s e l f } ) , \bar { \mathfrak { a } } } \sim \overline { { G m ^ { 2 } / r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } }$ and $F ^ { \mathrm { ( e x t ) , a } }$ , $G m ^ { 2 } / d ^ { 2 }$ ,where $r _ { 0 }$ is the typical body size and $d$ is the distance betwee the nearest bodies.Since d ≥ ro,the self-force would be much large than the external force. However,in Newtonian gravity the sclf-foro vanishes exactly. The pressure term in eq. (5.210) vanishes because i is the integral of a gradient,and on the boundary $p = 0$ The second term,using eq. (5.208),is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { F _ { i } ^ { ( \mathrm { s e l f } ) , \mathrm { a } } = G \int _ { V _ { \alpha } } d ^ { 3 } x \rho ( t , { \bf x } ) \frac { \partial } { \partial x ^ { i } } \int _ { V _ { \alpha } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \frac { \rho ( t , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } ) } { | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | } } } \\ & { } & { = - G \int _ { V _ { \alpha } } d ^ { 3 } x \int _ { V _ { \alpha } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } ( x - x ^ { \prime } ) ^ { i } \frac { \rho ( t , { \bf x } ) \rho ( t , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } ) } { | { \bf x } - { \bf x } ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } . } \end{array}
$$

The integrand is odd under the exchange of $x$ with $x ^ { \prime }$ while the integra tion domain is symmetric under this exchange,so thc integral vanishes and there is no self-force. It is however worth observing that the twu factors $\rho ( t , { \bf x } )$ and $\rho ( t , { \bf x } ^ { \prime } )$ entered the above integral in two conceptu ally distinct way. One factor,which is the one explicitly written in th second term in eq. (5.206), is really the density of “passive gravitationa mass，which measures the response of matter to an external gravita tional feld.The second factorwhich enters through eq.(5.207),is th density of the“active gravitational mass”,which is the source for th gravitational field. The fact that these two densities are in fact equali crucial to the vanishing of the integral in eq. (5.212).Thus,the vanish ing of the self-force in Newtonian gravity is a non-trivial result,rooted in the equality between active and passive gravitational mass.

55In principle y should be written as $\mathbf { y } _ { a }$ .However,we willuse it as an integration variable,so its dependence on α will only appear through the integration domain,and we omit its index $\boldsymbol { a }$

In the equation of motion (5l205),the first term that depends on the internal structure of the bodies,and not just on their masses, is ther obtained performing a multipole expansion of the external force. We introduce a coordinate55

centered on the $a$ -th body.Since we have shown that the self-force vanishes,eq.（5.205） becomes

$$
m _ { a } \frac { d ^ { 2 } z _ { a } ^ { i } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } y \rho ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ( t ) + { \bf y } ) \partial _ { i } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , { \bf a } } ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ( t ) + { \bf y } ) .
$$

The density of the $a$ -th body is localized around $\mathbf y = 0$ ,so it is convenient to introduce the notation $\dot { \rho } _ { a } ( t , \mathbf { y } ) \equiv \rho ( t , \mathbf { z } _ { a } ( t ) \dot { + } \mathbf { y } )$ ,while the external potential at ${ \bf z } _ { a } ( t ) + { \bf y }$ can be expanded around the value at the point ${ \bf 2 } _ { a } ( t )$ ,plus small corrections.Thus,the multipole expansion is obtained writing

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \partial _ { i } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } + { \bf y } ) = [ \partial _ { i } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } + y ^ { j } \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 1 } { 2 } y ^ { j } y ^ { k } \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } \partial _ { k } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } + . . . ] ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Inserting this expansion into eq. (5.214) we get

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle m _ { a } \frac { d ^ { 2 } z _ { a } ^ { i } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = [ m _ { a } \partial _ { i } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } + I _ { a } ^ { j } \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { a } ^ { j k } \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } \partial _ { k } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } + . . . ] ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \displaystyle I _ { a } ^ { j } = \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } y \rho _ { a } ( | t , { \bf y } ) y ^ { j } \nonumber , } } } \\ { { { \displaystyle I _ { a } ^ { j k } = \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } y \rho _ { a } ( | t , { \bf y } ) y ^ { j } y ^ { k } \nonumber , } } } \end{array}
$$

and so on. However,the dipole in eq.l (5.217) vanishes identically because of the definitions (5.204)and (5.213).In eq. (5.216) we can replace $I _ { \mathrm { q } } ^ { j k }$ by the quadrupole moment $Q _ { a } ^ { j k } = | I _ { a } ^ { j k } - ( 1 / 3 ) \delta ^ { j k } I _ { a } ^ { l l }$ , since the factor $\delta ^ { j k }$ ， when contracted with $\partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } \partial _ { k } U ^ { \mathrm { ( e x t ) , a } }$ in eq. (5.215)， produces V²U(ext),a,and the Laplacian of the external potential is proportional $1 0 \sum _ { b \neq a } \rho _ { b }$ and therefore vanishes inside the $a$ -th body.Thus,

$$
m _ { a } \frac { d ^ { 2 } z _ { a } ^ { i } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = m _ { a } \partial _ { i } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q _ { \mu } ^ { j k } \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } \partial _ { k } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ) + \ldots .
$$

The monopole term gives Newton's law.The quadrupole term is the first erm which depends on the inner struqture of the body. Each derivaive acting on $U ^ { \mathrm { ( e x t ) , a } }$ brings a contribution $O ( 1 / d )$ ，while each factor in the definition of the multipole moments,after integration over $V _ { a }$ , brings a factor $O ( r _ { 0 } )$ . Thus,the contribution of $I _ { a } ^ { i j }$ is smaller than the monopole term bya factor $O ( r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } / d ^ { 2 } )$ .Furthermore,in the quadrupole moment $Q _ { a } ^ { i j }$ ,only the non-spherical part of the matter distribution contibutes,and this gives another suppression factor, that we denote by $\epsilon$ In general $\epsilon \leqslant 1$ and, in many cases, $\epsilon \ll 1$ .In conclusion,defining

$$
\alpha \equiv \frac { r _ { 0 } } { d } \ll 1 ,
$$

le structure-dependent terms give a correction to Newton's law of order ,when a naive dimensional analysis|that does not take into account at the self-force vanishes exactly, would have rather suggested that ey are larger than the external Newtonian force by a factor $1 / \alpha ^ { 2 }$ verall,the terms that depend on the internal structure of the body are therefore suppressed,with respect to naive expectations,by a factor $\epsilon \alpha ^ { 4 }$ . As we have seen,the eqjality of active and passive gravitational mass is at the origin of this large cancellation,which is known as the "effacement principle”.

In order to generalize these results to Einstein gravity,it is useful to observe that there is a suggestive way of rewriting the Newtonian equations of motion,in a form that only involves surface integrals,rather than volume integrals. We start from eqs.(5.205) and (5.206).We neglect the pressure term,sinde this is a gradient and gives a vanishing contribution,and we use eq.(5.202).Then eq.(5.205) becomes

$$
m _ { a } \frac { d ^ { 2 } z _ { a } ^ { i } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi G } \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } x \ : \nabla ^ { 2 } U \partial _ { i } U ,
$$

where $U$ is the total potential,including the self-potential.Using the identity

$$
\partial _ { j } ( \partial _ { i } U \partial _ { j } U - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \delta _ { i j } \partial _ { k } U \partial _ { k } U ) = \nabla ^ { 2 } U \partial _ { i } U ,
$$

we can rewrite this as

$$
\begin{array} { r } { m _ { a } \frac { d ^ { 2 } \dot { z _ { a } ^ { i } } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \displaystyle \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } x \partial _ { j } t ^ { i j } } \\ { = \displaystyle \int _ { S _ { a } } d S _ { j } t ^ { i j } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
t _ { i j } = - \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi G } \left( \partial _ { i } U \partial _ { j } U - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \delta _ { i j } \partial _ { k } U \partial _ { k } U \right) ,
$$

and $d S _ { j }$ is the two-dimensional surface element,on a surface $S _ { a }$ bounding the volume $V _ { a }$ ,which is arbitrary except that it does not include any other volume $V _ { b }$ with $b \neq a$ .This surface-integral representation of the equation of motion is expecially interesting, since it shows that nothing depends on whether the gravitational field inside the bodies is weak o strong.In principle,one couldleven have a singularity inside the volume $V _ { a }$ ,but the equations of motion can be computed evaluating the “stress tensor” $t ^ { i j }$ on a surface which is far-away from the body (recall that we assumed $d \gg r _ { 0 }$ ，so we can $\mathrm { g } \flat$ to distances parametrically larger than $r _ { 0 }$ before enclosing any other body),where allfields are weak.

# Effacement of the internal structure in Einstein gravity

Just as we have done above for Newtonian gravity,we now show how to write the PN equations of motion of general relativity using surface integrals,using a variant of $\dot { \mathrm { ~ a ~ } }$ classical work by Einstein, Infeld and Hoffmann (1938),developed by Itoh and Futamase.We start from the Einstein equations in relaxed form,in the harmonic gauge.First of all,we want to define the analogous of the center-of-mass coordinates. Observe that in the Newtonian case the center-of-mass coordinates $z _ { a } ^ { i } ( t )$ are such that the mass dipole $\mathcal { I } _ { a } ^ { j }$ in eq.(5.217) vanishes.In the general relativistic case,we define the functions $z _ { a } ^ { i } ( t )$ considering the dipole moment of 700

$$
D _ { a } ^ { i } \equiv \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } y y ^ { i } \tau ^ { 0 0 } ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ( t ) + { \bf y } ) ,
$$

(where $\tau ^ { \mu \nu }$ is the effective energy-momentum tensor defined in eq. (5.73)) and requiring that $D _ { a } ^ { i }$ vanishes or,equivalently, that it takes a specified value.The functions $z _ { a } ^ { i } ( t )$ generalize the Newtonian notion of center-ofmass coordinates,and can be better called “center-of-felds” coordinates, since $\tau ^ { 0 0 }$ include also the contribution to the energy density from the gravitational field. We now define the quantity $P _ { a } ^ { \mu }$ as

$$
P _ { a } ^ { \mu } ( t ) = \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } y \tau ^ { 0 \mu } ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ( t ) + { \bf y } ) ,
$$

so this is an effective four-momentum of the $a$ -th body，which includes also the contribution of the gravitational field.Using the conservation $0 ! \tau ^ { \mu \nu }$ ,eq. (5.82),and the notation $v _ { \alpha } ^ { i } = \dot { z } _ { \alpha } ^ { i }$ ,we find

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { d P _ { a } ^ { \mu } } { d t } = \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } y [ \partial _ { 0 } \tau ^ { 0 \mu } + v _ { a } ^ { i } \partial _ { i } \tau ^ { 0 \mu } ] ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ( t ) + { \bf y } ) } } \\ & { } & { = \int _ { V _ { a } } d ^ { 3 } y [ - \partial _ { i } \tau ^ { i \mu } + v _ { a } ^ { i } \partial _ { i } \tau ^ { 0 \mu } ] ( t , { \bf z } _ { a } ( t ) + { \bf y } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Since $v _ { a } ^ { i }$ is just a function of time,independent of $y$ ，we can carry it outside the integral and we are left with a total derivative. Hence,the variation of $P ^ { \mu }$ is given by a surface integral,

$$
\frac { d P _ { a } ^ { \mu } } { d t } = - \int _ { S _ { a } } d S _ { j } \tau ^ { j \mu } + v _ { a } ^ { j } \int _ { S _ { a } } d S _ { j } \tau ^ { 0 \mu } .
$$

A relation between the “momentum” $P _ { \alpha } ^ { i }$ ,the“energy” $P _ { a } ^ { 0 }$ ,and the velocity $v _ { a } ^ { i }$ of the $a$ -th body can be obtained by taking the time derivative ifeq. (5.225). On the left-hand side we get zero since $D _ { a } ^ { i }$ vanishes (or is nyway a constant),by definition of “center-of-felds” coordinates.On he right-hand side we use the conservation of $\tau ^ { \mu \nu }$ and we integrate by arts, keeping the boundary terms. Then we obtain

$$
\stackrel { \triangledown } { P _ { a } ^ { i } } = P _ { a } ^ { 0 } v _ { a } ^ { i } + Q _ { a } ^ { i } ,
$$

here

$$
Q _ { a } ^ { i } = \int _ { S _ { a } } d S _ { j } y _ { a } ^ { i } \tau ^ { j 0 } - v _ { a } ^ { j } \int _ { S _ { a } } d S _ { j } y _ { a } ^ { i } \tau ^ { 0 0 } .
$$

Inally,taking the time derivative of eq.(5.229)and using eq.(5.228) o compute $d P _ { a } ^ { i } / d t$ and $d P _ { a } ^ { 0 } / d t$ , we arrive at an equation for $d v _ { a } ^ { i } / d t$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { P _ { a } ^ { 0 } \displaystyle \frac { d v _ { a } ^ { i } } { d t } = - \int _ { S _ { a } } d S _ { j } \tau ^ { j i } + v _ { a } ^ { j } \int _ { S _ { \diamond } } d S _ { j } \tau ^ { 0 i } + v _ { a } ^ { i } \int _ { S _ { a } } d S _ { j } \tau ^ { j 0 } } } \\ { { - v _ { a } ^ { i } v _ { a } ^ { j } \displaystyle \int _ { S _ { a } } d S _ { j } \tau ^ { 0 0 } - \displaystyle \frac { d Q _ { a } ^ { i } } { d t } . } } \end{array}
$$

This is an equation of motion for the $a \cdot$ -th body.The remarkable point is that it is written entirely in terms of surface integrals.On the righthand side this is explicit,wlile the quantity $P _ { a } ^ { 0 }$ on the left-hand side can be obtained by integrating the $\mu = 0$ component of eq. (5.228), with the initial condition that,when $v / c \to 0$ ， $P _ { a } ^ { 0 }  m _ { a } c ^ { 2 }$ ,where $m _ { a }$ is the (ADM) massof the body, so even $P _ { a } ^ { 0 }$ is determined by surface integrals.

In conclusion,even if somewhere inside the volumes $V _ { a }$ the gravitational field becomes strong，as is the case for neutron stars,or even if there is a horizon,as for black holes,the evaluation of the equation of motion (5.231) can be done on surfaces far from the sources,at a distance smaller,but of the order of the separation $d$ between the bodies. say at $r = d / 3$ .All these surface integrals therefore only involve weak fields.In other words,we have been able to replace the knowledge of the detailed internal structure of the source with a knowledge of the gravitational field at large distances from it.The equation of motions are the same,independently of whether a given value of the surface integral computed say at a distance $r | = d / 3$ ,was produced bya very relativistic source with strong self-gravity or by a nearly Newtonian source with negligible self-gravity, spread over a larger volume $V _ { a }$ .56 Then,the PN expansion is applicable even to strong field sources.57 Since the surface integral formulation is just an equivalent way of recasting the equations of motion derived from Einstein equations,it follows that even the PN expansion in its original formulation,discussed in Section 5.1,is valid for strong-field sources.

56Observe that this effacement principle is valid in general relativity，but not necessarily in some of its extensions.For instance,in the factor $\epsilon \alpha ^ { 2 }$ that we found in the Newtonian limit, the suppression factor ε is related to the fact that the gravitational field describes a massless particle with helicities $\pm 2$ ，which forces gravitons to couple to the quadrupole moment. In extensions of general relativity that include gravitationally interacting scalar felds this suppression factor is absent, since the scalar couples to the trace of $I _ { i j }$ .Furthermore,in certain scalartensor theories,the local value of Newton's constant $G$ is controlled by the local value of the scalar field. In this case, the inner structure of a body is affected by the presence of a companion,which modifies the value of $G$ inside the first body. Thus,in this strong form,the effacement principle is really a properties specific to general relativity.

The computation of the PNlexpansion with the surface integral method has been performed explicitly,up to 3PN order,by Itoh and Futamase see the Further Reading section,and the results are in full agreement with that found with the direqt PN expansion, with the important added bonus that this computation shows explicitly that the result is valid for strong fields.

# Structure-dependent corrections in compact binaries

We are now in the position to estimate the post-Newtonian order at which corrections that depend on the internal structure of the body show up in the equations of motion of an inspiraling binary. The physical effect that induces a correction in the equations of motion is the fact that the tidal force exerted by the first body distorts the second body,inducing in it a quadrupole moment. The interaction between this quadrupole moment and the first body produces a force,which modifies the orbit.

The above discussion shows that,using the surface integral method. it is possible to perform the computation staying always in a Newtonian weak-field regime,so we expect that a simple estimate of this tidal effect based on a Newtonian description should give the correct order of magnitude. In Newtonian gravity,the tidal force exerted by a body al a distance $d$ on a body of radius $r _ { 0 }$ is of order

$$
F _ { \mathrm { t i d a l } } \sim \frac { G m r _ { 0 } } { d ^ { 3 } } .
$$

The typical ellipticity $\epsilon$ induced by such a force is of order of the ratio of this tidal force to the typical self-gravity,

$$
F _ { \mathrm { s e l f } } \sim \frac { \mathit { G m } } { \mathit { r } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Therefore

$$
\epsilon \sim \frac { F _ { \mathrm { t i d a l } } } { F _ { \mathrm { s e l f } } } \sim \alpha ^ { 3 } ,
$$

where $\alpha = r _ { 0 } / d$ .The corresponding induced quadrupole moment is $Q _ { i j } \sim \epsilon m r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }$ According to eq. (5.219)，with $m \partial _ { k } U ^ { ( \mathrm { e x t } ) , \mathrm { a } } \sim F _ { k } ^ { ( }$ Newton) (where $F _ { k } ^ { ( \mathrm { N e w t o n } ) }$ istheNetoi structure-dependent interbody force of order

$$
{ \cal F } ^ { ( \mathrm { i n d u c e d } ) } \sim \frac { Q _ { i j } } { m } \partial _ { i } \partial _ { j } { \cal F } ^ { ( \mathrm { N e w t o n } ) } \sim \epsilon r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \frac { 1 } { d ^ { 2 } } { \cal F } ^ { ( \mathrm { N e w t o n } ) } .
$$

From eq. (5.234),we then find

$$
F ^ { ( \mathrm { i n d u c e d } ) } \sim \alpha ^ { 5 } F ^ { ( \mathrm { N e w t o n } ) } .
$$

On the other hand, for a compact body we have $r _ { 0 } \sim G m / c ^ { 2 }$ ，and therefore $\alpha \sim G m / ( c ^ { 2 } d )$ .From the virial theorem, $( G m / d ) \sim v ^ { 2 }$ ，so $\alpha \sim ( v ^ { 2 } / c ^ { 2 } )$ .In conclusion,for compact bodies,

$$
F ^ { ( \mathrm { i n d u c e d } ) } \sim \left( { \frac { v } { c } } \right) ^ { 1 0 } F ^ { ( \mathrm { N e w t o n } ) } .
$$

A full relativistic analysis indeed confirms this estimate.So,the first structure-dependent term gives a 5PN efect in the equation of motion. This is well beyond the present state-of-the art which,as we discussed, is the 3.5PN order.

In conclusion (after one has found a consistent regularization of the point-particle singularity) the PN formalism can be legitimately applied even in the presence of strong fields,and the corrections dependent on the internal structure can be neglected up to the extremely high 5PN order.As long as the two bodies are far from the merging stage (hence u/c is not too close to one), these 5PN effects can be neglected,and both the orbital motion and the GWs that are produced are determined miquely by the masses of the bodies,independently of whether the internal structure is highly relativistic or almost Newtonian.58

# 5.6 Radiation from inspiraling compact binaries

The most important application of the above formalism is to the inspiral f compact binaries.We already sawin Section 4.4.1,in the context of linearized theory, that a binary system gradually spirals inward because f the emission of GWs,and the resulting waveform increases in amplitude and in frequency, producing a characteristic “chirp". This long

58In our discussion we have considered spinless bodies.Otherwise,the orbital motion and the GW generation is determined by two parameters for each body, its mass and its intrinsic angular momentum.Both can be obtained measuring the gravitational field at large distances.

inspiral phase is followed bya phase in which the two object plunge toward each other,and mergel The resulting system,typically a black hole,finally settles down to its ground state,radiatingaway the energy stored in its excited modes. This is the so-called “ringdown phase".So, the evolution of a compact binary system can be separated into these three phases:inspiral,merging,and ringdown．The merging phase is particularly difficult to model,and here the detailed nature of the source (e.g.whether we have black holes or neutron stars) is also important. The merger and ringdown phases will be analyzed in detail in Vol.2, which is devoted to the issues in GW physics which depends on the spe cific nature of the source.Here we will rather discuss the inspiral phase. which is universal,at least upito a very high PN order.

# 5.6.1 The need for a very high-order computation

The reason why the computation of the waveform to a very high PN order is crucial,is that GW ekperiments are hunting for signals which are buried in a noise orders of magnitudes larger than the signal itself. To extract such a small signal from the noise there exists a standard technique,called matched filtering, that we will discuss in great detail in Chapter 7,that works if we|know well the form of the sinal.

For an inspiraling compact|binary，we saw in eq.(4.23) that,in a ground-based interferometer, the signal enters into the detector bandwidth,say at $f _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \sim 1 0 ~ \mathrm { H z }$ ，about 17 minutes before the coalescence. and the signal sweeps up in frequency, performing a vary large number of cycles,59 before the two stars merge.In order to exploit optimally the signal present in the detector,and therefore to detect sources at farther distances,we need to have an accurate theoretical prediction of the time evolution of the waveform,and especially of the phase,which is rapidly changing.To understand how stringent is this requirement,and also to write compactly the PN corrections,it is useful first of all to introduce some new notation. In place of the source frequency $\omega _ { s }$ ，we introduce the dimensionless variable

$$
x \equiv \left( \frac { G m \omega _ { s } } { c ^ { 3 } } \right) ^ { 2 / 3 } ,
$$

where $m = m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 }$ is the total mass of the system and $\omega _ { s } = 2 \pi f _ { s }$ is the orbital frequency of the sdurce.Writing $x = [ ( G m / r ) ( r \omega _ { s } / c ^ { 3 } ) ] ^ { 2 / 3 }$ and observing that $\dot { G } m / r \sim v ^ { 2 }$ and $r \omega _ { s } \sim v$ ,we see that

$$
x \left( - { \cal O } \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 2 } } \right) . \right.
$$

Thus,the $v / c$ corrections can be expressed as correction in powers of $x ^ { 1 / 2 }$ .We also define the symmetric mass ratio

$$
\nu \equiv { \frac { \mu } { m } } = { \frac { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } { ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

and the post-Newtonian parameter

$$
\gamma \equiv \frac { G m } { r c ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

which is $O ( v ^ { 2 } / c ^ { 2 } )$ .We finally introduce the dimensionless time variable

$$
\Theta \equiv \frac { \nu c ^ { 3 } } { 5 G m } ( t _ { c } - t ) ,
$$

where $t _ { c }$ is the time at which the coalescence takes place.

We can now rewrite some Newtonian results for a chirping binary, obtained in Section 4.1,in terms of these parameters.In particular, the relation between the frequency and the time to coalescence for circular orbit,eq.(4.19),reads simply (taking into account that in Section 4.1 we computed the radiation emitted by a circular orbit in the quadrupole approximation,so $\omega _ { s } = \omega _ { \mathrm { g w } } / 2 = \pi f _ { \mathrm { g w } } )$

$$
x = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \Theta ^ { - 1 / 4 } .
$$

The accumulated orbital phase,defined by

$$
\phi = \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } d t ^ { \prime } \omega _ { s } ( t ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

can be writen, using eq. (4.30) together with $\Phi = 2 \phi$ (see eq. (4.28)),

as

$$
\phi = \phi _ { 0 } - \frac { 1 } { \nu } \Theta ^ { 5 / 8 } ,
$$

or,eliminating $\Theta$ in favor of $x$ ，

$$
\phi = \phi _ { 0 } - \frac { x ^ { - 5 / 2 } } { 3 2 \nu } .
$$

Finally,the number of cycles spent|in the detector bandwidth can be written, using eq. (4.23), as $\mathcal { N } _ { \mathrm { c y c } } = \mathcal { N } ( f _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ) - \mathcal { N } ( f _ { \mathrm { m a x } } )$ ,where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \mathcal N } ( f ) \equiv \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 3 2 \pi ^ { 8 / 3 } } \left( \frac { G M _ { c } } { c ^ { 3 } } \right) ^ { - 5 / 3 } f ^ { - 5 / 3 } } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { x ^ { - 5 / 2 } } { 3 2 \pi \nu } . } } \end{array}
$$

All these relations receive corrections from the PN expansion,that can be written as an expansion in powers of $x ^ { 1 / 2 }$ , see eq. (5.239),and that will be examined in detail below. In particular,the PN corrections to eq. (5.247) take the form

$$
N ( x ) = \frac { x ^ { - 5 / 2 } } { 3 2 \pi \nu } [ 1 + { \cal O } ( x ) + { \cal O } ( x ^ { 3 / 2 } ) | + { \cal O } ( x ^ { 2 } ) + { \cal O } ( x ^ { 5 / 2 } ) + . . . ] .
$$

If we want to track the evolution of the GW signal, we need a template which reproduces the number of cycles with a precision at least $O ( 1 )$ We seefrom eq. (5.248) that, since the leading term in $\mathcal { N } ( x )$ is proportional to $x ^ { - 5 / 2 }$ , we need to include the corrections up to $O ( x ^ { 5 / 2 } )$ in order to have an error not larger than ${ \dot { Q } } ( 1 )$ on $\mathcal { N } ( x )$ . This means that we need to compute the PN corrections tothe phase at least up to 2.5PN level,i.e. corrections smaller by a factor $( v / c ) ^ { 5 }$ with respect to the leading term Actually, this is not even enough because,once we have accumulated an error of order one on the number of cycles,our template has clearly gone out of phase with the signal,so a more accurate computation is really required in order to exploit optimally the information contained in the output of a ground-based interferometer,at least up to 3PN order,and better yet to 3.5PN.

An equivalent way of understanding the need for a high-order PN computation is to look at the waveform,rather then at the number of cycles $\mathcal { N } ( x )$ ,and to observe that the last term in the expression (4.37) for the phase $\Psi _ { + } ( f )$ of the GW amplitude is,in terms of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { x } }$

$$
\frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( \frac { G M _ { c } } { c ^ { 3 } } 8 \pi f \right) ^ { - 5 / 3 } = \frac { 3 } { 1 2 8 \nu } x ^ { - 5 / 2 } .
$$
