25Observe that $S _ { n } ( \omega )$ has dimensions $\mathrm { H z ^ { - 1 } }$ ti $S _ { n } ^ { 1 / 2 } ( \omega )$ $\mathrm { H z } ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ $S _ { \xi } ( \omega )$ has dimensions $\mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } / \mathrm { H z }$ The sensitivity of the bar is usually displayed plotting either $S _ { n } ( \omega )$ or $S _ { n } ^ { 1 / 2 } ( \omega )$ This is more $S _ { \xi } ( \omega )$ or $S _ { \xi } ^ { 1 / 2 } ( \omega )$ tivity of different experiments like bars and interferometers.In fact,a bar and an interferometer with the same sensitivity to GWs,i.e.with the same $S _ { n } ( \omega )$ ,would have very different spectral density of the displacement,since for bars the length-scale that enters in the transfer function is the bar's length $L = 3 \mathrm { ~ m ~ }$ ，while for interferometers it is the pathlength of the light in the interferometer arms．As we will see,for ground based interferometers this is of the order $1 0 ^ { 2 }$ kms.Therefore,in different detectors,very different values of $S _ { \xi } ( \omega )$ can correspond to the same minimum value of $\tilde { h } ( \omega )$ that can be measured.

the fact that it produces a stochastic force,responsible both for thed sipation term $- \gamma _ { 0 } \dot { \xi } _ { 0 }$ ,and for fuctuations around it. To take fuctuation into account the above equation must be replaced by

$$
m _ { 0 } ( \dot { \xi } _ { 0 } + \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \xi _ { 0 } ) = { \cal F } _ { \mathrm { s t o c } }
$$

with

$$
\langle F _ { \mathrm { s t o c } } \rangle = - m _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 0 } \dot { \xi } _ { 0 } .
$$

We write $F _ { \mathrm { s t o c } } = - m _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 0 } \dot { \xi } _ { 0 } + F ( t )$ ，where $F ( t )$ is a stochastic for that describes the thermal fluctuations,known as the Nyquist force.B definition $\langle F ( t ) \rangle = 0$ .Thermal noise fluctuations can be described by stochastic Gaussian process, since they are the sum of many independen contributions,so the stochastic properties of $F ( t )$ are uniquely define byits average value that,as we have seen,is zero,and by its auto correlation function $\langle F ( t ) F ( t ^ { \prime } ) \rangle$ ．At the microscopic level, $F ( t )$ isdu to atomic collisions,at a rate which can be, say, $O ( 1 0 ^ { 1 9 } )$ collisions pe second.Therefore,on a macroscopic time-scale,the force at time $t$ an at time $t ^ { \prime }$ are completely uncorrelated if $t \neq t ^ { \prime }$ ,and we can write

$$
\langle F ( t ) F ( t ^ { \prime } ) \rangle = A _ { 0 } \delta ( t - t ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

We will see in a moment how to fix the constant $A _ { 0 }$ . The dynamics d $\xi _ { 0 } ( t )$ is now governed by

$$
\ddot { \xi } _ { 0 } + \gamma _ { 0 } \dot { \xi } _ { 0 } + \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \xi _ { 0 } = { \frac { F ( t ) } { m _ { 0 } } } ,
$$

which has the solution $^ { 2 6 } \mathrm { T h e }$ Green's function isselected uniquely imposing the causality condition,i.e. requiring that the particular solution of the inhomogeneous equation,at time $t$ ，depends only on $F ( t ^ { \prime } )$ at $t \leqslant t$

$$
\xi _ { 0 } ( t ) = \xi _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { h o m } } ( t ) + \frac { 1 } { m _ { 0 } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } d t ^ { \prime } G ( t - t ^ { \prime } ) F ( t ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

where $\xi _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { h o m } } ( t )$ is the general solution of the homogeneous equation and $G ( t )$ is a Green's function,i.e.a solution of eq.(8.1l2) with $F ( t ) / m$ replaced by $\delta ( t )$ . The Green'\$ function can be easily found performing the Fourier transform and repeating basically the same steps that lead to the integration of eq. (8.22),26 and we get

$$
G ( t ) = \frac { 1 } { \psi _ { 0 } } \theta ( t ) e ^ { - \gamma _ { 0 } t / 2 } \sin \omega _ { 0 } t ,
$$

where $\theta ( t )$ is the step function, $\theta ( t ) = 0$ for $t < 0$ and $\theta ( t ) = 1$ for $t > 0$ Therefore

$$
\xi _ { 0 } ( t ) = \xi _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { h o m } } ( t ) + \frac { 1 } { m _ { 0 } \omega _ { 0 } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } \mathopen { } \mathclose \bgroup \left| d t ^ { \prime } e ^ { - \gamma _ { 0 } ( t - t ^ { \prime } ) / 2 } \sin [ \omega _ { 0 } ( t - t ^ { \prime } ) ] F ( t ^ { \prime } ) . \aftergroup \egroup \right|
$$

We assume for simplicity that $F ( t )$ is switched on at $t = 0$ ，and that $\xi _ { 0 } ( 0 ) = 0$ and $\dot { \xi } ( 0 ) = 0$ .With these boundary conditions we get, for $t > 0$ ，

$$
\xi _ { 0 } ( t ) = { \frac { 1 } { m _ { 0 } \omega _ { 0 } } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } d t ^ { \prime } e ^ { - \gamma _ { 0 } ( t - t ^ { \prime } ) / 2 } \sin [ \omega _ { 0 } ( t - t ^ { \prime } ) ] F ( t ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

Using eq. (8.111) and introducing $u = t - t ^ { \prime }$ we then obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \langle \xi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) \rangle = \displaystyle \frac { A _ { 0 } } { m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } d u e ^ { - \gamma _ { 0 } u } \sin ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 0 } u } } \\ { { \displaystyle \simeq \frac { A _ { 0 } } { 2 m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \gamma _ { 0 } } ( 1 - e ^ { - \gamma _ { 0 } t } ) \ : , } } \end{array}
$$

where in the last line we neglected terms which are small for $\gamma _ { 0 } \ll \omega _ { 0 }$ The time evolution of the average kinetic plus potential energy of the mode $\xi _ { 0 }$ due to thermal noise is therefore given by

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \langle E ( t ) \rangle = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m _ { 0 } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \langle \xi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \rangle + \frac { 1 } { 2 } m _ { 0 } \langle \dot { \xi } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \rangle } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = m _ { 0 } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \langle \xi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \rangle } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } \displaystyle \simeq \frac { A _ { 0 } } { 2 m _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 0 } } ( 1 \vert - e ^ { - \gamma _ { 0 } t } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

In the limit $t  \infty$ ，the system thermalizes and the equipartition of energy states that $\langle \dot { E } _ { \mathrm { k i n } } ( t ) \dot { \rangle }  ( 1 / 2 ) k T$ and $\langle E _ { \mathrm { p o t } } ( t ) \rangle  ( 1 / 2 ) k T$ ，S0 $\langle E ( t ) \rangle  k T$ .Comparison with eq.(8.118) fixes the value of $A _ { 0 }$ ，

$$
A _ { 0 } = 2 k T m _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 0 } ,
$$

and therefore

$$
\langle E ( t ) \rangle = k T ( 1 - e ^ { - t / \tau _ { 0 } } ) ,
$$

where $\tau _ { 0 } = 1 / \gamma _ { 0 }$ ． This shows that,while asymptotically $\langle E ( t ) \rangle  k T$ ， equilibrium is reached only on a time-scale $t \gg \tau _ { 0 }$ .Ona time-scale $\Delta t \ll \tau _ { 0 }$ ,expanding the exponential in eq.(8.120) we rather get

$$
\left. E ( t = \Delta t ) \right. \approx k T \frac { \Delta t } { \tau _ { 0 } } ,
$$

confirming the physical intuition that led to eq.(8.1O7).It is instructive to realize that the result (8.1l9) is aparticular case of a very general theorem.According to the definition (7.15),which expresses in general the relation between the spectral density and the auto-correlation function of any quantity,the (single-sided) spectral density of the force $F ( t )$ ， that we denote by $S _ { F } ( \omega )$ ,is related to the auto-correlation function of $F ( t )$ , $R _ { F } ( t ^ { \prime } - t ) \equiv \langle F ( t ^ { \prime } ) F ( t ) \rangle$ ，by

$$
\langle F ( t ^ { \prime } ) F ( t ) \rangle = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } { \frac { d \omega } { 2 \pi } } S _ { F } ( \omega ) e ^ { - i \omega ( t ^ { \prime } - t ) } .
$$

Comparison with eq.(8.111) shows that $S _ { F } ( \omega ) = 2 A _ { 0 }$ ,so the spectral density is fat. Equation (8.119) can therefore be seen as a relation between the fluctuations due to the force $F$ (represented by $S _ { F }$ )and the dissipation (represented by $\gamma _ { 0 }$ ），

$$
S _ { F } = 4 k T m _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 0 } .
$$

This result is a particular case of the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, which can be formulated as follows.Let $x ( t )$ bya variable describing

a linear system (either mechanical or electrical) subject to an externa force $F ( t )$ ,and let $v ( t ) = \dot { x } ( t )$ be the velocity. In Fourier space, we ca always cast the equation of motion of a linear system in the general for

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \tilde { F } ( \omega ) = Z ( \omega ) \tilde { v } ( \omega ) . } \end{array}
$$

This defines the impedance $Z ( \omega )$ .Its inverse, $Y ( \omega ) = Z ^ { - 1 } ( \omega )$ ,is called the admittance. The fluctuatipn-dissipation theorem states that th (single-sided) power spectrum of the force responsible for thermal fluc tuations, $S _ { F } ( \omega )$ ,is related to the real part of $Z$ by

$$
S _ { F } ( \omega ) = 4 k T \mathrm { R e } Z ( \omega ) .
$$

For the damped oscillator,eq. (8.112) gives

$$
Z = - { \frac { i m _ { 0 } } { \omega } } ( \omega ^ { 2 } - \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + i \gamma _ { 0 } \omega ) ,
$$

so Re $Z = m _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 0 }$ and we recoyer eq. (8.123).The real part of $Z$ in general is responsible for dissipation in the system,so eq.(8.125)relate fuctuations to dissipation.

We can now compute the spectral density of the noise due to thermal fuctuations,considering for the moment only the bar's mode $\xi _ { 0 }$ .Below we will generalize to the bar-transducer system. Writing the velocity as $\tilde { v } ( \omega ) = - i \omega \tilde { \xi } _ { 0 } ( \omega )$ ,eq. (8.124) can be written as

$$
\tilde { \xi } _ { 0 } ( \omega ) = \frac { 1 } { | - i \omega Z ( \omega ) } \tilde { F } ( \omega ) ,
$$

and therefore

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { S _ { \xi } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( \omega ) = \frac { 1 } { \omega ^ { 2 } | Z ( \omega ) | ^ { 2 } } S _ { F } ( \omega ) } \\ & { ~ = \frac { 4 k T m \gamma _ { 0 } } { \omega ^ { 2 } | Z ( \omega ) | ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

From the explicit forms (8.126)and (8.23)we see that $Z ( \omega )$ is related to the transfer function of the mode $\xi _ { 0 }$ ， $T _ { 0 } ( \omega )$ ,by

$$
T _ { 0 } ( \omega ) = \frac { 2 L } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \frac { m _ { 0 } \omega } { i Z ( \omega ) } .
$$

Therefore we can rewrite eq. (8.128) as

$$
S _ { \xi } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( \omega ) = \frac { 4 k T \gamma _ { 0 } } { \dot { m } _ { 0 } \omega ^ { 4 } } \left( \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 L } \right) ^ { 2 } | T _ { 0 } ( \omega ) | ^ { 2 } .
$$

The contribution to the noise spectral density due to the thermal noise is obtained using eq. (8.106).We see that $| T ( \omega ) | ^ { 2 }$ cancels, and we end up with

<table><tr><td>Sthermal(f)=</td><td>T kT f QMu f4，</td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

where we eliminated $L$ using $\omega _ { 0 } = \pi v _ { s } / L$ ，with $v _ { s }$ the speed of sound in the bar,we used $m _ { 0 } = M / 2$ ，with $M$ the total mass of the bar, % = wo/Qo,and we expressed everything in terms of f = ω/(2π). We can make the following comments.

·We have found that $S _ { \xi } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( \omega _ { \parallel } )$ is proportional to $| T _ { 0 } ( \omega ) | ^ { 2 }$ Since however the contribution of thermal noise to $S _ { n } ( \omega )$ is obtained dividing $S _ { \xi } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( \omega )$ by $| T _ { 0 } ( \omega ) | ^ { 2 }$ ,see eq. (8.106),the transfer function $T _ { 0 } ( \omega )$ cancels,and $S _ { n } ( f )$ shows no special feature around the resonance frequency $f _ { 0 }$ ；rather,it hasa smooth frequency dependence, $S _ { n } ( f ) \sim f ^ { - 4 }$ .This means that,if thermal noise were the only source of noise,resonant bars would be wide-band detectors, that is,their sensitivity would not be limited to a region close to $f _ { 0 }$ ．As we will see below,bars become narrow-band detectors only when we include the noise introduced by the read-out scheme.

n 3S

·We understand the importance of a large quality factor, since we found $S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f ) \sim 1 / Q _ { 0 }$ This is in agreement with eq. (8.107), since ${ \tau _ { 0 } } = Q _ { 0 } / \omega _ { 0 }$ ，S0 $( \Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ) _ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } \sim 1 / Q _ { 0 }$ .Furthermore,we learn from eq.(8.131) that thermal noise can be fought lowering $T$ (obviously),but also taking $\mid _ { M }$ and $v _ { s }$ as large as possible.The dependence $1 / M$ expresses the physically obvious fact that thermal noise is not effective in generating a coherent motion of a massive object. We also see that $S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f )$ decreases at high frequencies, but this result will be modified when we include the transducer.

a as ted

The computation of the noise spectral density performed above takes mto account only the bar mode $\xi _ { 0 }$ .We now consider the bar-transducer ystem,and we compute the effect of thermal noise on the transducer lisplacement.At first sight，eq.(8.131) can be alarming，because it mggests that,if the thermal noise of the bar is proportional to $1 / M$ ， te thermal noise of the transducer should be proportional to $1 / m _ { \mathrm { t } }$ ,and terefore it will be very large,since the transducer is very light.This ould ruin completely the usefulness of the resonant transducer scheme. llowever,eq.(8.131)has been derived fora single oscillator,while here Tehave two coupled oscillators.We now show that it is indeed correct tat,inthe bar-transducer system,the thermal noise in the transducer is $\mathrm { \Phi } _ { \mathrm { } } ( f ) \sim 1 / m _ { \mathrm { t } }$ ,except in a narrow frequency range around the resonant frequency. This will provide a first(but,as it turns out,not the most mportant) reason that limits the bandwidth of resonant bar detectors. To this end，we go back to eq.(8.93)，which gives the transducer lsplacement $\tilde { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } } ( \omega )$ ，when we applya force $F _ { 0 }$ on the bar and a force $F _ { \mathrm { t } }$ mthe transducer. In terms of the transfer function given in eq. (8.95) can write

loise end

$$
\widetilde { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } } ( \omega ) = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 L } \frac { T _ { \mathrm { t } } ( \omega ) } { \omega ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { \widetilde { F } _ { 0 } ( \omega ) } { m _ { 0 } } - \frac { \widetilde { F } _ { \mathrm { t } } ( \omega ) } { m _ { \mathrm { t } } } \frac { \omega ^ { 2 } - \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + i \omega \gamma _ { 0 } } { \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \right] .
$$

compute the effect of thermal noise,we take as $F _ { 0 }$ and $F _ { \mathrm { t } }$ the Nyquist brces acting on the bar's fundamental mode and on the transducer,

respectively. From the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, in the form of eq.(8.123),we have

$$
S _ { F _ { 0 } } = 4 k T m _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 0 } , \qquad S _ { F _ { \mathrm { t } } } = 4 k T m _ { \mathrm { t } } \gamma _ { \mathrm { t } } .
$$

Assuming that the Nyquist forces acting on the bar and on the transducer are uncorrelated we find, for the spectral density of the transducer displacement,

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/503d24b3306ea1831f30e7671de4cb63f01aaafc2a7fc781ea174ad651b58d8c.jpg)

$$
S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ( \omega ) = \frac { \pi ^ { 4 } } { 4 L ^ { 2 } } \frac { | T _ { \mathrm { t } } ( \omega ) | ^ { 2 } } { \omega ^ { 4 } } 4 k T \left[ \frac { \gamma _ { 0 } } { m _ { 0 } } + \frac { \gamma _ { \mathrm { t } } } { m _ { \mathrm { t } } } \frac { ( \omega ^ { 2 } - \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + \omega ^ { 2 } \gamma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } \right] .
$$

As discussed above, the noise spedtral density is obtained dividing by |Tt(ω)²,so we get (using L = πvs/wo,mo = M/2,γo = wo/Qo,n = $\omega _ { 0 } / Q _ { \mathrm { t } }$ and expressing the result inlterms of $f = \omega / 2 \pi$ ）

$$
S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f ) = \pi { \frac { k T } { M v _ { s } ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { f _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } { f ^ { 4 } } } \left[ { \frac { 1 } { Q _ { 0 } } } + { \frac { 1 } { \mu Q _ { \mathrm { t } } } } { \frac { ( f ^ { 2 } - f _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( f f _ { 0 } / Q _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } } { f _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } } \right] .
$$

The term $1 / Q _ { 0 }$ in the bracket islthe thermal noise of the bar， see eq.(8.131). The second term is the transducer thermal noise,and we see that it is indeed proportional tΦ $1 / m _ { \mathrm { t } }$ , so it is enhanced by a factor $1 / \mu$ compared to the bar's noise. What saves the situation is the fact that $1 / m _ { \mathrm { t } }$ is multiplied by a function of $f$ which becomes very small at $f = f _ { 0 }$ ，

$$
S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f _ { 0 } ) = { \frac { \pi } { Q _ { 0 } } } { \frac { k T } { M v _ { s } ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { f _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } { f ^ { 4 } } } \left[ 1 + { \frac { 1 } { \mu Q _ { \mathrm { t } } Q _ { 0 } } } \right] .
$$

Numerically, for $Q _ { 0 } \sim Q _ { \mathrm { t } } \sim 1 0 ^ { 6 }$ ahd $\mu = 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ ， we have $1 / ( \mu Q _ { \mathrm { t } } Q ) \sim$ $1 0 ^ { - 8 }$ ,so at $f = f _ { 0 }$ the transducer thermal noise is completely negligible, and it remains smaller or of the same order of magnitude of the bar's thermal noise as long as $( f ^ { 2 } - f _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } / \big | f _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } \leqslant \mu ( Q _ { \mathrm { t } } / Q _ { 0 } )$ ,or,since $\left( Q _ { \mathrm { t } } / Q _ { 0 } \right) =$ $O ( 1 )$ , as long as $\vert f - f _ { 0 } \vert { \lesssim } f _ { 0 } { \sqrt { \mu } }$

We see that the term $( f f _ { 0 } / Q ) ^ { 2 }$ in eq. (8.135) gives a totally negligible contribution to $S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f )$ even when $f ^ { 2 } - f _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = 0$ so we can neglect it everywhere,and we can write more simply

$$
S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f ) = \pi \frac { k T } { M v _ { s } ^ { 2 } } \frac { f _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } { f ^ { 4 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { Q _ { 0 } } + \frac { 1 } { \mu Q _ { \mathrm { t } } } \frac { ( f ^ { 2 } - f _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { f _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } \right] .
$$

In Fig. 8.8 we plot the function $[ S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ , and the two separate contributions from the bar and the transducer. With the parameters givenin thefigurecaption,at $f = f _ { 0 }$ we get $\left[ S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f _ { 0 } ) \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } \simeq 6 \times$ $1 0 ^ { - 2 2 } \mathrm { H z } ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ Observe that the thermal noise is minimum at $f _ { 0 }$ ,rather than at the normal mode frequencies $f _ { \pm }$ . # 8.3.2 Read-out noise and effective temperature

We now consider the other crucial soutce of noise in resonant detectors, that is, the noise introduced by the read-out scheme.We have seen that the output of a capacitive transducer is a potential, $V$ ，which is modulated by the displacement of the light mass,as in eq. (8.97),and furtheramplified electronically.Any amplifier has however an associated wideband noise,described bya spectral density of the output potential, $S _ { V }$ approximately constant in frequency,and with dimensions volts $^ { 2 } / \mathrm { H z }$ Since $V = \alpha \xi _ { \mathrm { t } }$ ,any fluctuation in the potential due to electronic noise results in an error in the measurement of $\xi _ { \mathrm { t } }$ ,which can be described by a spectral density of the displacement

$$
S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } = { \frac { 1 } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } } S _ { V } .
$$

With good approximation $\alpha$ and $S _ { V }$ are independent of the frequency $S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } }$   
Observe also that $S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathsf { a m p l } }$ can be made smalle increasing $\alpha$ ,that is, trans  
ferring more efficiently the energy from the mechanical oscillators to the   
amplifier.

As we did for thermal noise,we can characterize the amplifier noise eitherin terms of the energy that must be deposited by a GW burst in order to overcome it,or in terms f the noise spectral density.We mnsider frst the energetic point of view. If,as in eq. (8.107),we use a sampling time $\Delta t$ ,the bandwidth is $\Delta f \simeq 1 / \Delta t$ .Then the fluctuations $\mathrm { i n } \ S _ { \mathrm { t } } ^ { 2 }$ due to amplifier noise in such a sampling time are given by

$$
\langle \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } ^ { 2 } ( t ) \rangle = \int _ { f _ { 0 } - \Delta f / 2 } ^ { f _ { 0 } + \Delta f / 2 } d f S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } = S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } \Delta f .
$$

The corresponding minimum value of the detectable energy is therefore given by

$$
\overline { { ( \Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ) _ { \mathrm { a m p l } } = m _ { \mathrm { t } } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \langle \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } ^ { 2 } ( t ) \rangle } } \cong m _ { \mathrm { t } } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } \frac { 1 } { \Delta t } .
$$

We see that $( \Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ) _ { \mathrm { a m p l } }$ is proportional to $1 / \Delta t$ .This is due to the act that,if the sampling time is small,the bandwidth is large and we re fooded with amplifier noise. On the contrary,we saw in eq. (8.107) that $( \Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ) _ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } }$ is proportional to $\Delta t$ .Therefore,putting together the amplifier and thermal noise,we discover that there is an optimum ralue of the sampling time $\Delta t$ and therefore of the bandwidth.We come back to thisbelow

The other useful (and more detailed） characterization of amplifer isesefieseciy $S _ { n } ^ { \mathsf { a m p l } } ( f )$ According to Ie general definition (8.106), to get $\$ 0$ we must divide the spectral tyofthetis $S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } }$ by the squared modulus Mfthe transducer transfer function $T _ { \mathrm { t } } ( \omega )$ ,given in eq. (8.95).Recalling that $\bar { \gamma } = \omega _ { 0 } / Q _ { m }$ , see eq. (8.96), we get

Fig. 8.10 $\log _ { 1 0 } [ S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } ( f ) / \mathcal { A } ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ,as a function of the frequency, for $\mu \mathbf { \Sigma } =$ $2 . 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ and $f _ { 0 } = 9 1 5 ~ \mathrm { H z }$

270terve that $S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } }$ is propor $1 / \alpha ^ { 2 }$ ,see eq. (8.138), and therefore to $1 / \beta ^ { 2 }$ ，see eq.(8.101). Therefore eq.(8.145） show that $\Gamma$ is proportional to $1 / \beta ^ { 2 }$ and，from eq.(8.144), $\Delta f$ is proportional to $\beta$ Therefore the bandwidth can be enlarged increasing $\beta$ ，i.e.the coupling of the mechanical to the electric modes, compare with Note 20 on page 434.

$$
S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } ( f ) = { \cal A } \frac { \left[ ( f ^ { 2 } - f _ { + } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( f f _ { 0 } / Q _ { m } ) ^ { 2 } \right] \left[ ( f ^ { 2 } - f _ { - } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( f f _ { 0 } / Q _ { m } ) ^ { 2 } \right] } { f ^ { 4 } f _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } ,
$$

where

$$
\mathcal { A } = \frac { \pi ^ { 4 } } { 4 L ^ { 2 } } S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } .
$$

In Fig. 8.10 we show the dimenslonless quantity $[ S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } ( f ) / \mathcal { A } ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ,for the same values of $f _ { 0 } , \mu , Q _ { 0 }$ and $\phi _ { \mathrm { t } }$ used above for thermal noise. The amplifier thermal noise is minimum at the two resonance frequencies $f _ { \pm }$

Thermal and amplifier noise are the two dominant contributions to the strain sensitivity in present detectors,so we can now put these two effects together and obtain a first understanding of the detector's sensitivities. We start from the energy considerations, that are less accurate (since the detailed frequency dependence is integrated over) but more intuitive. Combining eqs. (8.107) and (8.140) we see that the minimum detectable energy is

$$
\Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \sim k T \frac { \Delta t } { \tau } + m _ { \mathrm { t } } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } \frac { 1 } { \Delta t } ,
$$

where $\Delta t$ is the sampling time. We take $\tau$ to be the relaxation time of the full three modes system (mechanical plus electric),and we write $\tau = Q / \omega _ { 0 }$ ,where $Q$ is the overall quality factor of the system. Since the first term is proportional to $\Delta t$ and the second to $1 / \Delta t$ ,we can minimize $\Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } }$ choosing an optimal value pf $\Delta t$ This gives

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \Delta f \simeq \frac { 1 } { ( \Delta t ) _ { \mathrm { o p t } } } \sim \pi \frac { f _ { 0 } } { Q } \Gamma ^ { - 1 / 2 } . } } \\ { { \displaystyle \Gamma = \frac { m _ { \mathrm { t } } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } S _ { \mathrm { t } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } } { | Q k T } , } } \end{array}
$$

In typical experimental situationsl we can have $Q = O ( 1 0 ^ { 6 } )$ and $\Gamma =$ $O ( 1 0 ^ { - 8 } - 1 0 ^ { - 9 } )$ .Therefore,at a frequency $f _ { 0 } \simeq 1 ~ \mathrm { k H z }$ we can have $\Delta f = O ( 1 0 { - } 1 0 0 ) ~ \mathrm { H z }$ ：An important point that we understand from this result (and which was fully realized only in the 198Os) is that the useful bandwidth of a resonant bar has nothing to do with the width of the peak of the resonance in the transfer function. In fact,the latter is $( \Delta f ) _ { \mathrm { r e s } } = f _ { 0 } / Q$ and, for typical values $Q \sim 1 0 ^ { 6 }$ and $f _ { 0 } \sim 1 ~ \mathrm { k H z }$ it is extreimely small, of order of $1 ~ \mathrm { \ m H z }$ .Instead the useful bandwidth. given in eq. (8.144), is many order of magnitude larger: it depends on $Q$ only as $1 / Q ^ { 1 / 2 }$ rather than as $1 / Q$ (since $\Gamma \sim 1 / Q$ ，and we see from eq. (8.145) that it can be made larger lowering the amplifier noise, so it really depends on the details of the read-out system.27

When $\Delta t = ( \Delta t ) _ { \tt o p t }$ , the two terms on the right-hand side of eq.i become equal,and

$$
\Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \sim 2 k T \frac { ( \Delta t ) _ { \mathrm { o p t } } } { \tau } .
$$

Writing $( \Delta t ) _ { \mathrm { o p t } } \sim 1 / \Delta f$ and $1 / \tau _ { 0 } = \gamma _ { 0 } = \omega _ { 0 } / Q$ ,we get

with an effective temperature $T _ { \mathrm { e f f } }$ given by

$$
\overline { { T _ { \mathrm { e f f } } \sim \frac { 4 \pi } { Q } } } \frac { \bar { f } _ { 0 } } { \Delta f } T \simeq 4 \Gamma ^ { 1 / 2 } T .
$$

Fora bar cooled at a thermodynamic temperature $T \simeq 2 \mathrm { K }$ with $Q \simeq 1 \times$ $1 0 ^ { 6 }$ ， $\Delta f \simeq 1 0 \ : \mathrm { H z }$ ， $f _ { 0 } \simeq 9 0 0 \ : \mathrm { H z }$ eq.(8.148） gives $T _ { \mathrm { e f f } } \simeq 2 \ : \mathrm { m K }$ .This result is quite interesting;it means that,eveh ina barat the thermodynamical temperature of $2 \mathrm { \ K }$ ,we can detect bursts that deposited in it an energy $E _ { s }$ of just a few mK.The fact that,sampling the output at a fast rate, we can dig deeply into thermal noise,is of course just an example of the general concept of matched filtering that we discussed in Section 7.3.

To have a more detailed picture,we consider now the noise spectral density. The total noise spectral density is

$$
S _ { n } ( f ) = S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f ) + S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } ( f ) ,
$$

withe $S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f )$ of teaaisesiluam $S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } ( f )$ plifer noise given in eq. (8.141). The total strain sensitivity is $S _ { n } ^ { 1 / 2 } ( f )$ Explicitly,recalling that $\mu = m _ { \mathrm { t } } / m = 2 m _ { \mathrm { t } } / M$ ,we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { S _ { n } ( f ) = \displaystyle \frac { \pi k T } { M v _ { s } ^ { 2 } f _ { 0 } } \{ \frac { f _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } { f ^ { 4 } } [ \frac { 1 } { Q _ { 0 } } + \frac { 1 } { \mu Q _ { \mathrm { t } } } \frac { ( f ^ { 2 }  f _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { f _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } ]  } } \\ { { \displaystyle  + \frac { Q \Gamma } { \mu } \frac { [ ( f ^ { 2 } - f _ { + } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( f f _ { 0 } / Q _ { m } ) ^ { 2 } ] [ ( f ^ { 2 } - f _ { - } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( f f _ { 0 } / Q _ { m } ) ^ { 2 } ] } { f ^ { 4 } f _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } \} . } } \end{array}
$$

We recall that $Q _ { 0 } , Q _ { t }$ are the quality factors of the fundamental mode of the bar $\xi _ { 0 }$ and of the transducer $\xi _ { \mathrm { t } }$ ，respectively; $Q _ { m }$ is the total mechanical quality factor,see eq.(8.96) and $Q$ ，which enters in the definition (8.145) of $\Gamma$ ,is the total quality factor of the system.

We see that,for given values of the quality factors and of $\mu$ ,thefactor Idetermines the relative importance of thermal and read-out noise,and therefore controls the bandwidth $\Delta f$ ，

In Figs.8.11-8.13 we plot the logarithm of the total strain sensitivity $| S _ { n } ( f ) | ^ { 1 / 2 }$ (solid line) and the separate contribution from thermal noise of the bar and of the transducer $[ S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { t h e r m a l } } ( f ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ (dot-dashed line),and from the amplifier noise, $[ S _ { n } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } ( f ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ (dashed line).We use for defimiteness the values $\mu = 2 . 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ ， $Q _ { 0 } = Q _ { \mathrm { t } } = Q = 2 \times 1 0 ^ { 6 }$ ， $T = 1 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }$ ， $M = 2 3 0 0 \mathrm { k g }$ ， $v _ { s } = 5 4 0 0 \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s } .$ ， $f _ { 0 } = 9 1 5 \ : \mathrm { H z }$ ,and we vary $\Gamma$ ,so we change the relative importance of amplifier and thermal noise.

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/80e4ca047a7035eabdcb8fbdc133f59e69afa59503b19d84fd005970e114089d.jpg)  
Fig. 8.11 $\log _ { 1 0 } [ S _ { n } ^ { 1 / 2 } ]$ as a function of the frequency,in $\mathrm { H z }$ ,taking $\Gamma =$ $1 0 ^ { - 7 }$ .The other parameters are given in the text.Dashed line: amplifier noise.Dot-dashed line: thermal noise (bar plus transducer). Solid line: total.Here the amplifier noise dominates,except very close to the resonances.

Fig.8.13 The same as Fig.8.11,but with $\Gamma = 1 0 ^ { - 9 }$ .Now thermal noise dominates (which means that one has chosen a transducer too light).

# 8.3.3 Back-action noise and the quantum limit

In this section we discuss another source of intrinsic noise,the bad action noise,that in present resonant detectors is small,but which cu become important for the design of advanced detectors,and also has a intrinsic conceptual interest,because of its relation with fundament limitations imposed by quantum mechanics.

When we studied the double oscillator,in Section 8.2.1,we introduce external forces acting on the oscillator $\xi _ { 0 }$ (which represents the funds mental mode of the bar）and bn the oscillator $\xi _ { \mathrm { t } }$ ，which represents thm light mass,and we denoted these forces by $F _ { 0 }$ and $F _ { \mathrm { t } }$ respectively. then wrote the equations of motion in the form (8.72)-(8.73). Further more,we had an elastic force acting between the bar and the light mas Defining the spring constant $k = \overline { { m _ { \mathrm { t } } \omega _ { \mathrm { t } } ^ { 2 } } }$ ,eqs. (8.72) and (8.73) can b rewritten as

$$
\begin{array} { r } { m ( \ddot { \xi } _ { 0 } + \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \xi _ { 0 } ) = + k ( \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } - \xi _ { 0 } ) + F _ { 0 } , } \\ { m _ { \mathrm { t } } \ddot { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } } = - k ( \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } - \xi _ { 0 } ) + F _ { \mathrm { t } } . } \end{array}
$$

Of course, since the force $k ( \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } | - \xi _ { 0 } )$ acts between the bar and the lighi mass, it enters with opposite signs in the equations for $\ddot { \xi } _ { 0 }$ and for $\ddot { \xi } _ { t }$

As we saw in Section 8.2.2,this coupling between $\xi _ { 0 }$ and $\xi _ { \mathrm { t } }$ is a schematization of an extended device which consists of a capacitor,which in turn is part of an LC circuit,and is connected to an amplifier. This read-out has been designed so that the mechanical oscillations of the transducer are transformed into an electric signal. Unfortunately thi process is reversible,and fluctuations in the electric circuit generate a force that actually shakes the transducer and the bar.Any noise in the electronic apparatus will then appear as a stochastic force $F _ { 0 t }$ acting between the bar and the light mass.This force is called the back-action exerted on the bar and on the light mass by the amplifier. More generally,it describes the effect of any fuctuation taking place between the bar and the transducer,e.g. fuctuations of the electric field in the capacitor.Then eqs.(8.151) and (8.151) must rather be written as

$$
\begin{array} { r } { m ( \ddot { \xi } _ { 0 } + \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \xi _ { 0 } ) = + k ( \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } - \xi _ { 0 } ) + F _ { 0 t } + F _ { 0 } , } \\ { m _ { \mathrm { t } } \ddot { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } } = - k ( \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } - \xi _ { 0 } ) - F _ { 0 t } + F _ { \mathrm { t } } , } \end{array}
$$

or,including also the disipation terms as in eqs. (8.88)and (8.89),

$$
\begin{array} { r } { m ( \ddot { \xi } _ { 0 } + { \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \xi _ { 0 } ) = + k ( \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } - \xi _ { 0 } ) + \left[ F _ { 0 t } + m _ { \mathrm { t } } \gamma _ { \mathrm { t } } ( \dot { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } } - \dot { \xi } _ { 0 } ) \right] + \left[ F _ { 0 } - m \gamma _ { 0 } \dot { \xi } _ { 0 } \right] , } \\ { m _ { \mathrm { t } } \ddot { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } } = - k ( \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } - \xi _ { 0 } ) + \left[ F _ { 0 t } + m _ { \mathrm { t } } \gamma _ { \mathrm { t } } ( \dot { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } } - \dot { \xi } _ { 0 } ) \right] + F _ { \mathrm { t } } . \qquad ( 8 . 1 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 7 } ) ^ { - 1 } . } \end{array}
$$

Having extracted explicitly these dissipation terms, now $F _ { 0 }$ and $F _ { 0 t }$ are stochastic forces with zero mean, $\left. F _ { 0 \mathrm { t } } \right. = \left. F _ { 0 } \right. = 0$ ，characterized by their spectral densities $S _ { F _ { 0 } }$ and $S _ { F _ { 0 \mathrm { t } } }$ . (Since we are studying the noise acting on the bar,we can assume that there is no GW contribution to $F _ { 0 }$ ; otherwise,we can just extract the GW force and write it explicitly.) Then the spectral densities of eq.(8.133) must be replaced by

$$
S _ { F _ { 0 } }  S _ { F _ { 0 } } + S _ { F _ { 0 \mathrm { t } } } , ~ S _ { F _ { \mathrm { t } } }  S _ { F _ { \mathrm { t } } } + S _ { F _ { 0 \mathrm { t } } } .
$$

To evaluate $S _ { F _ { 0 t } }$ we consider for instance the case of a voltage amplifer.28 Ina voltage amplifer the noise can be described by a voltage noise plus independent fluctuations in the current.We denote the single-sided spectral densities of the voltage and current noise by $v _ { n } ^ { 2 }$ (measured in $\mathrm { v o l t s } ^ { 2 } / \mathrm { H z } )$ and $i _ { n } ^ { 2 }$ (in $\mathrm { a m p } ^ { 2 } / \mathrm { H z } )$ ,respectively. The spectral density of the output voltage $V$ is given by $S _ { V } = v _ { n } ^ { 2 } + | Z | ^ { 2 } i _ { n } ^ { 2 }$ where $Z$ is the impedance,and we neglected correlated noisebetween voltage and current.It is customary to define the noise temperature $T _ { n }$ of the amplifier by $2 k T _ { n } = v _ { n } i _ { n }$ (where the factor of 2 is due to the fact that we use singleside spectral densities),and the amplifer noise resistance $R _ { n } = v _ { n } / i _ { n }$ Introducing further the dimensionless quantity $\lambda = R _ { n } / | Z |$ ,known as the impedance match ratio,and using eq. (8.101),one gets

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { S _ { \xi _ { \mathrm { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } \left( v _ { n } ^ { 2 } + | Z | ^ { 2 } i _ { n } ^ { 2 } \right) } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { 2 k T _ { n } } { m _ { \mathrm { t } } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } \beta } \left( \lambda + \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

28This example issomewhat oldfashioned,since modern resonant detectors use SQUIDS rather than voltage amplifiers,but is the simplest setting for illustrating the general ideas.

The back-action force due to current fluctuations can be computed in this case observing that the power dissipated in the electric circuit is $P =$ $V I$ The contribution to $V$ due to the displacement of the transducer is obtained from eq. (8.98), $V = Z _ { \mathrm { 2 1 } } \dot { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } }$ ，so the corresponding power is $P = Z _ { \mathrm { 2 1 } } \dot { I } \dot { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } }$ . This is the power generated by a force of modulus $Z _ { 2 1 } I$ acting ona mechanical object with velocity $\dot { \xi } _ { \mathrm { t } }$ .Therefore a current $I$ in the circuit exerts on the transducer a force of modulus $F _ { \mathrm { 0 t } } = Z _ { \mathrm { 2 1 } } I$ and the spectral density of this back-action force is $S _ { F _ { 0 t } } = | Z _ { 2 1 } | ^ { 2 } i _ { n } ^ { 2 }$ ，where $i _ { n }$ is the spectral density of the current. As we saw below eq. (8.98), $| Z _ { 2 1 } | = \alpha / \omega _ { 0 }$ ，s0 $S _ { F _ { 0 \mathrm { t } } } ~ = ~ \alpha ^ { 2 } i _ { n } ^ { 2 } / \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }$ In terms of $T _ { n }$ and $\lambda$ ，we have $i _ { n } ^ { 2 } = 2 k T _ { n } / \lambda | Z |$ ,and therefore

$$
S _ { F _ { \mathrm { t } } } + S _ { F _ { \mathrm { 0 t } } } = 4 k T m _ { \mathrm { t } } \gamma _ { \mathrm { t } } + 2 k T _ { n } { \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \lambda \vert Z \vert } } .
$$

Expressing the result in terms of $\beta$ ,given in eq.(8.102),we find

$$
S _ { F _ { \mathrm { t } } } + S _ { F _ { \mathrm { 0 t } } } = 4 m _ { \mathrm { t } } \gamma _ { \mathrm { t } } \left( k T + k T _ { n } { \frac { \beta Q _ { \mathrm { t } } } { 2 \lambda } } \right) .
$$

Therefore,at least in a first approximation,29 the back action is formally equivalent to a shift of the transducer temperature

$$
\boxed { T  T + \frac { \beta Q _ { \mathrm { t } } } { 2 \lambda } T _ { n } . }
$$

29A more detailed computation based on the full set of equations governing the coupled mechanical and electric modes show that the strain sensitivity of the back-reaction has a different form,asa function of the frequency, from that due to thermal noise.

For the amplifer noise we use eq. (8.156). Typically $\lambda \gg 1$ s0, $S _ { \xi _ { \mathfrak { t } } } ^ { \mathrm { a m p l } } \simeq$ $2 k T _ { n } \lambda / m _ { \mathrm { t } } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } \beta$ .Then,including the back-action,the minimum detectable energy (8.143) becomes

$$
\Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \sim \left( k T + \frac { \beta Q } { 2 \lambda } k T _ { n } \right) \frac { \Delta t } { \tau } + \frac { 2 k T _ { n } \lambda } { \beta \omega _ { 0 } \Delta t } .
$$

This expression raises an interesting issue of principle because it shows that,even if one were able to cool the bar to a negligibly small valu of the thermodynamic temperature $T$ ， still there would be a minimum value of the detectable energy, since even at $T = 0$ we have both a terui proportional to $\Delta t$ and a term prpportional to $1 / \Delta t$ .Setting $\scriptstyle { \mathcal { T } } = 0$ in eq.(8.160) and writing $\tau = Q / \omega _ { 0 }$ we have

$$
\Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \sim k T _ { n } \left( \frac { \beta \omega _ { 0 } } { 2 \lambda } \Delta t + \frac { 2 \lambda } { \beta \omega _ { 0 } } \frac { 1 } { \Delta t } \right) .
$$

This is minimized choosing $( \Delta t ) _ { \mathrm { o p t } } = 2 \lambda / ( \beta \omega _ { 0 } )$ , and the corresponding minimum detectable value of the energy is

$$
\Delta E _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \sim 2 k T _ { n } .
$$

Therefore,even if we cool the detector to $T$ close to zero,an ultimate limitation is provided by the ampllfier noise temperature. For a SQUID. $T _ { n } \sim 1 0 ^ { - 3 } ~ \mathrm { m K }$ ，while the lowest effective temperatures that have been reached by bars at present are of the order of $0 . 3 ~ \mathrm { m K }$ , so this limit at present is not very important from a practical point of view.

Nevertheless, this observation brings us to a second interesting question,namely,what is the minimum value that can be obtained in principle for the amplifier noise temperature $T _ { n }$ ? As we will discuss in the next subsection (and as was shown by Heffner,already in 1962) any linear amplifier (e.g.an object that preservesa linear relation between the input and output values of the amplitude and the phase of the signal) working at a frequency $\omega _ { 0 }$ , as a consequence of the uncertainty principle. has a minimum noise temperature given by

$$
k T _ { n } \Big \vert \sim \hbar \omega _ { 0 } .
$$

We therefore discover that the ultimate limitation for a resonant bar operating with a linear amplifier is given by the uncertainty principle,

This is known as the standard quantum limit. It states that the best we can do(with a linear amplifier) is to detect an acoustic oscillation of the fundamental mode of the bar which,at the quantum level, corresponds to a single phonon.

A measure of how far we are presently from the quantum limit is provided by

$$
N _ { \mathrm { p h o n o n } } = \frac { k T _ { \mathrm { e f f } } } { \hbar \omega _ { 0 } } .
$$

For $T _ { \mathrm { e f f } } \simeq 2 ~ \mathrm { m K }$ and $f _ { 0 } = 9 0 0 ~ \mathrm { H z }$ ， $N _ { \mathrm { p h o n o n } } \simeq 5 \times 1 0 ^ { 4 }$ . Both the group that runs the EXPLORER and NAUTILUS detectors and the AURIGA group have attained $N _ { \mathrm { p h o n o n } } \simeq 1 0 ^ { 2 }$ so,for the moment,the quantum limit is not the main limitation in fesonant bars,although we are getting closer to it. The detection of a single-phonon excitation in a two-ton bar would be a remarkable technical achievement,and would correspond to an energy detection sensitivity at the level $\hbar \omega _ { 0 } \simeq 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 1 } \mathrm { ~ J }$

Further reflection shows that the quantum limit,in itself,cannot be an absolute limit.Quantum mechanics does not forbid arbitrarily precisemeasurements of energy,but only arbitrarily precise simultaneous measurements of conjugate variables.The origin of the problem is that a linear amplifier allows us to reconstruct both the amplitude and the phase of the motion of the oscillator $\xi _ { \mathrm { t } }$ .However,for a harmonic oscillator,to know its amplitude and its phase is equivalent to knowing simultaneously the position and the momentum,and it is here that the Heisenberg principle comes into play. The quantum limit can therefore be evaded if we give up the information on the phase. We discuss the issue in more detail in the next subsection.

# 8.3.4 Quantum non-demolition measurements

Consider a generic harmonic oscillator, with mass $m$ and frequency $\omega _ { 0 }$ ， described classically by a coordinate $x \backslash ( t )$ . The solution of the equation of motion with the initial conditions $x ( 0 ) = x _ { 0 }$ and $\dot { x } ( 0 ) = v _ { 0 }$ is

$$
x ( t ) = x _ { 0 } \cos \omega _ { 0 } t + { \frac { v _ { 0 } } { \omega _ { 0 } } } \sin \omega _ { 0 } t .
$$

Let $\boldsymbol { v } ( t ) = \dot { \boldsymbol { x } } ( t )$ ,and define $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 }$ as

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { X _ { 1 } = x ( t ) \cos \omega _ { 0 } t - \frac { v ( t ) } { \omega _ { 0 } } \sin \omega _ { 0 } t , } \\ & { X _ { 2 } = x ( t ) \sin \omega _ { 0 } t + \Biggl | \frac { v ( t ) } { \omega _ { 0 } } \cos \omega _ { 0 } t . } \end{array}
$$

Using eq. (8.166) and the corresponding expression for $v ( t )$ ,we see that $X _ { 1 } = x _ { 0 }$ and $X _ { 2 } = v _ { 0 } / \omega _ { 0 }$ ，S0 $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 }$ are conserved on the equations of motion,and eq.(8.166) can be rewritten as

$$
x ( t ) = \mathrm { R e } \left[ ( X _ { 1 } + i X _ { 2 } ) e ^ { - i \omega _ { 0 } t } \right] .
$$

At the quantum level,we denote the pperators by a caret,so $\hat { x }$ and $\hat { p }$ are the position and momentum operators,with $[ \hat { x } , \hat { p } ] = i \hbar$ As usual, the Hamiltonian of the harmonic oscillator is

$$
\hat { H } _ { 0 } = \frac { \hat { p } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } m \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \hat { x } ^ { 2 } = \hbar \omega _ { 0 } \left( \hat { N } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ,
$$

where $\hat { N } = \hat { a } ^ { \dag } \hat { a }$ and

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \hat { a } = \left( \frac { m \omega _ { 0 } } { 2 \hbar } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( \hat { x } + i \frac { \hat { p } } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \right) , } \\ { \hat { a } ^ { \dagger } = \left( \frac { m \omega _ { 0 } } { 2 \hbar } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( \hat { x } - i \frac { \hat { p } } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \right) . } \end{array}
$$

In the Heisenberg picture we define the operators $\hat { X } _ { 1 }$ and $\hat { X } _ { 2 }$ as in eqs. (8.167) and (8.168),

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t ) = \hat { x } ( t ) \cos \omega _ { \mathrm { p } } t - \frac { \hat { p } ( t ) } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \sin \omega _ { 0 } t , } \\ & { } & { \hat { X } _ { 2 } ( t ) = \hat { x } ( t ) \sin \omega _ { 0 } t + \frac { \hat { p } ( t ) } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \cos \omega _ { 0 } t . } \end{array}
$$

Similarly to what happens classically, in the absence of external forces the Heisenberg operators $\hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t )$ and $\hat { X } _ { 2 } ( t )$ are conserved,

$$
\frac { d \hat { X } _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial \hat { X } _ { i } } { \partial t } - \left[ \frac { i } { \hbar } [ \hat { X } _ { i } , H _ { 0 } ] = 0 . \right.
$$

This property selects $\hat { X } _ { 1 }$ and $\hat { X } _ { 2 }$ as particularly useful observables, since any change they experience must bedue to external disturbances,such as GWs or other external forces. The interaction of the quantum harmonic oscillator with an external classical force $F ( t )$ can be described by an interaction Hamiltonian $\hat { H } _ { \mathrm { i n t } } = - F ( t ) \hat { x }$ .In the presence of $F ( t ) , \hat { X } _ { 1 }$ and $\hat { X } _ { 2 }$ are no longer conserved; rather

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \hat { X } _ { 1 } } { d t } = - \frac { i } { \hbar } [ \hat { X } _ { 1 } , H _ { \mathrm { i n t } } ] = - \frac { F ( t ) } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \sin \omega _ { 0 } t , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \hat { X } _ { 2 } } { d t } = - \frac { i } { \hbar } [ \hat { X } _ { 2 } , H _ { \mathrm { i n t } } ] = + \frac { F ( t ) } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \cos \omega _ { 0 } t . } \end{array}
$$

This integrates to

$$
\hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t ) = \hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t _ { 0 } ) - \frac { 1 } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } d t ^ { \prime } F ( t ^ { \prime } ) \sin \omega _ { 0 } t ^ { \prime } ,
$$

and similarly for $\hat { X } _ { 2 }$ . The important point is that, since $F ( t )$ is a classical force,the second term on the right-hand side is a $c$ -number rather than an operator.Suppose that at time $t _ { 0 }$ we perform a measurement of the observable $\hat { X } _ { 1 }$ on the oscillator. We will find some value $X _ { 1 } ^ { 0 }$ ,and the measurement leaves the oscillator in the corresponding eigenstate $| X _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } \rangle$ of $\hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t _ { 0 } )$ . In the Heisenberg picture|the state $| X _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } \rangle$ does not evolve, while the operator $\hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t )$ evolves as in eq.(8.178). Then,since the second term on the right-hand side of eq. (8.178 is a $c$ -number, for $t > t _ { 0 }$ we have

$$
\hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t ) \vert X _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } \rangle \nonumber = X _ { 1 } ( t ) \vert X _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } \rangle ,
$$

with

$$
X _ { 1 } ( t ) = X _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } - \frac { 1 } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } d t ^ { \prime } F ( t ^ { \prime } ) \sin \omega _ { 0 } t ^ { \prime } .
$$

So,at time $t > t _ { 0 }$ the oscillator is still in an eigenstate of $\hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t )$ ,and a measurement of $\hat { X } _ { 1 }$ at time $t$ will give the above value,and will not affect the oscillator state (apart possibly for a phase,since states in the Fock space that differ by a phase correspond to the same physical state).In other words,when the classical force $F ( t )$ is acting,after the first measurement，all successive measurements of ${ \hat { X } } _ { 1 }$ leave unchanged the state of the oscillator,even if the result $X _ { 1 } ( t )$ of this measurement changes continuously in time,see eq. (8.180).

This is of course very different from what happens when we rather measure the position operator ${ \hat { x } } ( t )$ of the oscillator,since in this case each time we perform a measurement of ${ \hat { x } } ( t )$ we have a wavefunction reduction which forces the oscillator in an eigenstate of ${ \hat { x } } ( t )$ ,and the result obtained at $t = t _ { 0 }$ does not fix uniquely the outcome of a successive measurement at $t > t _ { 0 }$ . It is clear that $X _ { 1 }$ (or $X _ { 2 }$ ）are the best quantities to be measured when quantum miechanics becomes important,since froma series of repeated measurements of it we can reconstruct deterministically the time-evolution of the external classical force $F ( t )$ A measurement that leaves the state of the system unchanged,as the measurement of $\hat { X } _ { 1 } ( t )$ described above,is called a quantum non-demolition measurement.

Having established that ${ \hat { X } } _ { 1 }$ and $\hat { X } _ { 2 }$ are the most useful observables for our purposes,we can discuss what the uncertainty principles has to tell about their measurement.From'the definition,eq.(8.173),we see that

$$
[ X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } ] = { \frac { i \hbar } { m \omega _ { 0 } } } .
$$

Therefore, on any state,

$$
\Delta X _ { 1 } \Delta X _ { 2 } \geqslant \frac { 1 } { 2 } | \left. [ X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } ] \right. | = \frac { \hbar } { 2 m \omega _ { 0 } } .
$$

Suppose that we measure $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ using an amplifier with a bandwidth $\Delta f \ll f _ { 0 }$ .This means that we sample the oscillator position over a time $\Delta t$ much longer than the period of the oscillator.Therefore we are actually measuring the average value of $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ over a period, that we denote by ${ \bar { X } } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \bar { X } } _ { 2 }$ ,respectively. If we use a linear ampliffer, when the input is given by eq.(8.169),the output is of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { y ( t ) = \mathrm { R e } \left[ A ( \hat { X } _ { 1 } \stackrel { \cdot } { + } i \bar { X } _ { 2 } ) e ^ { - i \omega _ { 0 } t } \right] , } \end{array}
$$

with $A$ a constant amplification factor,in general complex. Since ${ \bar { X } } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \tilde { X } } _ { 2 }$ are treated symmetrically in this expression,their errors are {equal, $\Delta \bar { X } _ { 1 } = \Delta \bar { X } _ { 2 }$ .In this case eq.(8.182) gives

$$
{ \Delta \bar { X } _ { 1 } = \Delta \bar { X } _ { 2 } \geqslant \left( \frac { \hbar } { 2 m \omega _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \mathrm { ~ . ~ }
$$

Recall from eq. (8.173) that $( X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } ) \}$ are obtained from $( x , p / m \omega _ { 0 } )$ performing a rotation by an angle $\omega _ { 0 } t$ Then $\Delta \bar { X } _ { 1 } = \Delta \bar { X } _ { 2 }$ implies also $\Delta x = \Delta p / m \omega _ { 0 }$ which,together with $\Delta x \Delta p \geqslant \hbar / 2$ ,means

$$
\Delta x = \frac { \Delta p } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \geqslant \left( \frac { \hbar } { 2 m \omega _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

Therefore,if we monitor the fundamental mode of the bar with a linear amplifer,quantum mechanics sets a limit to the error $\Delta x$ Writing,as

usual, $m = M / 2$ ,where $M$ is the total massof the bar, we have

$$
( \Delta x ) _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \simeq 2 . 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 9 } \mathrm { c m } \left( \frac { 2 0 0 0 \mathrm { k g } } { M } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( \frac { 1 \mathrm { k H z } } { f _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

In terms of energy,writing

$$
\hat { H } _ { 0 } = \frac { m \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { \hat { p } } { m \omega _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \hat { x } ^ { 2 } \right] ,
$$

we see from eq. (8.185) that,even if the oscillator is in its ground state there is a minimum error on the energy

$$
( \Delta E ) _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \simeq \frac { m \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { \Delta p } { \dot { \eta } \omega _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + ( \Delta x ) ^ { 2 } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \hbar \omega _ { 0 } .
$$

More generally, if one takes as wavefunction a Gaussian both in $\tilde { \lambda }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ ,centered on the expectation values $\langle \hat { X } _ { 1 } \rangle$ and $\langle \hat { X } _ { 2 } \rangle$ ,and with variances $\Delta X _ { 1 } = \Delta X _ { 2 } = ( \hbar / 2 m \omega _ { 0 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ,one finds that the variance o the average number of quanta in this state is $\Delta N = ( N + 1 / 4 ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ an therefore

$$
( \Delta E ) _ { \mathrm { m i n } } = \hbar \omega _ { 0 } \left( N + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

We have therefore understood how eq.(8.l64) comes out:it is a conse quence of the uncertainty principle and of the specific way of performing the measurement with a linear amplifier,which gives $\Delta X _ { 1 } = \Delta X _ { 2 }$ Now. however,we can also understand how,at least in principle,such a limit can be evaded: from eq.(8.178) we see that measuring either $X _ { 1 }$ or $X _ { 2 }$ is sufficient to reconstruct to external force $F ( t )$ ,which in our case means to measure the incoming $\mathrm { G W }$ .So，we need to perform a very precis measurement of,say, $X _ { 1 }$ ，giving up completely the information of $X _ { 2 }$ Strategies of this type are called back-action evasion measurements, since the back-action force on $X _ { 1 }$ caused by the measurement process,whicl in general is responsible for the uncertainty principle, has been evaded or rather has been concentrated uniquely on the conjugate variable $X _ { 2 }$

More precisely, from eq. (8.180), we have

$$
\frac { d X _ { 1 } ( t ) } { d t } = - \frac { 1 } { m \omega _ { 0 } } F ( t ) \sin \omega _ { 0 } t ,
$$

and from this we can accurately reconstruct $F ( t )$ ，except when $\sin \omega _ { 0 } t$ is close to zero.However,when $F ( t )$ is a classical force,we can also use a second transducer,on which we measure only $X _ { 2 } ( t )$ ，since this second transducer is affected by the same classical force $F ( t )$ . Then from this measurement we get

$$
\frac { d X _ { 2 } ( t ) } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { m \omega _ { 0 } } F ( t ) \cos { \omega _ { 0 } t } ,
$$

and $F ( t )$ can be completely reconstructed.

# 8.3.5Experimental sensitivities

In this section we discuss the sensitiyities of existing resonant detectors.Actually,the experimental situation is in continuous evolution;all detectors typically alternate data-taking periods with upgrades,old experiments terminate their activity,and new ones are sometime proposed. Thus,any discussion of the experimental situation is bound to become obsolete on a relatively short time-scale.We therefore discuss only very briefly the various detectors,referring the reader to the web pages of the various collaborations in the Further Reading section for updated information,and we rather discuss what results can be achieved with existing or foreseeable sensitivities.

Presently (2007) three resonant barsare in operation: AURIGA (Legnaro,near Padua)，EXPLORER (CERN) and NAUTILUS(Frascati, Iear Rome),while a fourth bar,ALLEGRO (Baton Rouge,Louisiana), terminated operation during 2007. EXPLORER and NAUTILUS are operated by the same collaboration (ROG).We show in Fig.8.14 a picture of NAUTILUS and in Fig.8.15 apicture of AURIGA.In Fig.8.16 we show a typical strain sensitivity curve of the EXPLORER and NAU-TILUS detector,and in Fig.8.17a typical the strain sensitivity of AU-RIGA.We see that, thanks to the improvement in the read-out system discussed in Section 8.2.2, this strain sensitivity has a minimum value

$$
S _ { n } ^ { 1 / 2 } \simeq 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 1 } \mathrm { H z } ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,
$$

and stays below $4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 1 } \mathrm { H z } ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ over a bandwidth of $1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { H z }$ We now discuss,using the results of Section 7.3,the sensitivity to bursts, periodic and stochastic signals that can be obtained with these strain sensitivities.

# Sensitivity to bursts

Using eq. (7.96) at $S / N = 1$ (of course a higher threshold,typically of order $5 \mathrm { - } 6$ ,must be used),we can estimate that $| \tilde { h } ( f _ { 0 } ) | \simeq [ S _ { n } / ( 4 \Delta f ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ Usingnvece $S _ { n } ^ { 1 / 2 } \mathrm { \sim } 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 1 } \mathrm { H z ^ { - 1 / 2 } }$ over a bandwidth $\Delta f \sim 1 0 0 \ \mathrm { H z }$ gives the order-of magnitude estimate for the mini-mum detectable value of $\tilde { h } ( f _ { 0 } )$ ，

$$
\left| \tilde { h } ( f _ { 0 } ) \right| \sim 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 2 } \mathrm { H z } ^ { - 1 } .
$$

Assuming a sine-Gaussian waveform (7.103),centered on the resonance frequency $f _ { 0 }$ of the detector and of duration $\tau _ { g }$ ,and using eq.(7.105), we get the minimum detectable value of the GW amplitude,

$$
h _ { 0 } \sim 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 9 } \left( \frac { 1 \mathrm { m s } } { \tau _ { g } } \right) ,
$$

or equivalently,using eq. (7.110),the minimum detectable value of $h _ { \mathrm { r s s } }$

$$
h _ { \mathrm { r s s } } \sim 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 1 } \mathrm { H z } ^ { - 1 / 2 } \left( \frac { 1 \mathrm { m s } } { \tau _ { g } } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

For resonant bars it is often useful to think directly in terims of il energy deposited by a burst in the detector.In this case,inserting numerical values30 $M = 2 2 6 0 ~ \mathrm { k g }$ ， $L = 3 ~ \mathrm { m }$ ， $f _ { 0 } = 9 4 0 \ \mathrm { H z }$ , and measurim $E _ { s }$ in milliKelvin,eq.(8.19) glves

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/ed43ab533168c6890cdbf75b0c634838a933f046c4a658acdbe1e863b9c210a6.jpg)

$$
\tilde { h } ( f _ { 0 } ) \simeq 2 . 5 \times \mathrm { \small { 1 0 ^ { - 2 2 } H z ^ { - 1 } } } \left( \frac { E _ { s } } { 1 \mathrm { m K } } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

and we see from eq.(8.193) that present resonant detectors can measun excitations that leave in a baf an energy $E _ { s } \sim 1 ~ \mathrm { m K }$ Assuming agai a sine-Gaussian waveform centered within the detcctor bandwidth,am using eqs. (7.104) and (7.110),we get $h _ { \mathrm { r s s } } ^ { 2 } = ( 2 / \pi ) ^ { 1 / 2 } | \tilde { h } ( f _ { 0 } ) | ^ { 2 } / \tau _ { g }$ Insert ing this into eq.(7.112) and using eq. (8.196) we find that a GW bur that deposited in a resonant bar an energy $E _ { s }$ should originate from process that liberated in GWs the energy

![](../chunks/Gravitational Waves Volume 1 Theory and Experiments (Michele Maggiore) (z-library.sk, 1lib_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/3455c697dc8486820d5dedc37399d618f564a630ce97300f77527f17a6569050.jpg)  
Fig.8.16 The strain sensitivity $[ S _ { n } ( f ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ofEXPLORER (the curve with two minima) and NAU-TILUS (the curve with one broad minimum） in 2004.The peaks around 940 and $1 0 0 0 ~ \mathrm { { H z } }$ ，respectively,are reference signals sent to the detectors to monitor the SQUID gain.
